philosophy: A U/B control/tempo deck that wins by out-valuing opponents through superior card draw and disruption, neutralizing fast aggro and out-grinding midrange/control strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands, 1–2 early interaction/cantrips, and a clear path to hit UUU by turn 3. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early spells, 0–1 lands, or hands lacking any way to stabilize against aggro. Prioritize keeping Mental Note or Counterspell if your mana base supports them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available. Turn 2: Develop mana, drop a 2-drop threat or counterspell, and set up for Mental Note. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/dual lands to ensure consistent UUU access for Mental Note and Lorien Revealed by turns 3–4. Avoid flooding with non-basic lands if you lack fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red, Elves, Affinity): +2/1 Dispel, +2/1 Drown in Sorrow, +2/1 Surgical Extraction. -2/2 Lorien Revealed, -1 Mental Note. vs Control/Midrange (Jund, Grixis): +2/1 Thoughtseize, +2/1 Drown in Sorrow, +2/1 Surgical Extraction. -2/2 Aggro hate. vs Mirror: +2/1 Drown in Sorrow, +2/1 Surgical Extraction, -2/2 Lorien Revealed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard counterspells for game-ending threats or critical combo pieces. Use Mental Note to filter into Lorien Revealed or answers, but avoid casting it into known discard. Manage your graveyard carefully; use Surgical Extraction against Jund or affinity-based strategies. Don't overextend into sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have sufficient interaction to protect them or when you can close the game in one turn. Hold back counterspells for Lorien Revealed, key combo pieces, or opponent's game-winners. Prioritize board wipes if the opponent is developing a wide board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), fast combo finishes, or ramp accelerants. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or when they hold up mana for counterspells. Track graveyard contents against Jund and affinity decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed into a game-ending threat, or a sequence of removal/counters to clear the board and attack. If the deck includes combo pieces, dig for them using Mental Note and Lorien Revealed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, board control, and the opponent has no sweepers or interaction left. Commit when you can safely cast Lorien Revealed and follow up with a threat or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has a locked board, you're out of resources, and have no comeback plan. Concede against fast combos with no interaction left, or against control decks that have out-valued you and have sweepers ready.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Pressure with cheap cantrips and threats at end of turn. Use Mental Note to dig for uncounterable threats or answers. Cast Lorien Revealed at end of turn to protect it from counterspells. Don't overcommit to expensive spells if you lack protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use early interaction and discard to disrupt mana development. Counter key mana rocks or accelerants. Stabilize with removal and card draw. Don't let them get ahead; apply pressure with threats once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and counterspells to stabilize. Use discard to remove burn spells or key aggro threats. Don't cast Lorien Revealed early; focus on surviving turns 1–3. Once stabilized, use card draw to out-value and close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with superior card draw and efficient threats. Pressure with threats to force interaction, then dig through counters with Mental Note. Win the resource war by managing your graveyard and using Surgical Extraction to disrupt their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war by holding counterspells for their Lorien Revealed or key threats. Use Surgical Extraction to disrupt their draw engines. Sideboard in more disruption and discard. Play conservatively early, then out-value in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw card draw to targeted hate and disruption. Prioritize answers to the format's top threats, adjust land counts if needed, and tailor your game plan to the specific matchup. Always sideboard in graveyard hate against Jund and affinity.
notes: Mental Note and Lorien Revealed are vulnerable to discard and sweepers; sequence carefully. Don't cast Lorien Revealed into a known sweeper. Watch for graveyard hate and adjust your game plan accordingly. Practice end-of-turn sequencing to protect your key spells.

philosophy: This deck leverages the Madness mechanic to disrupt opponents while applying relentless early pressure, winning by out-valuing through discard synergy, efficient removal, and closing games with unblockable damage or resilient threats. It excels against slower, resource-heavy archetypes and control decks that overcommit to answering the board.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals or shocklands if available), at least one discard outlet or Madness enabler, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (Lava Dart, Lightning Bolt, or a 1/2 drop). Mulligan on 4+ lands with no early plays, or 3+ lands with only removal and no threats/discard. Keep 2 lands if paired with a discard outlet + early threat or two cheap spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then either play a 1-drop or discard a cheap spell with Madness to pressure or answer a threat. Turn 2: Develop with a 2-drop or cast a discarded Madness spell for value. Sequence mana to ensure R on T1, BR on T2, and UBR on T3. Always prioritize casting discarded spells over letting them exile, and use discard to strip key threats or counterspells before they resolve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Blue Terror, Elves): +2 Lightning Bolt, +1 Lava Dart, -2 discard outlets, -1 utility U card. vs Control (Grixis Affinity, Jund Wildfire): +1 Kessig Flamebreather, +2 discard enablers, -2 removal, -1 land. vs Mirror: +1 targeted discard, +1 cheap counter/utility, -2 removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your hand as a dynamic resource; discard strategically to fuel Madness rather than dumping randomly. Preserve mana for interaction on opponent's turn, and remember that uncast Madness spells exile, so don't plan around graveyard recursion. Use U mana for utility or protection when needed, but prioritize R/B for pressure and discard.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready to answer their response or when they've exhausted interaction. Hold back if they have sweepers, hard counters, or graveyard hate. Trade removal efficiently: Lava Dart for 2-damage threats or ping, Lightning Bolt for 3-damage threats or finishers, and save discard for high-value targets.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath, Terror), hard counters (e.g., Counterspell, Negate), and discard/graveyard hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline). Note when opponents start drawing extra cards, have protection spells, or are holding up mana for multiple turns, as these signal a control or combo game plan.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kessig Flamebreather as a resilient threat or burn finisher, chains of Lava Dart and Lightning Bolt for unblockable damage, or discarding a high-value Madness spell that resolves to swing the board. Close games by forcing interaction until it's gone, then commit remaining resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction left, has low life total (≤10), or you've established board dominance with resilient threats. Also commit when you can safely discard a high-impact Madness spell that they cannot counter or answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when facing a locked-out control deck with no win conditions, or when you're down to a dead hand with no lands/discard outlets and the opponent has a board wipe or hard counter ready. Also concede if you're consistently out-valued in the discard war and lack a clear path to close.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove their counterspells before they can be cast, or cast spells in response to their counterspells to manipulate the stack. Rely on cheap, uncounterable effects if available, and avoid overcommitting to expensive spells they can easily bounce or counter.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum early pressure before they stabilize. Use removal to kill ramp creatures before they can cast big spells, and don't overextend into sweepers. Force them to use removal early, then close the game with discard and burn.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal over threats. Use Lava Dart and Lightning Bolt to stabilize, and save discard for their key threats or discard enablers. Don't overcommit to the board; trade efficiently and outlast their early burst.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure consistently to force interaction. Use discard to strip protection, counterspells, or key answers. Close games quickly before they can draw into sweepers or finishers, and value out their discarded spells with Madness.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by keeping hands with multiple discard outlets and early threats. Trade removal efficiently, value out their discarded spells, and prioritize keeping life total high to outlast their late-game threats. Sideboard in targeted discard and cheap interaction to out-value them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends: against control-heavy fields, bring in more discard and resilient threats; against aggro, prioritize removal and life gain if available. In mirrors, sideboard in targeted discard and cheap interaction. Always track opponent's sideboarded hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, counterspells) and adjust discard targets accordingly. Avoid overloading on removal in control matchups, as you still need to close the game.
notes: Common mistake: Discarding randomly without a plan. Always ask if the discard fuels a Madness spell, removes a threat, or strips a key answer. Don't overcommit to the graveyard; Madness exiles, not returns. Kessig Flamebreather likely synergizes with discard or burn, so sequence it to maximize value. Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust your discard targets to protect your win conditions.

philosophy: Jund Wildfire is a midrange-graveyard synergy deck that stabilizes early with efficient disruption and incremental value, then transitions into a high-impact late game fueled by sacrifice outlets, card draw, and direct damage. It excels against combo and control archetypes by applying consistent pressure while leveraging graveyard recursion and targeted removal to control the board and close out games.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing Ichor Wellspring or duals), 1-2 early threats (1-2 mana creatures), and at least one interaction (Cast Down, discard, or sacrifice outlet). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, all spells costing 3+ mana, or no way to interact before turn 3. Keep hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands on turn 1 if you have a turn 2 threat.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a 1-drop or hold for disruption. Turn 2: Play Ichor Wellspring or a dual land, cast a 2-drop threat or disruption. Prioritize establishing a functional mana base and early board presence. Against aggro, hold removal for turn 2-3 threats; against control, develop board and apply pressure while preserving interaction for key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): +2 Graveyard hate, +2 additional removal, -2 land ramp. vs Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +2 more disruption, +2 finishers, -2 early removal. vs Mirror: +1 targeted removal, +1 graveyard recursion, -2 generic disruption.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction versus threat development. Use Ichor Wellspring to fuel graveyard/sacrifice synergies and draw into answers. Avoid overcommitting to the board if the opponent has sweepers or unanswered removal. Trade efficiently to maintain card advantage, and use Fanatical Offering to convert excess creatures into value or damage when ahead.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for opponent's answers or when you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Hold back if you suspect a board wipe, if your removal is exhausted, or if the opponent is building a combo. Prioritize trading your removal for their key threats to preserve long-term value.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, big finishers, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for key spells or hold up interaction. Track how many removal spells they have answered and whether they are protecting specific creatures. Pay attention to graveyard contents to anticipate recursion or sacrifice synergies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact creatures, sacrifice-to-damage combos via Fanatical Offering, and graveyard-fueled burn. Cast Down can clear key blockers or deal 2 damage to controllers. Use incremental damage and card advantage to close out games when the opponent is at low life or out of answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is at low life, or you have removed their main answers. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns and have enough interaction to protect your board from sweepers or targeted removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a locked combo, have no interaction against a sweeper-plus-finisher strategy, or are consistently dead to topdecks. If the opponent has 3+ removal and you have no threats, or if your graveyard is empty and you lack a win condition, consider folding to preserve resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter or can be cast in response. Hold up interaction for key spells and use sacrifice outlets to bypass counters if they target spells. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their win condition.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use Cast Down on key ramp creatures and don't let them stabilize. Trade efficiently and close out before they hit their big threats. Prioritize graveyard hate if they rely on recursion.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and discard. Use Ichor Wellspring to draw into answers and transition to midrange threats once stabilized. Don't overextend into board wipes or sacrifice outlets prematurely. Focus on surviving turns 1-3 and out-value them in the midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with midrange threats and use disruption to protect your board. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their win condition. Win through attrition by maintaining card advantage and closing out games before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through better resource management and sideboard cards. Prioritize graveyard advantage and targeted removal. Trade efficiently and out-value them in the late game. Use Fanatical Offering to convert excess creatures into value, and track their removal to time your commits.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the matchup. Against control/combo, increase disruption and late-game finishers. Against aggro, prioritize early interaction and hate cards. In the mirror, lean into graveyard synergy and targeted removal. Always keep at least one copy of Cast Down and a land ramp source, and avoid overcommitting to sideboard cards that don't fit the primary game plan.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without removal, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers, and mismanaging Ichor Wellspring's mana/draw engine, leading to dead draws. Unique interaction: Fanatical Offering can be used to sacrifice low-cost creatures for value, but avoid sacrificing your only win condition. Always track opponent's graveyard and removal count, and remember that Cast Down's 2 damage to its controller can be used strategically to finish off low-life threats or disrupt combo pieces.

philosophy: Accelerate mana with efficient dorks to cast massive threats and pump spells, overwhelming opponents through combat damage before they can stabilize. Excels against slow, reactive decks that lack early interaction or hate cards.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands and at least one mana dork (Llanowar/Fyndhorn). Mulligan to 4+ lands with no dorks, or hands with only dorks and no lands. Prioritize hands that can play a dork on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, then Llanowar Elves. Turn 2: Play land, then Fyndhorn Elves or second Llanowar. Turn 3: Play a big threat (e.g., Avenging Hunter) or pump spell, using 3-4 mana. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your threat on time.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Bring in Bojuka Bog, Grafdigger's Cage, or Rest in Peace vs. combo/graveyard. Bring in direct damage or small creatures vs. aggro (Mono Red Madness). Bring in disruption or card advantage vs. control (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire). Keep mainboard intact vs. mirror.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana acceleration over card draw early. Use pump spells efficiently to maximize combat damage. Don't overcommit to board if opponent has sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough mana to answer or out-value removal. Hold back if opponent has potential sweepers or hate. Use combat tricks to trade favorably.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), hate cards (Cage, Bog), and counters. Note when opponent taps out for a big spell vs. holding mana for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Avenging Hunter + pump spells, or multiple large creatures with +1/+1 counters. Win through combat or direct damage if needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ mana, opponent has no sweepers, and you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 life, opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're completely locked out by hate cards with no way to break through.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats. Cast dorks early to build mana advantage. Use pump spells in response to counters if needed.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with card draw or efficient removal. Don't overextend; wait for them to tap out.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early threats to race. Use pump spells to stabilize. Bring in small creatures or removal post-sideboard.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply early pressure. Don't let them stabilize. Use card advantage to outlast them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the mana race. Prioritize dorks and efficient threats. Use disruption to slow their ramp. Trade favorably in combat.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the matchup. Against hate-heavy decks, bring in ways to bypass or destroy hate. Against fast decks, prioritize stabilization and racing. Maintain a balance between ramp and interaction.
notes: Common mistake: Overextending into sweepers. Always count opponent's mana and board state. Watch for Blood Moon effects. Use pump spells to maximize value. Avenging Hunter likely has a specific ability; treat it as a finisher.

philosophy: Grixis Affinity accelerates into a board of artifact creatures using cheap mana and cantrip engines, leveraging flexible removal and card advantage to outpace midrange and control while punishing slow aggro. It wins by converting artifact density into immediate pressure and resilient value.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana accelerator or cheap artifact, and a turn-1/2 play (Arcbound Ravager, Myr Enforcer, or Thoughtcast paired with an artifact). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early artifacts, or hands with only removal/draw spells and no way to cast them on curve.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then a cheap artifact or mana rock. If you have a cantrip, cast it to dig. Turn 2: Deploy an Arcbound Ravager or Myr Enforcer, using artifacts for mana or +1/+1 counters. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your colors for Thoughtcast or Galvanic Blast if needed, but prioritize artifact acceleration.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Blue Terror (Control): Bring in more pressure, discard, and ways to bypass counters. vs Mono Red Madness (Aggro): Bring in artifact hate, more removal, and life total stabilization. vs Jund Wildfire (Midrange): Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and card advantage. vs Elves (Combo): Bring in artifact hate, sweepers, and early pressure. vs Grixis Affinity (Mirror): Bring in extra counters, discard, and artifact recursion/protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Thoughtcast to cycle cheap artifacts and draw spells, keeping hand size stable. Don't overcommit mana to artifacts if you're low on interaction. Protect your cantrip engine; it's your lifeline.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent has no answers. Hold back if they have sweepers or targeted removal. Use Galvanic Blast efficiently on key threats or to clear blockers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and combo win conditions. Note when they tap out for a single spell—that's your window to attack or cast Thoughtcast.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully powered Arcbound Ravager or Myr Enforcer, often buffed by artifacts or combat tricks. Reckoner's Bargain can also close games by drawing you into a win condition or enabling a graveyard combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers, or you've established card advantage that they can't match.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no artifacts to cast, and the opponent has a board of removal or a combo that resolves on their next turn. Also concede if you're on the draw against a control deck with multiple counters and no way to break through.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtcast to dig for multiple spells, forcing them to counter multiple threats. Cast artifacts and spells at unexpected times (end step, after they tap out). Use Galvanic Blast to remove their key counterspell sources if possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use Galvanic Blast to remove their ramp sources or early threats. Don't let them stabilize; keep the board state volatile.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and life total. Use artifacts as blockers early. Reckoner's Bargain can stabilize your hand. Don't overextend into combat tricks.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total while they dig for answers. Use Thoughtcast to out-draw them. Force them to use interaction on low-impact artifacts to set up bigger threats later.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtcast and card draw to out-value them. Use discard to strip their key artifacts. Protect your own engine with counters or protection.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure acceleration to a more interactive midrange game plan. Add more targeted removal, discard, and hate cards against specific archetypes. Reduce pure mana rocks if facing control, and increase pressure tools against combo.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to artifacts without interaction, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Unique interaction: Thoughtcast can be used to cycle artifacts into the graveyard for Reckoner's Bargain or other synergies. Always track their hand size; if they have no cards, they're likely on a combo or control plan.

philosophy: Mono Red Rally aims to cast an excessive number of spells per turn to trigger rally synergies, generating incremental value through damage, card draw, and board presence. It wins by out-resourcing slower decks through cumulative pressure and racing faster ones with instant-speed disruption and explosive turns.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one turn-1 spell (Galvanic Blast or a cheap rally creature), and a way to chain spells (instants/sorceries). Mulligan to 4+ cards if missing lands or turn-1 play. Keep 3-card hands only if they contain a complete turn-1 package (land + spell + rally enabler).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast a cheap spell (Galvanic Blast for tempo or a rally creature). Turn 2: Play land, cast another spell to trigger rally, develop board. Prioritize playing lands on curve to enable spell density. Sequence instants to maximize rally triggers before combat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: +2 Galvanic Blast, +1 Wrenn's Resolve, -2 creatures. Against Jund Wildfire: +2 Wrenn's Resolve, +1 discard/removal, -2 lands. Against Elves/Mono Red Madness: +2 direct damage, +1 artifact destruction, -2 value creatures.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve instant-speed spells for rally triggers and interaction. Don't overextend into sweepers. Track graveyard if relevant, but focus on hand density for spell chaining.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough spells to trigger rally and survive interaction. Hold back instants for protection or finishing blows. Use Wrenn's Resolve to stabilize or close games.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counters, or removal. Note when opponents tap out for big plays. Track artifact counts (vs Affinity) or hand size (vs Madness).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Wrenn's Resolve planeswalker abilities, Galvanic Blast chains for lethal, or a fully buffed Goblin Bushwhacker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 3+ spells in hand, a land drop, and the board state allows it. Go all-in if opponent has no sweepers or counters.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 lands with no spells, facing a locked board or massive sweepers. Against control with no win conditions or against fast aggro with no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast spells in response to their counters, use cheap spells to trigger rally before they can respond, sideboard in more uncounterable or redundant threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Play fast, use direct damage to interrupt ramp steps, sideboard in artifact destruction or early interaction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Galvanic Blast for removal, hold back Wrenn's Resolve for value, track life totals carefully.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, use instant-speed spells to disrupt their mana base or planeswalkers, win before they stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Race for life total, track hand size, prioritize discard or direct damage, win the spell-chaining race.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure spell density to matchup-specific tools. Against control, add more finishers and protection. Against aggro, add early interaction and life gain if available. Always keep at least one copy of Wrenn's Resolve and Galvanic Blast unless heavily matchup-specific.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to rally triggers without considering board state. Always count spells in hand before casting. Wrenn's Resolve is fragile; protect it or use it at end step. Galvanic Blast can be used for both removal and damage, so prioritize based on board state.

philosophy: Assemble three Urza lands to cast 7-mana threats like Emrakul or Ugin, outscaling opponents with card advantage and board wipes while using early disruption to survive the midgame.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably Urza’s Mine/Power Plant/Tower), one mana accelerator (Sol Ring/Arcane Signet), and at least one early interaction or discard. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no Urzas, or hands with only accelerators and no lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or use discard on a key threat. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another accelerator or discard, and begin sequencing Urza lands into your mana base. Prioritize untapping lands that enable Tron by turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo: Bring in Relic of Progenitus, discard spells, and artifact hate. vs Control/Blue Terror: Bring in Koth, Emrakul, and additional discard to pressure through counters. vs Elves/Affinity: Bring in Relic, artifact destruction, and life-gain spells. vs Mirror: Bring in Ugin, additional Emrakul, and graveyard hate to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana until you can untap three Urza lands simultaneously. Use Relic of Progenitus to protect your graveyard from recursion or combo engines. Use Lembas sparingly for life or to enable Tron if mana is tight.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can protect them or when you have a clear path to victory. Hold back Emrakul if the board is too unstable; use Koth to clear lands or Ugin to reset if needed.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard recursion (Elves, Jund), artifact chains (Affinity), and counterspells (Blue Terror, Grixis). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emrakul, the Promised End (draws cards, bounces threats, wins via card advantage), Ugin, the Spirit Dragon (board wipe + card draw), or Koth, the Warhammer (destroys lands, pumps, and attacks).
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have assembled Tron, have answered or outpaced the opponent’s interaction, and can safely untap your threat. If you have Emrakul and they have no removal, cast it immediately.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re consistently mulliganed to dead hands, face a locked-out board with no removal, or are in a matchup where your win conditions are completely neutralized (e.g., no Tron assembly against aggressive decks with no early interaction).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Koth to destroy lands and break their mana, or cast Emrakul/Ugin when they’re tapped out. Use discard to remove counterspells before you cast your threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with accelerators, use Relic to stop their graveyard ramp, and pressure with Koth or early discard to disrupt their land drops.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to remove key threats, hold up interaction for turn 2-3, and stabilize with Koth or early Emrakul if possible. Use Lembas for life to survive until your big threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with discard and Koth to destroy their lands. Use Emrakul to bypass counterspells and generate card advantage. Don’t overcommit to one threat; have backup plans.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with Ugin or Emrakul, using Relic to deny their graveyard value, and leveraging discard to strip their key spells. Prioritize card advantage and graveyard hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure ramp to a more interactive midrange game plan. Add targeted hate (Relic, artifact destruction, counterspell hate) and adjust your threat curve based on metagame trends. Keep your Tron assembly intact but ensure you have answers for the top 3 matchups.
notes: Candy Trail is not a recognized Magic card; focus on standard Tron staples like Urza’s lands, Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, and Koth/Emrakul/Ugin. Common mistake: untapping Urza lands individually instead of saving them for a simultaneous Tron activation. Always count your lands and accelerators to guarantee a turn 3 Tron.

philosophy: Golgari Gardens is a resilient token midrange deck that wins by generating a growing board of Saprolings, using them as fuel for efficient removal and card advantage, and leveraging recurring threats like Avenging Hunter to out-value opponents in attrition wars.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or fetches if available, otherwise basics that hit B/G), at least one early interaction or threat, and a curve-1/2 play. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no early plays, or only removal without threats or mana development.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Develop mana and cast a cheap creature or land, then use Defile on your own creature/token to generate a Saproling, or on an opponent's threat if ahead. Turn 3: Deploy Avenging Hunter or other midrange threats, using Saprolings to fuel Fanatical Offering for removal and card draw. Sequence lands to ensure B/G mana by turn 2/3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in additional cheap removal (Defile, Fanatical Offering) and early threats. vs Control/Blue Terror: Add card draw, hand disruption, and late-game finishers. vs Combo/Grixis Affinity: Bring in artifact hate, extra removal, and graveyard disruption. vs Mirror: Prioritize removal and card advantage, sideboard in targeted hate for specific threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Saprolings as currency. Use them to fuel Fanatical Offering for removal and draw, but preserve enough to maintain board presence and fuel Avenging Hunter's death triggers. Manage your graveyard carefully if the format contains hate cards; otherwise, leverage it for recursion and value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or card advantage to follow up. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy spot removal. Use Defile to clear key blockers or pump your own board, and time Fanatical Offering to trade up efficiently.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Terror), spot removal, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents save removal for your Avenging Hunter or key Saproling generators. Track graveyard usage and opponent's hand size to gauge their ability to answer your board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A board of 4+ Saprolings, Avenging Hunter, and efficient removal. Win by trading up with Fanatical Offering, pumping your board with Defile, and racing to lethal or out-grinding control through card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for key threats, and the opponent is low on answers. Commit when you can protect your board or when the opponent has no sweepers or spot removal left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one life with no removal, face a locked board, or are facing a combo that has already assembled its win condition. Also concede if you are completely out of resources in a control mirror or facing a format-specific lock.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use removal that doesn't target spells, cast threats in response to counterspells, and rely on board presence that doesn't require stacking spells. Prioritize instant-speed interaction and value engines that don't rely on the stack.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use efficient removal to stop key ramp creatures, and race to close the game before they stabilize. Use Defile to clear early threats and Fanatical Offering to trade up.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Defile and Fanatical Offering, use Saprolings to block and trade, and transition to midrange once the initial assault is weathered. Prioritize survival over aggression in the first two turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, use card advantage to out-value them, and close out games quickly before they can stabilize. Use removal efficiently to clear blockers and avoid overcommitting to a single threat.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by better resource management, superior sequencing, and sideboard adjustments. Prioritize card advantage and removal, and be willing to trade up with Fanatical Offering. Control the board state and force unfavorable trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your removal and card draw to the specific matchup. Against aggro, increase early interaction and reduce high-cost threats. Against control/combo, add more card advantage, hand disruption, and late-game finishers. In mirrors, prioritize removal and card advantage, and consider sideboarding in additional graveyard hate or artifact destruction if needed.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting Saprolings to Fanatical Offering without considering board state. Always weigh the trade-off between removal/draw and board presence. Defile can be used on your own creatures to grow your board, but don't waste it if you're ahead. Avenging Hunter's death trigger is a key win condition; protect it or plan for its eventual sacrifice. Watch for format-specific hate cards like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void.

philosophy: Dimir Faeries operates as a tempo-control archetype that disrupts the opponent’s early game with cheap flying threats and efficient counterspells, then transitions into a resilient midgame where Lorien Revealed provides card advantage and an evasive win condition. It excels against spell-reliant and slow-combo strategies while requiring careful resource management to survive fast aggro and heavy ramp.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands (prioritizing duals or fixing), at least one Spellstutter Sprite or equivalent turn-1 threat, and a counterspell or removal suite. Mulligan if you have three or more lands with no early plays, or three or more spells with no lands. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn-1 Sprite or turn-2 Lorien Revealed while retaining mana for disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Spellstutter Sprite to establish early disruption and board presence. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Lorien Revealed or another threat, or hold up mana for counterspells/removal. Sequence lands to ensure consistent blue/black mana. If the opponent casts a key spell, use counterspells or Sprite’s sacrifice ability to disrupt their plan. Never tap out for Lorien Revealed if you lack protection or disruption.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in Snuff Out, additional targeted removal, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control/combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Keep counterspells, bring in card advantage and graveyard hate if applicable. Against Jund Wildfire: Bring in more removal, permanent hate, and ways to disrupt their mana/land destruction. In the mirror: Keep disruption, bring in additional counterspells and targeted removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Manage mana to hold up for counterspells and removal while developing Lorien Revealed. Use Spellstutter Sprite’s sacrifice ability sparingly for high-impact spells. Track your graveyard for potential recursion threats and avoid overcommitting to Lorien tokens if the board is unstable. Prioritize card advantage to outlast opponents in longer games.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have board presence or can protect them with counterspells and removal. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Lorien Revealed to stabilize against aggro or to pressure control decks. Maintain flexibility by keeping mana open for interaction rather than overextending into vulnerable positions.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., board wipes, tide effects), heavy removal, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for key spells to identify windows for disruption. Track the graveyard for potential recursion or graveyard-based strategies. Pay attention to mana development to anticipate when opponents can cast their most threatening spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed token army, resilient flying creatures, or targeted removal to clear the path. Use card advantage to outlast opponents and close out games before they can stabilize. Leverage flash removal to answer late-game threats and secure victory.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Lorien Revealed on the board, sufficient mana to protect it, and the opponent has no sweepers or heavy removal. Also commit when you’ve disrupted their key spells and can close out before they recover. Use token generation to overwhelm opponents who lack board wipes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a fast aggro deck with no removal, if you’re swept and have no recovery tools, or if the opponent has a combo that resolves and you lack answers. Also concede if you’re out of resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory, or if the matchup is mathematically unwinnable due to resource disparity.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Spellstutter Sprite to make their counterspells more expensive. Cast threats in response to their counterspells to exploit the stack. Use flash removal (Snuff Out) to answer threats after they resolve. Prioritize card advantage to out-resource their disruption.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early disruption and removal to slow their acceleration. Use Snuff Out to answer big threats before they can stabilize. Lorien Revealed can outvalue ramp if you survive the early game. Disrupt their mana sources and key spells to prevent them from reaching their win conditions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early threats to race, use removal to clear key attackers, and hold up mana for counterspells and removal. Lorien Revealed can stabilize and turn the board. Prioritize survival over aggression, and use card advantage to outlast their initial burst.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with flying threats, disrupt their draw steps, and use card advantage to outlast. Lorien Revealed provides a win condition they can’t easily answer. Force them to spend resources on interaction while you build an insurmountable board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, protect Lorien Revealed, and use counterspells to disrupt their key spells. Win the removal race by targeting their threats efficiently. Track their mana to identify windows for disruption and avoid overcommitting into potential sweepers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against aggro-heavy formats, increase removal and hate cards to stabilize early. Against control/combo, add card advantage and graveyard hate to out-resource opponents. In mirrors, prioritize counterspells and targeted removal to win the interaction war. Always ensure your mana base can support the sideboarded cards and maintain flexibility for unexpected matchups.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Lorien Revealed without protection, misusing Spellstutter Sprite’s sacrifice ability on low-impact spells, and failing to track opponent mana for sweepers. Unique interactions: Sprite’s ability can be used to counter your own spells if needed, and Lorien Revealed tokens are flying and can be pumped. Always prioritize keeping your board state flexible and avoid tapping out for non-essential plays. Practice stack manipulation to maximize disruption and protect your key permanents.

philosophy: Rapidly cycle your deck using draw/discard outlets to fuel the graveyard, then cast cheap direct damage spells via Madness to race opponents to zero life. This deck wins by outpacing control and midrange strategies through relentless discard-and-burn loops, turning your graveyard into a secondary hand of instant-speed damage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetchable or duals), at least one discard outlet (Alms of the Vein, Vampire's Kiss, or Grab the Prize), and 1-2 cheap burn spells. Keep if you can play a land and cast an outlet or burn on turn 1 or 2. Mulligan on pure burn with no outlets, pure outlets with no mana, or hands with 3+ lands and no play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast discard outlet if mana allows, or play land. Turn 2: Cast second outlet or burn spell. Sequence: Cast outlet -> opponent discards or you discard -> cast burn from graveyard via Madness -> repeat. Prioritize finding more outlets over burn early to establish the engine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: Bring in targeted discard (Duress/Thoughtseize), artifact hate (Shatterstorm/Null Rod), and extra burn. vs Jund Wildfire/Elves: Bring in faster burn, life management, and discard to disrupt their mana/engines. vs Mono Red Madness: Keep board, bring in redundant discard, extra burn, and artifact hate if they run artifacts.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hold mana for instant-speed burn responses, but commit to casting burn via Madness as soon as you have a discard outlet on the battlefield. Never hoard burn spells in hand; intentionally discard them to fuel the graveyard. Track your graveyard burn count and opponent's life total closely.
midgame_priorities.board_control: This is a face-race deck with minimal board presence. Commit burn spells aggressively. Only hold back if facing a massive board wipe, if you're at lethal next turn and opponent has a clear, or if you need to preserve mana for a critical discard to strip a key answer.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, board wipes, and opponent discard outlets. Note if the opponent is playing draw spells (they're trying to out-value you) or discard (they're disrupting your graveyard). Track when they tap out for big threats or answers to time your burn spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lethal burn spells cast from the graveyard via Madness. Chain multiple cheap burns (Shock, Lightning Strike, Lightning Helix, etc.) to close out the game. Use discard outlets to dig for the final burn spell if needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 2+ burn spells in the graveyard and a discard outlet on the battlefield, or when you're at 3 or less life and can deal lethal this turn. Commit fully if the opponent has no removal or counterspells in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're at 5+ life, have no burn in the graveyard, no outlets in hand/graveyard, and the opponent has a board wipe or counterspell ready. Also concede against locked combo or unbreakable midrange boards where you cannot disrupt or race.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells from hand before committing. Cast burn spells in response to their counterspells if you have priority, or sequence your discard outlets to force them to counter multiple spells. Rely on graveyard recursion to bypass single-target counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum early pressure. Do not let them stabilize. Use discard to hit their mana dorks, land drops, or early threats. If they get a big board, burn it down or race past it. Prioritize life total over board presence.
threat_responses.against_aggro: You are the aggro. Do not slow down. Use discard to disrupt their early plays and key threats. If they get too wide, use any available burn to clear key threats, but prioritize closing the game over board control.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Discard their removal and counterspells. If they try to grind, you must win before they find their answer. Use discard to strip key answers, then burn through their life total. Avoid trading burn for their removal unless it's lethal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Race to discard. Prioritize keeping discard outlets. Use your own discard to strip their outlets and burn spells. Win by having more burn in the graveyard, better sequencing, or out-drawing them with your outlets. Respect their discard and time your own carefully.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Against control/combo, increase discard and artifact hate. Against midrange/ramp, add faster burn and life management. In mirrors, keep discard heavy and add redundant burn. Always carry flexible answers like Shatterstorm or Duress for unknown matchups, and remember to sideboard out redundant burn if the format shifts toward longer games.
notes: Common mistake: Hoarding burn spells in hand instead of discarding them to fuel the graveyard. Remember, discard outlets are your engine; burn spells are your fuel. If you discard a burn spell to an opponent's effect, it still goes to the graveyard and can be cast via Madness. Ensure you have priority to cast from the graveyard, and watch out for 'discard to graveyard' vs 'discard to graveyard for Madness' timing. In Unknown format, verify format-specific restrictions, but the core loop remains consistent.

philosophy: White Aggro aims to overwhelm opponents with efficient, high-impact creatures and spot removal, leveraging card advantage engines to maintain relentless pressure. It excels against slow, combo-dependent, or control-heavy decks that struggle to answer a wide board of flyers and evasive threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a 1-drop (Novice Inspector) or a 2-drop (Kor Skyfisher). Keep hands with Thraben Charm if paired with a creature or land. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 3+ mana costs, or hands with multiple charms but no threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Novice Inspector (or Kor Skyfisher if you have 2W). If you have a charm, use it to clear a potential blocker or protect your attacker. Land sequencing: Prioritize basic plains; sequence fetch/shock lands to ensure no untapped lands on turn 1/2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Jund Wildfire): +2/4 Thraben Charm, +2/4 additional flyers/removal. vs Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +2/4 flyers, +2/4 protection/removal. vs Elves: +2/4 spot removal, +2/4 flyers. vs Mirror: +2/4 removal, +2/4 flyers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Don't overextend into sweepers. Use Thraben Charm's protection mode to save key threats from spot removal. Keep a card in hand for interaction. Graveyard is generally irrelevant for mono-white aggro, but watch for opponent's recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit when you have card advantage or when the opponent has no removal. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal unanswered. Use flyers to trade up or pressure the face.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, etc.), spot removal (Path, Swords), and combo win conditions. Note when they tap out for a counter or removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kor Skyfisher and other flyers are primary finishers. Use Thraben Charm's damage mode to close out games. Equipment or direct damage can finish.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 3+ threats on board, opponent has no removal/sweeper, and you can attack for lethal or force a trade that leaves you ahead.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1 life, opponent has a board wipe ready, you have no threats, and your hand is empty. Also concede against combo decks that have assembled their win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thraben Charm's protection mode on key spells. Attack with multiple threats to force multiple counters. Don't overcommit to a single expensive threat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early. Don't let them stabilize. Use removal on their mana dorks or early threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Trade efficiently. Use Thraben Charm to protect your blockers or remove their key attackers. Don't overextend into their combat tricks.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Use card draw to out-value them. Force them to use removal on low-value threats to exhaust their hand.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades. Use Thraben Charm to protect your threats or remove theirs. Prioritize card advantage. Flying threats win combat.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw aggression to a more resilient, value-oriented game plan. Bring in more spot removal and protection for key threats. Adjust land count if needed, and consider bringing in additional flyers or card draw to outlast longer games.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without assessing sweepers. Unique interaction: Thraben Charm's modes can be split across targets if you have multiple targets, but typically used on one. Watch out for opponent's 'bounce' or 'tuck' effects. Always keep a land in play to ensure you can cast your threats on curve.

philosophy: Spy Combo seeks to disrupt the opponent's key permanents and resources while accelerating its own mana, then executing a stealthy, multi-step combo that leverages land drops and evasion to close out the game. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific permanents or stacked counters, while struggling against fast aggro that can race its disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one land acceleration spell (Land Grant), and a disruption piece (Masked Vandal) or early combo piece. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no acceleration, or 3+ non-lands with no way to play them on turn 1 or 2. Keep any hand with a turn-1 play that sets up your combo or disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Masked Vandal if possible, or hold for a turn-2 Land Grant + Vandal. Turn 2: Cast Land Grant to accelerate into Generous Ent or a combo piece. Prioritize playing lands untapped to enable mana-heavy spells. If facing aggro, consider holding Vandal for removal; if facing control/combo, prioritize acceleration.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional counterspell alternatives, and faster threats. Against Mono Red Madness/Jund Wildfire: Bring in board wipes, life gain, and direct damage. Against Elves: Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and faster combo pieces. Against mirror: Bring in extra disruption and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for key combo turns or disruption windows. Use Land Grant strategically to ensure you can cast Generous Ent or combo pieces without flooding. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary discards unless it enables your combo.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can follow up. Hold back Generous Ent if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use Masked Vandal to remove key permanents (artifacts, enchantments, or combo pieces) rather than trading into small creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and targeted removal. Note when opponents tap out for key permanents. Track graveyard contents for reanimation or discard threats. Pay attention to mana bases for potential flood/screw.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Generous Ent combined with land acceleration and evasion/stealth mechanics to close out the game. Execute the combo when you have protection or can bypass removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a guaranteed combo turn, protection from removal, or when the opponent is tapped out for a sweeper. Also commit if you're ahead on board and can race.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no stabilization tools, if you're completely locked out of your combo with no draw support, or if the opponent has multiple sweepers and targeted removal in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use uncounterable spells, split your combo into multiple steps, or use land acceleration to cast threats before they can respond. Hold up mana for protection or use cheap disruption to clear the way.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Masked Vandal to disrupt their mana base. Use targeted removal on key ramp creatures. Accelerate your own game plan to outpace them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize stabilization with disruption and targeted removal. Use Generous Ent as a late-game win condition if you survive the early game. Don't overcommit to combo if you're below 10 life.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with threats and disruption. Force them to use resources on your board while you set up your combo. Use land acceleration to outpace their card draw.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards, disrupting their combo pieces, and using Masked Vandal to remove their key permanents. Prioritize card advantage and protection for your combo.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against control/combo, add more disruption and protection. Against aggro/ramp, add early interaction and sweepers. Always sideboard in card draw to ensure you hit your combo pieces. Adjust your land count if you're flooding or screwing.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the combo too early without protection. Another: Misusing Masked Vandal on small creatures instead of key permanents. Unique interaction: Land Grant can enable landfall or land-based combo triggers. Always track opponent's mana and hand size to predict responses.

philosophy: Gruul Ponza is a disruptive midrange archetype that wins by systematically dismantling the opponent’s mana base with cheap land destruction while deploying efficient, escalating threats. It excels against combo and control strategies that rely on specific mana or board presence, leveraging Utopia Sprawl and resilient creatures to out-value and outlast opponents.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing basics or duals that can untap), at least one 1-2 mana threat, and a land destruction spell. Keep Utopia Sprawl only if paired with a 2-drop threat or ramp. Mulligan pure land, pure removal, or hands missing basic land sources needed for turn 1-2 plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic Forest or dual land, cast a 1-mana threat or discard spell. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a 2-mana threat or Utopia Sprawl if you have a target. Prioritize establishing board presence while simultaneously disrupting opponent’s mana with Acid-Moss/Thermokarst on their non-basics or key utility lands. Against ramp, prioritize land destruction early to slow acceleration.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in spot removal, additional early threats, and Utopia Sprawl for defense. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more land destruction, graveyard hate, and additional Utopia Sprawl/escalation. vs Elves/Affinity: Bring in artifact hate, additional land destruction, and board wipes. vs Mirror: Bring in additional disruption, discard, and efficient threats to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Optimize mana for casting threats and land destruction in the same turn. Preserve hand disruption for key turns. Don't overcommit to Utopia Sprawl without a reliable target; use it to close games or stabilize. Sequence land destruction to break the most impactful lands first.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you've established mana denial or when the opponent is vulnerable to Utopia Sprawl. Hold back against heavy removal or combo turns. Prioritize efficient trades: use creatures to block/kill threats while preserving land destruction for mana or key permanents.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch opponent's land drops (basics vs non-basics), graveyard activity, and mana development. Key tells: Holding mana on turn 3-4 often signals combo/control. Playing multiple non-basics early suggests vulnerability to land destruction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Utopia Sprawl on a resilient threat combined with efficient creatures is the primary win condition. Once mana is disrupted and board control is established, swing with boosted threats to pressure for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you've broken key mana, they're low on removal, or you have a clear path to lethal with Utopia Sprawl + threats. Also commit when they're on the draw and you can pressure them before they stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing an un-disruptable fast combo, if your hand is completely dead against their strategy (e.g., only land destruction against a mana-independent deck), or if you're down to one card and they have a clear win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter or counter them with discard. Sequence land destruction to break their mana so they can't cast counters later. Utopia Sprawl is cheap enough to cast in response to their mana development.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early land destruction to slow acceleration. Use Utopia Sprawl to stabilize against early threats. Trade efficiently and don't let them get ahead on board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early threats to race, use Utopia Sprawl to block and counter-attack, and hold land destruction for key utility lands. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and stabilize before going for the win.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats, use land destruction to break mana and force basics, and use Utopia Sprawl to swing for lethal. Don't let them stabilize; keep pressure on and disrupt as needed.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by playing more efficiently, using land destruction to break their mana, and leveraging Utopia Sprawl to close out games. Prioritize hand disruption and efficient threats to out-value them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land base if needed and bring in matchup-specific hate. Increase disruption and Utopia Sprawl against control/combo, and increase early threats and spot removal against aggro. Ensure a clear win condition post-sideboard and avoid overloading on one-sided hate cards that leave you vulnerable to other archetypes.
notes: Common mistakes include overusing land destruction on non-impactful lands, holding Utopia Sprawl too long without a target, and mismanaging mana to cast threats and disruption in the same turn. Play the long game, disrupt their plan, and close out with Utopia Sprawl + threats. Avoid overcommitting early; Ponza thrives in mid-to-late game resource wars and wins by out-resourcing opponents rather than racing.

philosophy: Jeskai Ephemerate is a tempo-control combo deck that aims to assemble an infinite mana loop using Lorien Revealed and Ephemerate to generate overwhelming card advantage, board wipes, or direct damage. It wins by out-resourcing control and combo decks while stabilizing against aggro with precise counterspells and bounce effects before closing out games with Skred or repeated ETB triggers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 or turn-2 land, and at least one of: Ephemerate, Lorien Revealed, Counterspell, or a cheap bounce target/creature. Keep hands that can sequence into a turn-2 Lorien or turn-2 Ephemerate. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early interaction or combo pieces, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands lacking a way to protect your combo. Prioritize hands with 2 lands and 3+ spells if you have a turn-1 land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap cantrip or hold up mana for interaction. Turn 2: Cast Lorien Revealed if you have a land, or cast Ephemerate if you have a land and a bounce target. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your 2-drop and 3-drop turns. If opening with Lorien, play land, cast Lorien, and hold up Ephemerate next turn. If opening with Ephemerate, play land, cast Ephemerate, and hold up Lorien or a creature/land that can be bounced. Always hold up Counterspell if you suspect interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in additional counterspells, discard spells, and cheap removal. Reduce land count if needed. vs Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Keep core combo pieces, bring in more counterspells, bounce targets, and hand disruption. vs Jund Wildfire (midrange/control): Bring in more interaction, graveyard hate if applicable, and additional finishers. vs Mirror: Keep everything, side in extra counterspells and discard to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for counterspells and bounce effects; never overcommit to the board without protection. Use the Lorien + Ephemerate loop for card advantage and board wipes, but track opponent's interaction to avoid dead mana. Manage your hand by keeping answers until you know what the opponent is doing. Graveyard management is secondary unless facing graveyard-dependent decks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection (counterspell, bounce, or a resolved loop). Use Ephemerate to reset unfavorable boards or protect key combo pieces. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, heavy interaction, or a clear path to closing. Prioritize controlling the board over racing, unless Skred can close the game safely.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation, Cruel Ultimatum), counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or combo pieces. Track if they have discard or hand disruption. Pay attention to their mana base and land drops to predict their next turn's capabilities.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The Lorien Revealed + Ephemerate infinite mana loop is the primary win condition, allowing you to cast multiple spells, board wipes, or finish with Skred. Skred can also close games quickly if you can bounce it for extra damage or cast it uncounterably. Use repeated ETB triggers from bounced permanents to overwhelm opponents.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection (counterspell, bounce, or a resolved loop), the opponent is tapped out, or you can close the game before they can interact. Commit to the loop when you have enough mana to generate card advantage and board control, and when you've tracked that the opponent lacks sweepers or counterspells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one life, the opponent has a guaranteed sweeper + finisher, or you have no way to break through their board/control. Concede if you are completely locked out of your combo and the opponent has a clear path to winning.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Sequence spells to trade counterspells, using cheap, uncounterable threats or bounce effects to bypass counterspells. Target permanents already on the battlefield to avoid stack interaction. Use the Lorien loop to generate enough value to out-resource their counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use counterspells to stop key ramp spells. Bounce their mana sources if possible. Close games quickly with Skred before they can stabilize. Use the Lorien loop to draw into answers and out-value their ramp.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with counterspells and bounce effects. Use the Lorien loop to draw into answers and stabilize the board. Don't overcommit to the board. Use Skred to pressure their life total while you set up your combo.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and bounce effects. Use Skred to pressure their life total while they are locked down. Force them to use resources on your threats, then close the game with the Lorien loop or repeated ETB triggers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by using counterspells to protect your combo and disrupt theirs. Bounce their Lorien or Ephemerate to break their loop. Win by out-resourcing, closing faster, or forcing them to make mistakes in the stack.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your deck based on the metagame. Bring in more interaction vs control and combo, more aggression vs slow decks, and additional protection vs hate cards. Keep Lorien Revealed, Ephemerate, and Counterspell as your core. Adjust land counts if you're facing faster or slower decks. Prioritize sideboard cards that protect your combo or disrupt the opponent's game plan.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without protection, misplaying the Lorien + Ephemerate loop, and failing to track opponent's interaction. Unique interaction: Ephemerate can bounce itself to trigger Lorien repeatedly, generating infinite mana. Watch for opponent's graveyard hate if they run it. Format-specific rules may apply, so always check the banned list and format rules. Practice stack interactions and timing to maximize your combo potential.

philosophy: Caw-Gates is a control-ramp hybrid that stabilizes the early game with efficient removal and hand disruption, applies moderate pressure with cheap flyers, and transitions to a game-winning Lorien Revealed combo. It excels against midrange and control decks by out-resourcing them, while relying on early answers to survive aggro and combo.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early threat (Squadron Hawk, Knight of the Reliquary), and a removal spell (Prismatic Strands, Lightning Bolt, or Path/Swords). Drop hands with multiple Lorien Revealed, no early interaction, or unplayable mana curves. Prioritize hands that can establish a board or disrupt the opponent by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast Knight of the Reliquary or Squadron Hawk. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Hawk or use Bolt/Strands on a threat. Sequence WUR mana by tapping out for a Forest/Plains via Knight if needed. Use Knight to fetch lands for early Lorien setup or to protect against graveyard hate. Avoid overcommitting mana before turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red, Elves): +3 Prismatic Strands, +2 Lightning Bolt, -2 Lorien Revealed, -1 land. vs Control (Blue Terror, Grixis): +2 Thoughtseize/Discard, +2 additional removal, -2 aggro hate. vs Combo: +3 Prismatic Strands, +2 discard, -2 Lorien. vs Mirror: +2 Prismatic Strands, +1 discard, -1 land. Adjust based on specific metagame reads.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve removal for high-value targets; don't waste Strands on chump blockers. Use Knight of the Reliquary to fetch lands for Lorien or to protect your graveyard from hate. Manage hand size by trading discard for key threats or answers. Avoid casting Lorien without board control or backup threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have at least one answer to the opponent's primary win condition. Hold back Lorien until you've stabilized or forced the opponent to use their removal. Use Squadron Hawk to apply pressure while keeping options open for removal or Lorien activation.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Engineered Plague, Bloodline Culling) and board wipes (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or combo pieces. Track discard targets to anticipate their game plan. Be alert to artifact hate if facing Affinity.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed paired with Squadron Hawk and other creatures generates infinite flying damage. If Lorien is disrupted, close with a board of Hawks and efficient removal. Sometimes a single Bolt or Strands on a key threat is enough to stabilize and race.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, sufficient mana for Lorien + activation, and can protect it or have backup threats. Commit when the opponent has no removal for your creatures or Lorien, or when you can force them to use their answers on lesser threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has Rest in Peace/Engineered Plague and you cannot kill them before they stabilize, or if you're facing a fast combo you cannot disrupt. Concede if you're down to one card, opponent has a board wipe + threat, or you have no way to answer their primary win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats and discard to remove counterspells. Cast Lorien in response to something or when they're tapped out. Use Knight to protect Lorien or fetch lands to bypass counters. Don't overcommit to a single spell.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prismatic Strands is your primary tool; use it to remove ramp creatures or protect your board. Use discard to hit ramp spells early. Kill ramp threats before they stabilize. Lorien closes if they cannot answer it.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal (Strands, Bolt) and discard to hit threats. Use Knight to fetch lands for Lorien setup. Apply pressure with Hawks to force trades. Don't overextend into combat if you lack answers.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with discard and efficient removal. Lorien is difficult for control to answer without specific hate. Use Hawks to pressure and force them to use removal. Trade resources favorably to reach your win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Race for Lorien while using discard to hit their Lorien or key creatures. Prismatic Strands is crucial for board control. Manage your graveyard carefully to avoid self-hate. Win by out-resourcing or forcing them to overcommit.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust land count based on matchup speed. Bring in针对性 hate (Strands, discard, artifact/graveyard hate) and remove less relevant cards. Play more aggressively against slow decks and more defensively against fast ones. Prioritize keeping interaction in hand over committing to Lorien too early.
notes: Common mistake: Casting Lorien Revealed without board control or answers, leading to easy removal. Knight of the Reliquary is essential for mana fixing and protecting against graveyard hate; never sacrifice it lightly. Prismatic Strands is versatile but can be dead against combo if not careful. Watch for Rest in Peace/Engineered Plague, which can completely shut down your game plan. Always track opponent's discard targets to anticipate their strategy.

philosophy: A four-color combo deck that uses discard and draw spells to flood the graveyard, then leverages Sneaky Snacker to copy key instants or sorceries for infinite value or a game-winning line. It aims to out-resource midrange and control archetypes while racing aggressive decks with early disruption and late-game inevitability.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 7-card hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Faithless Looting), and Sneaky Snacker or a protection spell. Keep 6-card hands only if they contain a complete turn-1/2 setup (land, discard, Snacker/protection). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple Snackers without support, or only combo pieces with no mana or draw spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or discard spell to dig for pieces and prime the graveyard. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Sneaky Snacker, or use discard to set up the graveyard. Prioritize untapped lands for protection and combo execution. If the opponent is aggressive, consider holding up removal or protection instead of committing Snacker.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in board wipes, targeted removal, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in protection spells, extra draw, and backup wincons. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, artifact removal, and hand disruption. Keep a balanced mana base and protection suite in all matchups.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for protection versus combo execution. Keep Sneaky Snacker safe until the graveyard is primed with discard spells or key targets. Use Faithless Looting to dig for pieces while simultaneously setting up the graveyard. Avoid overcommitting mana to one step of the combo if the board state is unstable.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back Snacker if the board is unstable or if the opponent has unblocked removal. Use discard to disrupt opponent's key turns, especially when they are setting up their own combos or ramping. Prioritize board wipes against token or creature-heavy strategies.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, board wipes, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or combo pieces. Track graveyard contents to anticipate their hate or your own recursion needs. Pay attention to mana bases; tapped-out opponents are vulnerable to discard and Snacker recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Infinite Sneaky Snacker + Faithless Looting loop for card advantage, or copying a specific win condition (like a big spell or combo piece). Execute the loop when you have protection, a primed graveyard, and enough mana to resolve the final piece safely.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, a primed graveyard, and the opponent has no immediate removal or counterspells. If you can resolve the combo piece safely and the board is clear of threats, commit fully. Do not split your resources if a single decisive line wins the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one Snacker with no protection, the graveyard is wiped, and you have no backup plan. Also concede against fast aggro if you can't stabilize by turn 4-5, or against control if you've run out of resources and cannot break through their answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use protection (hexproof/shroud), split the combo into multiple steps, or use discard to force them to hold up mana. If they counter the setup, dig deeper with cantrips. Consider sacrificing Snacker to copy a protection spell from the graveyard if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and Snacker. Don't let them get to their big mana; disrupt their mana sources and race them. Use removal to eliminate key ramp creatures and prioritize survival over combo execution in the early turns.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize survival with removal, protection, and early disruption. Use Snacker to draw into answers. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to combo. Do not overextend into combat; trade efficiently and use discard to disrupt their curve.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage. Use discard to force them to reveal answers. Protect your combo pieces and wait for them to run out of resources. If they lock the board, use removal or protection to clear a path for Snacker recursion.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Trade protection and discard. Prioritize graveyard protection and Snacker recursion. Win by having better setup or out-resolving their combo. Track their graveyard to anticipate their win conditions and disrupt them with targeted hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate. Against control, add more protection and backup wincons. Against aggro, add removal and board wipes. Against combo, add graveyard hate and artifact removal. Always maintain a balanced mana base and keep at least two protection spells in the board to ensure combo consistency.
