notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting Sneaky Snacker too early without a primed graveyard. Another: Ignoring mana base development, which can stall the combo. Unique interaction: Sneaky Snacker can copy itself if you have a way to return it to the graveyard, but typically you use it to copy Faithless Looting for infinite draw. Protect it at all costs, as losing it to discard or removal can stall your game plan. Practice the exact combo sequence in practice games to avoid misplays during critical turns.

philosophy: Accelerate mana to deploy game-winning threats ahead of schedule, leveraging evasion and board impact to close games quickly against slower or control-oriented decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one ramp spell, and a threat (Annoyed Altisaur or Avenging Hunter). Keep hands with Utopia Sprawl if paired with a land or cheap creature. Mulligan hands with 4+ lands, no ramp, or only high-cost threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap ramp spell if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, ramp again or deploy a cheap threat. Prioritize land drops to ensure you can cast your ramp spells on curve, aiming to hit turn 3-4 for your signature threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Grixis Affinity): Bring in early removal, board wipes, or hate bears. Against control (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): Add more threats, discard effects, or direct damage. Against ramp/tribal (Elves): Include artifact/enchantment removal, hate creatures, or faster finishers. Against mirror: Side in additional removal, Utopia Sprawl hate, and value-oriented threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for your big threats; avoid overcommitting to low-impact plays. Track graveyard if relevant, but focus on hand and library resources to ensure you can cast threats when they hit.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough mana to protect them or close the game. Hold back Annoyed Altisaur if the opponent has board wipes or targeted removal. Use Avenging Hunter to stabilize or apply immediate pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for removal spells, board wipes, and counter magic. Note when opponents save removal for your key threats. Track their mana development to anticipate when they can answer your plays.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Annoyed Altisaur and Avenging Hunter, often boosted by Utopia Sprawl. Combine evasion with unblockable effects to deal lethal damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast a threat and have a clear path to close the game, or when the opponent is out of answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are behind on board with no ramp, facing a hard lock, or out of resources to recover. Also concede if you are out-raced by a faster archetype with no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast threats at end of turn, use multiple threats to force counter trade-offs, and rely on unblockable/evasive effects that bypass counters once resolved.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with faster threats, use early interaction to disrupt their mana, and sideboard in hate for their key ramp spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, use Avenging Hunter's ETB to clear the board, and hold back big threats until you can stabilize or close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with unblockable threats, use Utopia Sprawl to bypass blockers, and force them to use removal on multiple threats to overwhelm their resources.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by trading efficiently, sideboard in hate for Utopia Sprawl, and prioritize value plays over aggressive overextensions.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against control, add more pressure and discard. Against aggro, add early answers. Against ramp, add hate and faster threats. Keep the core ramp package intact but swap in matchup-specific answers to maintain consistency.
notes: Annoyed Altisaur cannot be blocked by creatures with power 4 or greater, making it a premier finisher. Utopia Sprawl makes creatures unblockable by creatures with power 2 or less. Combine them for massive damage. Watch out for board wipes and targeted removal. Don't overextend without protection. Remember that Gruul Ramp is a race; prioritize speed and consistency over complex combos.

philosophy: A B/G midrange strategy that leverages Food tokens to fuel life gain, combat tricks, and incremental value. It aims to stabilize early, outvalue opponents through resource generation, and close games with protected combat damage or late-game threats, excelling against grindy and combo decks while struggling against fast aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/fetches if available, otherwise basics that tap for B/G), at least one early game piece (Lembas, Eviscerator's Insight, or a creature), and a way to interact or stabilize. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or 2+ lands with only late-game finishers and no mana fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: T1: Play a land, cast Lembas if you have a creature to sacrifice later or to set up tokens. T2: Play a second land, cast Eviscerator's Insight or a creature, then sacrifice Food tokens for life gain and combat tricks. Sequence lands to ensure B/G mana by turn 2. Prioritize casting Food generators to build a token army and life total.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Mono Red Madness/Grixis Affinity (Aggro): Bring in more removal, life gain, and early interaction. vs Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity (Control): Bring in more value engines, card draw, and late-game threats. vs Jund Wildfire (Combo): Bring in hate bears, discard, or graveyard hate. vs Elves (Ramp): Bring in early pressure, removal, and life gain. vs Mirror: Bring in additional combat tricks and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting threats, generating Food tokens, and holding up interaction. Don't overcommit to token generation if you can't protect them. Use the graveyard strategically if the deck has recursion or sacrifice outlets.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have sufficient life total and interaction to protect them. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy removal. Use Fanatical Offering to swing combat or protect key creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (like Wrath of God), counterspells, and removal. Note when opponents are holding up mana for interaction. Track their life total and board state to know when to push damage vs. stabilize.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large creatures, combat tricks, or a specific win condition (e.g., a creature that gets bigger with Food tokens or life gain). Close the game by attacking with protected threats or using Fanatical Offering to break through blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, sufficient life total, and know the opponent lacks sweepers or removal. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your stabilization, or if you're dead to the board (e.g., facing a sweeper with no answers and no threats). Also concede if your hand is completely irrelevant to the matchup.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats, cast spells at unexpected times, or rely on uncounterable effects if available. Use Food tokens to fuel spells that bypass counters or win combat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on key ramp creatures, and stabilize with life gain. Don't let them get too far ahead.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize life gain, early removal, and blockers. Use Food tokens to stabilize life total and swing combat. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure, use card advantage to outvalue them, and close games quickly before they can stabilize. Use combat tricks to break through blockers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by managing your Food tokens better, using Fanatical Offering more efficiently, and outvaluing in the late game. Prioritize card advantage and protection.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against faster decks, cut late-game finishers for more interaction and early threats. Against slower decks, cut interaction for more value engines and card draw. Always ensure you have a plan to close the game and a way to stabilize.
notes: Common mistake: Overgenerating Food tokens without a way to sacrifice them or protect the board. Another: Holding up too much mana for interaction and falling behind on the board. Unique interaction: Fanatical Offering can be used on your own Food tokens to create a massive combat trick if you have a way to sacrifice them, but typically it's used on your main threats. Also, life gain from Food can be crucial against burn or aggressive decks.

philosophy: Familiars is a spell-slinging combo-control deck that leverages graveyard-fueled free spells and relentless cantrip draw to assemble Lorien Revealed or Snap + Familiar combos. It excels at out-valuing control and midrange decks while racing or stabilizing against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (fetches count as 1.5), at least one cantrip (Opt, Ponder, Brainstorm), and a Familiar or Lorien Revealed. Mulligan any hand with three or more lands, no draw spells, or only one land with no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip to dig for Sunscape Familiar or Lorien Revealed. If holding a fetch, use it to find a dual land that enters untapped to enable early spells. Turn 2: Cast Familiar to start fueling the graveyard, or continue cantripping if you have no threats. Prioritize setting up the engine over committing to the board unless facing aggressive decks that require early interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Mono Red Madness): Bring in 2-3 direct damage spells, 2-3 early interaction (Lightning Axe, Spell Pierce), and 1-2 additional lands. Against Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in 2-3 counterspells, 1-2 graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod), and 1-2 additional cantrips. Against Midrange (Jund Wildfire): Bring in 2-3 targeted removal, 1-2 artifact hate, and 1-2 additional draw spells. Against Elves: Bring in 2-3 artifact/enchantment hate, 1-2 additional counters, and 1-2 additional lands.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand. Cast spells from it only when necessary to enable combos or stabilize. Preserve cantrips to dig through disruption. Never overextend into spot removal; your value comes from card advantage, not board presence.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolving Lorien Revealed or when you can trade up using Snap + Familiar. Hold back if the opponent has unblocked removal or counterspells in hand. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and pressure without overcommitting.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially on your second spell), targeted removal (Lightning Axe, Go for the Throat), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod, Void Rend). Note when opponents tap out for disruption; this is your window to dig for Lorien or Snap.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed is the primary win condition; resolve it and draw into game-ending lands or spells. Snap + Sunscape Familiar can win instantly if you have a free spell in the graveyard. Use cantrips to assemble these pieces while maintaining card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough resources to resolve Lorien Revealed despite potential counters (e.g., you have Snap, multiple cantrips, or the opponent has tapped out). Commit when you can trade a Familiar for a key removal spell and still win via card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has established Rest in Peace/Null Rod and you have no graveyard hate or alternative win conditions. Concede if you are down to a dead hand of lands and removal against a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition, or if you cannot dig through their lock pieces.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Snap to cast free spells instantly, bypassing counterspells. Dig deeper with cantrips to find Lorien Revealed or additional threats. If facing heavy counters, commit multiple spells in a row or use Snap to win before they can react.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Disrupt their mana with early interaction or targeted removal. Use cantrips to dig faster than they can ramp. If they establish a board, use Snap + Familiar to combo off or trade up with card advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (Spell Pierce, Lightning Axe) to stabilize. Use cantrips to dig for removal or Lorien Revealed. Race if you can stabilize and have enough threats to outpace their damage. Do not overcommit to the board; value out their creatures with efficient removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with relentless cantrips and graveyard-fueled spells. Use Snap to bypass counterspells and win instantly. Dig for Lorien Revealed to draw into game-ending resources. Pressure their hand size and force them to spend resources on disruption.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Grind for card advantage with cantrips and Familiar. Track their graveyard to avoid falling for Snap + Familiar combos. Sideboard in additional graveyard hate and counterspells. Win by out-drawing them and resolving Lorien Revealed first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count based on matchup speed (add 1-2 lands vs. combo, remove vs. aggro). Prioritize bringing in graveyard hate against control and combo, while adding interaction and removal against aggro and midrange. In mirrors, focus on card advantage and graveyard disruption. Always keep at least one copy of Snap and Lorien Revealed in your main deck if possible, as they are critical to your game plan.
notes: Familiars is highly vulnerable to graveyard hate; always have a plan B (like Snap + Familiar or direct damage) if Rest in Peace or Null Rod is in play. Lorien Revealed is a combo piece, not a board wipe; use it to draw into wins, not to stabilize. Avoid overusing cantrips early if you have no threats; preserve them for when you need to dig through disruption. Watch for opponent's untapped mana; if they have blue mana open, assume counterspells and play accordingly.

philosophy: Glintblade is a disruptive midrange deck that leverages hand disruption to protect Glint-Eye Nephilim, aiming to stabilize early and close out games by sacrificing Glint Hawk to trigger the Nephilim’s death trigger, often enhanced by pump spells like Lembas.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one disruption spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), and ideally Glint-Eye Nephilim or Glint Hawk. Mulligan on 2+ lands with no spells, or 3+ lands with no disruption/Nephilim. Keep hands with Cast Down if you have a way to find a target or can disrupt the opponent's hand first.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition to remove key threats or combo pieces. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Fatal Push/Sheoldred's Edict if possible, or develop board with Glint Hawk/Nephilim. Sequence lands carefully using fetches to ensure consistent access to W and B while minimizing damage.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction, and additional removal. vs Control/Blue Terror: Bring in Cast Down, additional disruption, and hate cards. vs Mirror: Bring in extra disruption, Leyline, and sideboard cards that target the Nephilim/Hawk synergy.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping hand disruption for critical turns. Use Glint Hawk for card advantage or pumping Nephilim. Don't overcommit to the board if you can't protect the Nephilim. Manage mana to have open mana for protection spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit the Nephilim when you have disruption or removal ready to protect it. Hold back Glint Hawk if you need it for card draw or as a sacrifice target for pump spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline, Surgical Extraction), board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), and targeted removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares). Note if opponent is playing Blue Terror (counterspells, card draw) or Affinity (artifacts, fast board).

endgame_tactics.finishers: The primary finisher is sacrificing Glint Hawk to Glint-Eye Nephilim to trigger its death trigger, creating a 4/4 Angel. Enhance with Lembas or other pumps to make the Nephilim massive before it dies, or use the Angel token to attack through blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection for the Nephilim, enough removal to clear blockers, or when the opponent has no graveyard hate/board wipes. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face multiple pieces of graveyard hate, have no way to protect the Nephilim, or are facing a fast combo/aggro deck with no disruption. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no way to interact.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter, or cast them at end of turn. Use hand disruption to remove counterspells. Consider playing around them with split second or instant-speed interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use early disruption to hit their mana dorks or land drops. Bring in additional removal and hate cards. Stabilize with Glint Hawk for card advantage and pump the Nephilim to race.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early disruption and cheap removal. Use Glint Hawk to trade efficiently. Bring in Rest in Peace to stop graveyard synergies. Stabilize and close out games with the Nephilim.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with early threats and disruption. Use hand disruption to remove key control spells. Win with the Nephilim combo or out-value them with card draw from Glint Hawk.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the disruption war. Prioritize targeting their Glint Hawk and Nephilim. Use Leyline of the Void to shut down their graveyard recursion. Win by out-resourcing and protecting your combo better.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your disruption and hate cards to the metagame. Against control, bring in more hand disruption and Cast Down. Against aggro, bring in Rest in Peace and additional removal. Against mirror, bring in Leyline and extra disruption. Always keep a balance between disruption, removal, and hate cards.
notes: Common mistake: Sacrificing Glint Hawk too early without a target for Lembas or without triggering Nephilim. Always calculate the math for the Nephilim death trigger. Be aware of graveyard hate; if it's in play, pivot to winning with the Nephilim as a standalone threat or using other finishers. Remember that Lembas is a sorcery, so timing matters. Glint Hawk can be sacrificed to give another creature +2/+2 and flying, but cannot be sacrificed to itself.

philosophy: A four-color tempo-midrange deck that leverages Lembas for mana acceleration and Glint Hawk for card advantage to out-value opponents, using Galvanic Blast for flexible removal and finisher potential. It aims to stabilize early, out-resource the midgame, and close efficiently against control, combo, and aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/fixing), at least one Glint Hawk or Lembas, and a Galvanic Blast or early creature. Mulligan on 1-land hands, triple-colored non-fixing lands, or hands with only removal and no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Glint Hawk or Lembas if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, follow up with Glint Hawk (sacrifice for draw) or Lembas (pay upkeep for food), or play a cheap threat. Prioritize fixing lands to enable WURB spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red, Elves): +2 Disdainful Stroke, +2 Lightning Strike, -2 Lembas, -1 Glint Hawk. vs Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +2 Galvanic Blast, +2 Thoughtseize, -2 Glint Hawk, -1 Disdainful Stroke. vs Midrange (Jund Wildfire): +2 Path to Exile, +1 Galvanic Blast, -2 Glint Hawk, -1 Disdainful Stroke.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Glint Hawk to filter and maintain hand size; avoid over-sacrificing when ahead. Leverage Lembas food tokens for splash colors or late-game spells. Keep Galvanic Blast for key threats or finishers, not just chump blockers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or card advantage to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or countermagic. Use Glint Hawk's flying to trade favorably or apply pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal (Path, Lightning), and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out for big spells; that is your window to attack or cast Galvanic Blast.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Galvanic Blast for direct damage or clearing the final blocker, Glint Hawk for card advantage to find the win condition, and Lembas-generated mana for big spells. Combine flying threats with removal to close.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the board is clear or you control key threats, and the opponent has no sweepers or countermagic in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 2+ life with no removal, the opponent has a board wipe plus big threat, or you are facing a combo that has already assembled its win condition. Also concede if your hand is dead to their control shell.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Glint Hawk to dig for counterspell protection or cheap threats. Cast Galvanic Blast in response to their counters if needed. Do not overcommit to one spell; spread threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Glint Hawk and cheap spells. Use Galvanic Blast to remove their ramp creatures or finish them before they hit their big mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Glint Hawk for card draw, Lembas for mana, and Galvanic Blast to remove key threats. Do not overextend; trade efficiently and close quickly once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with flying threats and card advantage. Force them to use removal on Glint Hawk, then pivot to Galvanic Blast for direct damage or clearing blockers. Out-value them with hand disruption and efficient spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Glint Hawk to draw into more answers. Use Galvanic Blast to remove their Glint Hawks or key threats. Control the board with efficient trades and sideboard in additional disruption.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the specific matchup. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and direct damage. Against slow decks, bring in card advantage and late-game finishers. Always ensure your mana base can support the new colors, and adjust your curve to match the expected pace.
notes: Glint Hawk sacrifice is a key resource; use it to dig for answers or finishers, but avoid sacrificing it when you are already ahead. Lembas requires careful mana management; do not pay the upkeep cost if you are low on life or need the mana for interaction. Galvanic Blast can target two creatures for 2 damage each, making it excellent for trading with multiple small threats. Watch out for sacrifice outlets in the metagame that can punish Glint Hawk.

philosophy: Turbo Fog aims to stall the game with combat prevention and card draw, out-grinding opponents until you can establish a +1/+1 counter engine via Lorien Revealed. It excels against aggressive and midrange decks that rely on combat damage, but struggles against fast combo or heavy board sweepers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (preferably one producing U or G), at least one fog effect (Tangle/Fog), and a draw spell or Moment's Peace. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no early spells, or only high-cost spells without a functional mana base.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast Tangle or a draw spell if mana allows. Turn 2: Ramp with Growth Spiral or play a second land, then cast Moment's Peace to stall if pressured. Sequence lands to ensure U/G availability by turn 2 to cast key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Affinity): Bring in extra fog effects, life gain, and spot removal. Against control (Blue Terror, Grixis): Add card advantage, counterspells, and hate bears. Against ramp (Elves, Jund): Include graveyard hate and additional stalling. Against mirror: Bring in more Lorien Revealed, extra counters, and targeted removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard fog effects for combat phases and use card draw to maintain hand size. Avoid overcommitting to Lorien Revealed until you have a board of creatures to counter, and track graveyard resources to avoid dead draws.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have fog effects ready to protect them or when you can safely grow your board. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or removal in play, and use Moment's Peace to reset combat when necessary.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, fast combo turns, and removal targeting Lorien Revealed or key fog effects. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or run out of resources, as these are your windows to push the counter engine.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed with a board of creatures, or a late-game creature boosted by counters. Moment's Peace can buy time to set up the final turn, and fog effects ensure your creatures survive combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ fog effects in hand/graveyard, Lorien Revealed on the board, and enough creatures to make combat irrelevant. Push the counter engine when the opponent is tapped out or out of removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can win before turn 5, or if you're out of fog effects and card draw against a sweeper-heavy control deck. Also concede if your hand is dead against a known win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap fog effects, split your spells across turns, and win the game before they can counter key pieces. Moment's Peace can stall while they burn resources on counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize graveyard hate and additional stalling. Don't trade resources; out-value them with card draw and fog effects, and avoid giving them easy targets.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Flood the board with fog effects, draw cards, and stabilize. Use Moment's Peace to buy turns, then transition to Lorien Revealed once you're no longer at lethal.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and fog effects. Use fog to survive their sweepers, and push Lorien Revealed when they're tapped out or out of answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card draw and fog effects. Establish Lorien Revealed first if possible, and use targeted removal to disrupt their counter engine.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Increase fog density and life gain against combo/aggro, add card advantage and hate against control, and keep core fog effects and Lorien Revealed. Adjust land count if mana base issues arise.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Lorien Revealed too early without a board. Remember that fog effects don't stop non-combat damage, so track opponent's hand size to know when they can cast sweepers. Use Moment's Peace strategically to stall, not just as a draw spell.

philosophy: Black Sacrifice aims to out-value opponents by repeatedly sacrificing low-cost creatures for card advantage, life gain, or direct damage. It excels against slower control and combo decks that overextend, while struggling against fast aggro that can race or disrupt the graveyard.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a sacrifice outlet (Carrion Feeder, Mortician Beetle), and a cantrip/ritual (Village Rites, Dark Ritual, Thoughtseize). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only high-cost spells, or no way to generate initial value.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Dark Ritual or Thoughtseize to disrupt. Turn 2: Play a sacrifice outlet, then sacrifice a 1/1 (like Gravecrawler or token) to draw cards/gain life. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast outlets on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace/Leyline), +1 removal (Fatal Push), -2 value creatures. vs Control/Combo: +1 more removal, +1 graveyard recursion, -2 aggro hate. vs Mirror: +1 more value engines, +1 discard, -2 lands if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for threats vs. outlets. Don't overcommit to sacrifice if you need creatures to block or attack. Keep graveyard stocked but be mindful of hate. Use Village Rites/Dark Ritual to maintain hand size.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear advantage in card advantage or life total. Hold back outlets if the opponent has sweepers or hate. Use sacrifice to answer threats (e.g., Carrion Feeder to draw into removal).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline, Tormod's Crypt), sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), and combo wincons. Note when opponents tap out for big spells vs. holding up interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Graveyard recursion (Sheoldred, Liliana), relentless value engines (Mortician Beetle + Carrion Feeder), or direct damage from sacrifice outlets. Win by out-graving or out-valuing.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have a clear card advantage lead, opponent is low on interaction, or you can close the game before they can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a fast aggro with no stabilization tools, if the graveyard is permanently exiled, or if you're down to a dead hand with no outlets.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple threats (Village Rites, Dark Ritual) to force counter windows. Bounce or discard to disrupt their mana. Don't overcommit to one big spell.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use discard to hit their lands, and prioritize removal over value generation. Out-grave them if they go wide.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with life gain from Mortician Beetle/Village Rites, use removal to clear key threats, and transition to value once stabilized. Don't over-sacrifice early.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with value engines, use discard to hit their answers, and win through attrition. Force them to use resources on your small threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Prioritize discard, protect your outlets, and be willing to trade creatures to maintain graveyard presence.

sideboard_advice: After sideboarding, shift from pure value generation to matchup-specific disruption. Against hate-heavy decks, prioritize exile protection or alternative win conditions. Against fast decks, lean into early interaction and life gain. Always track opponent's graveyard strategy and adjust your hate cards accordingly.
notes: Common mistake: Over-sacrificing too early and running out of resources. Unique interaction: Carrion Feeder's draw triggers on sacrifice, not death, so it works with tokens and sacrifice outlets. Always keep a land in hand if you're playing a format with fetch/shock lands to avoid flooding.

philosophy: Dimir Terror is a control-oriented deck that wins by disrupting the opponent's early game, stabilizing with *Lorien Revealed*, and out-grinding them through card advantage and efficient removal via *Snuff Out*. It excels against midrange and control archetypes by trading resources efficiently and punishing misplays with *Counterspell*.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands (including duals/shocks) and at least one turn-1 or turn-2 play. Prioritize hands with *Counterspell* or *Snuff Out* paired with a land. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands with no interaction against aggro/combo. Keep hands with *Lorien Revealed* only if you have a land and a way to protect it or a threat to support it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. If you have *Counterspell* and can afford to hold priority, cast it; otherwise, hold it for protection. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast *Snuff Out* if an early threat appears, or play a land. Sequence lands to ensure blue and black mana availability for *Snuff Out* (UB) and *Counterspell* (UU). Never overextend on turn 2 without protection.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Go for the Throat, +1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Lorien Revealed, -1 Counterspell. vs Control: +2 Thoughtseize, +1 Inquisition of Kozilek, -1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Go for the Throat. vs Combo: +2 Rest in Peace, +1 Engineered Explosives, -1 Lorien Revealed, -1 Snuff Out. vs Mirror: +2 Duress, +1 Tormod's Crypt, -1 Lightning Bolt, -1 Go for the Throat.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for *Counterspell* until the opponent reveals their spell. Use *Snuff Out* on high-impact threats to maintain card parity. Avoid overcommitting to *Lorien Revealed* without protection; it's a liability if removed. Manage your graveyard by avoiding unnecessary exile effects unless facing graveyard-dependent decks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing multiple threats until you have established a *Counterspell* or *Snuff Out* in hand. Use *Lorien Revealed* to stabilize against midrange or control, but be ready to sacrifice it for card draw if you're running low. Prioritize board presence over raw card advantage if the opponent is closing the game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard spells, and removal. Note if the opponent is holding up mana for multiple turns (likely a counter or combo piece). Track graveyard hate if they play it early. Pay attention to mana development to predict their next play and adjust your interaction timing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: *Lorien Revealed* combined with a board of creatures, or a well-timed *Snuff Out* on a game-ending threat. If combo is present, *Counterspell* on the win condition. Late-game card advantage from *Snuff Out* and *Lorien Revealed* usually closes out slow decks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established card advantage, the opponent has no interaction, or you're facing a slow deck that can't recover from board loss. Commit when you can protect your threats with *Counterspell* or *Snuff Out*, or when you're ahead in life and can trade efficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single land, have no interaction, and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Concede against fast combo if you lack the specific hate, or against control if you've run out of resources and they have a lock. Don't bluff through established board states.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use *Snuff Out* on their counterspell if it's a creature, or play around it by casting threats at end step. Use *Lorien Revealed* to apply pressure that forces them to use multiple counters. Consider *Thoughtseize* to remove key counters before they can be cast.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize *Snuff Out* on mana dorks or acceleration spells. Use *Counterspell* on their big finishers. *Lorien Revealed* can stabilize against their early threats. Don't let them establish a mana advantage; trade efficiently and punish overextensions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep *Counterspell* for their best early threats. Use *Snuff Out* on any creature that reaches 2+ power. *Lorien Revealed* is key for stabilization; sacrifice it for draw if needed. Don't overextend; focus on survival and out-grinding them in the late game.
threat_responses.against_control: Trade resources efficiently. Use *Snuff Out* on their removal. Apply pressure with *Lorien Revealed* to force mistakes. Use *Counterspell* to protect your key spells. Out-value them in the late game by leveraging *Snuff Out*'s card advantage engine.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use *Snuff Out* on their *Lorien Revealed* or key threats. *Counterspell* is king; hold it for their win conditions. *Thoughtseize* to disrupt their hand. Prioritize graveyard hate if they rely on it. Play conservatively and punish overextensions.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction ratio based on the metagame. Against control-heavy fields, bring in more discard and counters. Against aggro, prioritize cheap removal and life gain. Always keep *Snuff Out* and *Counterspell* as your core interaction. Adjust land counts if needed, but typically keep the mana base consistent to maintain flexibility.
notes: *Lorien Revealed* is both a threat and a card draw engine; don't treat it as a static. *Snuff Out* is your primary removal and card advantage tool; use it wisely. *Counterspell* is your most important card; never cast it unless you're sure it will hit or you have a backup plan. Watch for graveyard hate, as it can neutralize your value engines. Common mistake: overcommitting to *Lorien Revealed* without protection, leading to a dead hand.

philosophy: Ruby Storm is an explosive combo deck that aims to cast 10+ spells in a single turn to reduce the mana cost of a high-damage win condition to zero or less. It wins by outpacing midrange and control archetypes while racing aggressive strategies through superior card advantage and mana acceleration.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 7 with 2-3 lands, 1+ ritual/fixer (Desperate Ritual, Manamorphose), and 1+ cantrip. Keep 6 with 2 lands, 2+ rituals/fixers, and 1+ cantrip. Keep 5 with 3+ rituals/fixers and 1+ cantrip. Always mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 0 rituals/fixers, or no wincon/draw engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast cantrip or early threat, play land. Turn 2: Cast ritual or fixer, draw cards, sequence lands for U/R/G access. Prioritize fixing into Manamorphose/Rituals over untapped lands if possible. Avoid overextending into interaction; focus on assembling the engine and drawing into the wincon.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity: +2/1 Spell Pierce/Dispel, +1/1 Red Elemental Blast, -2/1 cantrips. Against Mono Red Madness and Jund Wildfire: +2/1 Surgical Extraction, +1/1 Engineered Plague, -2/1 cantrips. Against Elves: +2/1 Nature's Claim, +1/1 Pyroclasm, -2/1 graveyard hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource to be spent, not saved. Prioritize card advantage over board presence. The graveyard is a secondary resource for recursion but a liability against hate; manage it by discarding low-value cards or using it to fuel rituals. Keep hand size low enough to avoid discard but high enough to maintain a wincon.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Storm rarely trades creatures. Avoid combat unless it buys a turn or protects your combo. Commit to the combo turn only when you have a wincon, 1+ mana sources, and a clear path to 10+ spells. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered interaction or a lethal threat on the board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponents holding mana on their end step or main phase without playing threats—often signals counterspells or discard. Track graveyard hate, board wipes, and hard counters. Note when opponents tap out for interaction, as this creates windows to go off safely.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A high-damage spell (e.g., Grapeshot, Lightning Axe, Tendrils of Agony) cast for zero or negative mana after casting 10+ spells. Execute by dumping hand into rituals/fixers, then casting the wincon. Ensure you have enough spells to reach the threshold before committing.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a wincon in hand, 1+ mana sources, and a clear path to 10+ spells without the opponent having a guaranteed counter or lethal threat. If the opponent is at lethal, commit immediately regardless of setup.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you have a dead hand (no wincon, no mana, no draw engine), the opponent has a hard counter you cannot work around, or you are facing a combo that will win before you can assemble yours. Also concede if you are at 1 life and the opponent has a board wipe plus a threat.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig for alternative wincons or disruption. Cast the wincon at end step or during the opponent's turn if possible. Use cheap spells to force counters, then cast the wincon on the following turn. If they have multiple counters, pivot to discard or an uncounterable wincon.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. Do not let them stabilize. Use discard to hit their mana sources or key threats. If they get ahead, go all-in on turn 3-4 with whatever you have. Prioritize speed over perfect setup.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early discard to hit threats or mana. If you have cheap removal or burn, use it to stabilize. If you cannot stabilize, race them with your combo turn. Do not overcommit to board control; Storm wins by going off, not trading.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind their hand with cantrips. Dig for the wincon and disruption. Use cheap spells to force interaction. If they have a hard counter, pivot to an alternative wincon or discard their hand to create a window. Patience is key; let them use their interaction on cantrips.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a spell-out war. Prioritize hand disruption (Thoughtseize, Duress) to hit their wincons or rituals. Wincons are often identical, so it comes down to who goes first and who has better disruption. Hold back a ritual if the opponent has a counter, then go off on the following turn.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo gameplan to a hybrid control/combo strategy. Bring in more interaction, hate cards, and alternative wincons to handle specific matchups. Adjust your land count if you are bringing in more non-basic lands or hate cards. Prioritize cards that disrupt the opponent's gameplan while protecting your own combo pieces, and be willing to play a more reactive game if the meta demands it.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to a combo turn without checking for counters, miscounting spells, holding too much mana, and ignoring graveyard hate. Manamorphose fixes into any color but taps for 1 damage; use it wisely to avoid self-damage or missing a critical land drop. Remember that Storm wins by spell count, so every card drawn matters. Always count your spells carefully before casting the wincon, and never assume you have enough mana without verifying the exact threshold.

philosophy: Mono-Blue Faeries is a tempo-control archetype that uses cheap interaction, evasion, and card advantage to disrupt the opponent’s game plan while setting up a resilient late-game finisher. It wins by out-grinding control, out-disrupting combo and ramp, and stabilizing against aggro with flash threats and counterspells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands, at least one turn-1 or turn-2 play (Spellstutter Sprite, Delver of Secrets, or card draw), and at least one interaction spell (Snap, Counterspell, or Spell Pierce). Mulligan if you have no lands, all spells costing 3+ mana, or zero interaction against aggro/combo decks.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Spellstutter Sprite or Delver of Secrets. Turn 2: Cast Snap or Counterspell, potentially flashing in Spellstutter Sprite to counter a key spell or protect itself. Sequence lands to guarantee blue mana on turns 1 and 2. If holding a hand with multiple cheap spells, prioritize establishing a board presence while saving interaction for the opponent’s first meaningful play.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness): +1 Spell Queller, +1 Daze/Force of Will (if legal), -1 Chalice of the Void, -1 card draw. vs Control (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): +1 Spell Queller, +1 Negate/Counterspell, -1 aggressive threats. vs Ramp/Combo (Elves): +1 Chalice of the Void, +1 Spell Pierce, -1 extra threats. vs Mirror: +1 Spell Queller, +1 Negate, -1 Chalice.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoards counterspells for key threats, uses flash creatures to protect combo pieces or counter removal, and manages the graveyard carefully (Snapcaster Mage needs it, but avoid giving it to opponents). Don’t overcommit mana if the opponent has interaction; preserve card advantage for late-game value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if they have sweepers or heavy removal. Use flash to respond to their plays and maintain board control without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), heavy removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Bolt), combo pieces (Elves’ Genesis, Affinity’s Arcbound Ravager), and control finishers (Teferi, Liliana). Note when they tap out for big spells and track their graveyard for Snapcaster Mage targets.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Delver of Secrets transforming into Inverter of Truth (with Chalice of the Void), Spellstutter Sprite + Snap/Counterspell + Spell Queller/Inverter, or grinding with card advantage and flash threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent is tapped out, or you’ve established board control with counterspells. Commit if you can protect your finisher or if the opponent has no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to one life, have no interaction, the opponent has a sweeper + finisher, or you’re in a dead matchup with no comeback (e.g., no lands, no counters, facing a locked-in combo).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells (Pierce, Sprite) to probe, cast multiple spells in a row to overwhelm counters, use card draw to out-value, and sideboard in cheaper interaction or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Deploy early interaction (Sprite, Pierce), Chalice of the Void on 1 or 2, counterspells for key ramp spells, and stabilize with flash threats. Disrupt their mana acceleration before they can cast their game-ending spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early counterspells, flash threats to trade efficiently, Daze/Force of Will if available, and sideboard in more removal/counter. Don’t overextend; stabilize by turn 3–4 and close out the game with evasion.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with evasion, use card advantage to out-grind, protect your combo pieces, and sideboard in more interaction to break their control shell. Force them to spend resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war. Hold counters for their key spells, use Spell Queller to protect your threats, win the card advantage race, and sideboard in more Negate/Queller. Play conservatively early and capitalize on their missteps.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan based on the metagame: bring in more interaction against control/combo, more early threats/removal against aggro, and Chalice/Pierce against ramp. In mirrors, prioritize Queller and Negate. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and adjust your mulligan strategy accordingly.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting without protection, mismanaging Snapcaster Mage’s graveyard, and forgetting that Spellstutter Sprite’s counter reduces as you cast more spells. Unique interactions: Sprite + Snap/Counterspell can counter multiple spells in a chain. Delver combo requires precise sequencing. Always track opponent’s mana and graveyard to maximize your interaction.

philosophy: Mono Red Burn is a pure damage deck that aims to deal 20 damage to the opponent as quickly as possible using efficient direct damage spells and a few efficient threats. It wins by racing faster than combo and aggressive decks, and by out-pressuring control decks before they can stabilize or draw into answers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 1-2 lands and 3+ spells. Prioritize hands containing Lightning Bolt, Galvanic Blast, or Experimental Synthesizer. Keep hands that can play a spell on turn 1. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, multiple copies of the same spell without lands, or hands with no turn-1 play and no way to develop mana.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play Experimental Synthesizer. Turn 2: Play a land, then cast Lightning Bolt or Galvanic Blast into face or the opponent's creature. If they play a creature, use Blast/Bolt to remove it and swing. Always sequence damage efficiently: prioritize removing blockers, then swing, then finish with direct damage. If you have a hand with multiple Bolts/Blasts and a land, consider playing a land on turn 1 to set up a turn-2 lethal or removal package.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): +4 Searing Blaze, +2 Flusterstorm, +2 Red Haze. Against Aggro (Elves, Grixis Affinity): +2 Pyroclasm, +2 Kessig Flamebreather, +2 Searing Blaze. Against Mirror: +2 Flusterstorm, +2 Searing Blaze. Remove redundant removal or direct damage based on the specific matchup, but always keep your core Bolt/Blast count high.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource for damage, not for developing a board. Count your damage constantly: hand + board + graveyard. Never overextend into sweepers. Prioritize keeping lethal in hand over board presence if the board is vulnerable to board wipes. Use Experimental Synthesizer as both a threat and a 2-damage finisher.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can safely trade or when you have enough direct damage to clear blockers. Don't swing with Synthesizer into a board full of removal unless you have answers or are going for lethal. Hold back Bolts and Blast if you need them for a specific threat or to close the game. Pressure early to force mistakes and resource expenditure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Pyroclasm, Flame Rift, Terror of the Peaks), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Go, Abrade), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for their win conditions. Track their graveyard if they play combo decks. Pay attention to mana availability to know when they can cast removal or counterspells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Direct damage spells are your primary finishers. Experimental Synthesizer can be sacrificed for 2 damage to close out games or trade efficiently. Use your last Lightning Bolt or Galvanic Blast to deal the final points of damage. Always calculate exact lethal before committing to an attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have lethal in hand or can guarantee it through trades. If the opponent has no sweepers and you're ahead on damage, commit threats. If you're facing a combo deck that hasn't stabilized, pressure relentlessly to race their win condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Concede if you're down to 1-2 damage and have no spells, or if you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win condition and you have no interaction. Also concede if you're drawing dead lands or if your hand is completely useless against a locked board or established control strategy.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Don't overcommit to one spell. Use cheap threats like Experimental Synthesizer to pressure and force responses. If they counter a Bolt, you still have Blast and Synthesizer. Sometimes you need to wait for a free spell or use a cheap threat to force a counterspell before casting your win condition. Flusterstorm can shut down their key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Burn is naturally favorable against ramp. Use early damage to race their mana development. Don't let them stabilize; pressure relentlessly. Use removal efficiently to clear their early threats, and don't overextend into their late-game sweepers or finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal efficiently. Don't burn face if you can remove a threat. Use Pyroclasm if you have it to clear multiple creatures. Trade trades and prioritize surviving the early turns. Once you stabilize, shift to dealing damage and closing the game before they can rebuild.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early and don't let them draw cards. Use Experimental Synthesizer to force interaction. If they stabilize, you need to have lethal ready. Sometimes you need to be patient and wait for a free spell. Track their graveyard and hand to know when they can cast removal or counterspells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race. Track damage carefully and count your lethal. Use Flusterstorm to protect your key spells or remove theirs. Prioritize removing their threats and managing your own. The player who calculates damage math better and manages their hand efficiently wins the mirror.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to face specific strategies. Bring in more removal and sweepers against aggro, and more direct damage and hate cards against control/combo. Keep your core Bolt/Blast count high to maintain your game plan. Don't over-index on hate cards; you still need to deal 20 damage to win. Always keep a minimum of 10-12 lands depending on your spell count.
notes: Common mistakes include swinging Experimental Synthesizer into a board you can't clear, forgetting to count damage (hand + board + graveyard), and overcommitting to the board against sweepers. Galvanic Blast's flexibility to target either a creature or the player is crucial for efficient damage and removal. Always track opponent's mana and graveyard to time your spells correctly.

philosophy: Cycle Storm aims to assemble a self-sustaining engine of fast mana and card draw to cast a massive number of spells in a single turn, culminating in a high-impact finisher that wins immediately or creates an insurmountable board state. It wins against slower, reactive decks by outpacing their answers and against combo mirrors by going off first or leveraging superior disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (prioritizing duals or fetchable sources), at least one fast mana spell (Cabal/Dark Ritual), and at least one card draw/selection spell or direct combo piece. Keep hands that can reliably go off on turn 1 or 2. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, zero fast mana, or only threats without acceleration. In a mulligan, prioritize keeping a single land with two spells if it includes a ritual and a cantrip.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a fast mana spell if possible, or cast a cantrip to dig for the combo. Turn 2: Accelerate mana with rituals, draw into more spells, and begin assembling the combo chain. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast rituals on the same turn you draw them. If you have a turn 1 or 2 combo, execute it immediately rather than holding up for interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror/Control: Bring in discard (e.g., Thoughtseize/Dismember), counterspells, and protection for your combo pieces. Against Mono Red Madness/Aggro: Bring in targeted removal, life gain, and early interaction to stabilize. Against Elves/Ramp: Bring in artifact hate, graveyard hate, and additional disruption to slow their acceleration. Against Grixis Affinity: Bring in artifact destruction, counters, and discard to hit key artifacts. Against mirror: Bring in additional disruption, discard, and protection to win the hand-advantage war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource to be spent on acceleration and card draw, not threats. Prioritize keeping cards in hand to fuel the combo and maintain redundancy. Manage your graveyard carefully if hate exists, but generally use it as a resource for recursion or as a secondary win condition. Never spend mana on non-essential spells once you have enough to go off.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on playing non-combo threats unless they're part of the win condition. Only interact if you're on the draw and need to stabilize, but prioritize going off over trading. If you have a turn 1 or 2 combo, never hold up mana for interaction unless you're certain you can't go off. The board state is irrelevant until you resolve your finisher.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard spells, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents play lands that suggest ramp or control. Track if they're holding up mana for protection or disruption. In mirrors, track their discard and disruption to avoid going off into a locked hand. Pay attention to when they sacrifice lands or play utility spells that indicate a combo or control game plan.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Songs of the Damned combined with a large number of spells cast that turn, generating tokens and life to close the game. Alternatively, use format-legal spell-synergy finishers like Empty the Warrens, Lightning Storm, or Goblin Bombardment. The goal is to cast 10+ spells to generate enough mana/tokens to win before the opponent can respond.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a reliable turn 1 or 2 combo, or when you've drawn into a self-sustaining chain that guarantees a win. Commit when you have enough mana to cast the finisher and still have priority. If you're on the draw and face a reactive deck, go off as soon as you have a guaranteed win condition rather than waiting for a perfect hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're on the play and miss your turn 1 or 2 combo with no recovery, if you're on the draw and face a hard lock (counterspell + discard) with no way to break it, or if the opponent has established an insurmountable board state (e.g., massive ramp/affinity) that you can't race. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap spells to force counters, then go off with a protected finisher. Utilize discard to remove counterspells, or use protection/enchantment removal. If possible, go off in response to their counterspell or use a spell that can't be countered. Sideboard in additional disruption and protection to break their lock.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply pressure with early disruption. If they get ahead, use discard to hit their key ramp pieces. Consider bringing in artifact hate or graveyard hate. Race them if possible, but prioritize disruption. If they stabilize, go off immediately to win before they can deploy their win condition.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early discard to hit their threats. Hold up interaction if you're on the draw and need to stabilize, but prioritize going off. If you can't stabilize, go off immediately to win before they kill you. Sideboard in life gain and targeted removal to survive the early game.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and redundancy. Go off before they can stabilize. Use discard to remove their answers. If they have a hard lock, consider sideboarding in additional disruption or protection. Play around their main deck answers by varying your timing and using multiple threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping hands with discard and protection. Go off on turn 1 if possible to catch them off-guard. Use disruption to prevent their combo from resolving. Track their hand to avoid going off into a locked state. Sideboard in additional disruption and protection to out-resource them in extended games.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo to a hybrid control/combo game plan. Bring in disruption, protection, and hate cards tailored to the metagame. Adjust your mulligan strategy to be more conservative, as you'll have more interaction. Prioritize keeping hands that can both disrupt and go off. In longer games, play more aggressively with your interaction and less passively with your combo pieces.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to board presence, holding up mana for interaction when you can go off, miscounting spells needed for the finisher, and failing to track opponent's discard/counters. Unique interactions: Songs of the Damned scales with spells cast, so include cheap cantrips and rituals to maximize its impact. Fast mana is critical; ensure your mana base supports it without flooding. In unknown formats, verify legality of key pieces and adjust sideboard accordingly. Always count your spells carefully before casting the finisher to avoid overpaying or underperforming.

philosophy: Boros Bully is a fast, aggressive midrange deck that leverages discard to dig for cheap flying threats and spot removal, aiming to overwhelm opponents through early board presence and direct damage before they can stabilize. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on setting up, while struggling against highly resilient aggro or artifact/enchantment-heavy strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands (mix of Plains/Mountains) and at least one of Faithless Looting, Kor Skyfisher, or Thraben Charm. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, three+ non-basic lands, or hands lacking early threats/interaction. Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 land and a turn-2 threat or discard spell.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast Faithless Looting to dig for a Kor Skyfisher or Thraben Charm, or play a cheap two-drop if available. If you draw Skyfisher on turn 2, cast it immediately for the flying attacker and 2 damage to stabilize or finish. Sequence lands to ensure double-colored mana is available for Skyfisher and Charm by turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: In 4x Thraben Charm (for exile mode), 2x additional discard, 2x burn spells. Out 2x Faithless Looting, 1x Skyfisher. vs Control/Combo: In 4x Thraben Charm, 2x discard, 2x direct damage. Out 2x Skyfisher, 1x discard. vs Mirror: In 4x Thraben Charm, 2x discard, 2x targeted removal. Out 2x burn, 1x land.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Faithless Looting is your primary card advantage engine; use it when your hand is empty or you need a specific answer/threat. Don't overcast it if you're at 3+ cards and have a clear path to win. Manage mana to keep Thraben Charm available for key turns. The graveyard is a resource for discard effects; avoid self-mill unless synergistic.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Skyfisher early to apply pressure and trade into blockers. Hold back Charm for high-value targets (flying threats, key artifacts/enchantments, or 2/2s). Don't overcommit to board if opponent has sweepers or hard removal; use discard to disrupt their hand instead.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up interaction or cast threats in response to non-removal spells), sweepers (hold up Charm or discard to disrupt setup), and graveyard strategies (discard to empty their library/graveyard). Note when opponents tap out for big spells; that's your window to attack.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kor Skyfisher with flying and haste is your primary win condition, supported by direct damage and discard to clear the path. Thraben Charm can exile key blockers or sweep minor threats to enable a lethal attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal (3+ damage away), opponent has no sweepers or hard removal in hand, or you've discarded their key answers. Also commit when you can trade Skyfisher into a blocker and still have lethal next turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no threats, opponent has established a board of unblockable flyers or a massive sweeper, or you're facing a combo deck that has already won and you lack the specific interaction to stop it. Also concede if you're consistently drawing dead hands post-sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast threats in response to non-removal spells or when opponent is tapped out. Use Thraben Charm's exile mode on their counterspell if it's a creature (unlikely) or focus on applying pressure they can't interact with.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum early pressure to race them. Use discard to hit their ramp spells or key threats. Thraben Charm can exile their early ramp sources if they're creatures. Don't trade up unnecessarily; win before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Thraben Charm's exile mode on key early threats. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Play Skyfisher to block flying threats or trade into bodies. Prioritize survival over aggression until you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly with early threats. Use discard to hit their answers or combo pieces. Thraben Charm can remove their key blockers or sweepers. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying damage and disruption.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping hands with multiple threats and interaction. Use Thraben Charm to exile their Skyfisher or key blockers. Trade efficiently and recognize when to concede a trade to gain card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw aggression to targeted interaction. Increase discard and spot removal against control/combo, while adding more early threats and burn against aggro. Always carry at least two copies of Thraben Charm for versatility. Adjust land counts if facing heavy artifact/enchantment or if mana base is consistently broken.
notes: Common mistake: overusing Faithless Looting when you already have a full hand, leaving you vulnerable to discard or sweepers. Always sequence plays to ensure you can cast Skyfisher and Charm on the same turn if needed. Thraben Charm's exile mode is strictly better than destruction against artifact/enchantment decks. Watch for 'Bully' mirror matches; card advantage and efficient trades win these.

philosophy: A hyper-aggressive, resource-generating combo-aggro deck that aims to assemble a single, unblockable, trampling threat with massive power/toughness to win in 2-3 turns. It excels against slow, reactive decks and combo strategies that lack early interaction or board wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand containing a land plus Bogles. Keep land + Bogles + Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth or Gilt-Leaf/Elvish Archdruid. Keep land + land + Bogles + Sprawl/Growth. Mulligan anything without a Bogles or without a land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Bogles. Turn 2: Play land, cast Bogles, cast Utopia Sprawl (or Gilt-Leaf/Elvish Archdruid). If you have a druid, cast it, then cast Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth on Bogles. Attack with Bogles. Turn 3: Cast Wild Growth/Utopia Sprawl on Bogles, attack for lethal. Use Malevolent Rumble from the graveyard for card advantage if needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Blue Terror): Bring in Lightning Strike/Abrade, Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction. vs Control/Combo (Jund Wildfire, Grixis Affinity): Bring in Swords to Plowshares, Path to Exile, Malevolent Rumble, Utopia Sprawl, Wild Growth. vs Mirror: Bring in Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Strike, Leyline of the Void.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always prioritize untapping lands to fuel the next turn's attack. Keep cards in hand for protection or finishing moves. Use Malevolent Rumble strategically to dig for Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth or answers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit your Bogles early and often. Never hold back a massive attack unless you have a clear answer to a specific threat (like a board wipe or targeted removal). If you have a board wipe in hand, hold it until they commit their board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation, etc.), targeted removal (Path, Lightning Strike), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline). Note when opponents tap out for interaction vs. when they hold up mana.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A single Bogles with Utopia Sprawl(s) and Wild Growth(s) for 10+ damage. Malevolent Rumble can close the game if you have enough mana and a creature to attack with.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast your next Sprawl/Growth and attack for lethal, or when the opponent has no interaction left. Never hold back a lethal attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction) with no way to break it, or if you're down to a dead hand against a fast deck with no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Malevolent Rumble from the graveyard to bypass counters. Cast Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth in response to their counterspells if you have priority. Use cheap threats to force interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them by going wide/committing early. Use Lightning Strike/Abrade to remove their ramp sources. Don't let them stabilize; pressure relentlessly.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Lightning Strike/Abrade to remove key threats. Use Swords to Plowshares/Path to Exile to bounce or remove. Rely on your early damage to race them.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure immediately. Use Malevolent Rumble to dig past their removal. Attack with everything you have. Force them to use multiple spells to stop one threat.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better card advantage (Malevolent Rumble) and better protection (Utopia Sprawl). Use Swords to Plowshares to remove their Bogles. Hold back a Sprawl/Growth to protect your threat.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo-aggro to a more resilient, interactive aggro/control hybrid. Bring in hate cards against specific archetypes, but keep enough Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth/Malevolent Rumble to maintain your win condition. Prioritize graveyard hate against combo, and removal/board wipes against aggro/control.
notes: Common mistakes include holding back a lethal attack, miscounting damage with Utopia Sprawl/Wild Growth, casting Malevolent Rumble too early without a target, and forgetting that Slippery Bogle can only be blocked by one creature. Unique interactions include Utopia Sprawl's trample working with Wild Growth's power boost, and Malevolent Rumble being castable from the graveyard if you have enough mana.

philosophy: This deck stabilizes early with resilient walls and accelerates mana using land search and ramp, then pivots to a combo finisher that capitalizes on its board presence or mana advantage. It excels against aggressive and midrange decks that rely on creature combat, while struggling against fast combo or heavy disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for fixers), Quirion Ranger, Winding Way, or early wall/mana dorks. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no ramp/fixing, or only expensive spells without mana acceleration. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a Ranger/Winding Way by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land. Turn 2: Cast Quirion Ranger or Winding Way to search a land and ramp. If the opponent plays a creature, drop a wall to block. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for walls and ramp. Use Ranger’s tap ability for flexible mana if needed for protection or combo pieces.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Blue Terror): Bring in additional walls, spot removal, and life gain. Against control/combo (Grixis Affinity, Jund Wildfire): Bring in card draw, disruption, and combo protection. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and hand disruption. Against Elves: Bring in sweepers and early pressure.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana acceleration to cast combo pieces. Use Quirion Ranger’s tap ability for flexible mana. Don’t overcommit walls if you can win via combo. Manage graveyard if relevant, and conserve cards in hand until you have a clear path to the win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit walls only when necessary to survive until combo pieces are assembled. Hold back high-value combo pieces until you have protection or a guaranteed win. Use walls to trade efficiently and preserve your combo pieces for the finish.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and combo win conditions. Note if opponents are playing land destruction or discard. Track graveyard hate if your combo relies on it, and monitor opponent’s mana development to predict their next play.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The combo win condition, typically assembled through mana advantage and board stability. Get there by ramping with Ranger/Winding Way and stabilizing with walls. Once the combo is in hand or on the stack, execute it immediately if possible.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast your combo, have removed or blocked key threats, and can protect your win condition. Commit when the opponent has no clear answer to your finisher or when you can trade your board for their life total.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to one life, have no lands/ramp, face a guaranteed sweep combo, or are in a dead matchup with no comeback tools. Concede if you’ve exhausted your resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split your combo into multiple steps, or use protection/disruption. Play around counters by having backup plans or casting at end step. If possible, use instant-speed interaction to clear the way for your combo.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with walls, use removal on key ramp creatures, and race if your combo is faster. Disrupt their mana base if possible, and prioritize surviving until you can execute your own combo.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with walls and cheap removal. Don’t overextend; focus on surviving until turn 4-5 to execute combo. Use life gain if available, and trade walls efficiently to preserve your combo pieces.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use card advantage to out-value them, and protect your combo from sweepers. Play around their removal by having multiple threats or using protection. Force them to make difficult choices between your combo and board presence.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize land drops, disrupt their ramp, and use targeted removal on their key combo pieces. Control the board with walls and force them to overcommit. Use hand disruption to steal key turns.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, increase interaction against control/combo, add more walls/removal against aggro, and tailor to specific metagame threats. Keep core ramp and combo pieces. Adjust land counts if needed, and prioritize cards that protect your win condition or disrupt theirs.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting walls too early, mismanaging mana for combo, ignoring graveyard hate, and failing to track opponent’s win conditions. Unique interactions: Quirion Ranger’s tap ability can be used for instant-speed mana for protection or combo pieces. Winding Way’s extra land can enable combo turns. Always play around sweepers and keep a backup plan if your combo is disrupted.

philosophy: Izzet Terror is a reactive control deck that neutralizes early threats with counterspells and removal, then overwhelms opponents using Lorien Revealed’s card advantage and token generation, closing out games with high-impact finishers like Skred. It excels against combo and aggressive decks that lack interaction, while struggling in long resource wars against established control or midrange strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early interaction (Counterspell, Spell Pierce, Lightning Strike, or Abrade), and Lorien Revealed if you have 3+ mana sources. Mulligan on 1-land hands, hands with only removal and no cantrips/interaction, or hands with multiple Lorien Revealed but no early spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Opt/Ponder to dig for interaction or Lorien. Turn 2: Play a second land, hold up 2-3 mana for counterspells against key threats. If opponent plays a vulnerable early creature, remove it or counter it. If you have 3 mana, cast Lorien Revealed to start generating tokens and drawing cards.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Mono Red Madness, Elves, Grixis Affinity): Bring in artifact hate, discard, and additional early interaction. vs Control/Midrange (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): Bring in additional counterspells, graveyard hate, and finishers like Skred. vs Mirror: Sideboard in additional card advantage and targeted removal, keep interaction heavy.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve counterspells for game-ending threats or combo pieces; use removal on tempo threats. Don’t overcommit to Lorien if you have no way to protect it or follow up. Track graveyard for reanimation threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Lorien tokens when you have board presence or can protect them with counterspells. Hold back on Skred until the opponent has no removal or can’t block effectively. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and find answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo win conditions, note when opponents tap out for big threats (your counterspell window), and track graveyard activity for reanimation or discard strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Skred (often pumped via Lightning Strike or similar effects) or Lorien Revealed token army. Secure victory by clearing the board of blockers, then attacking with Skred or tokens.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no removal, counterspells, or board wipes in hand, and you have a card advantage advantage. Commit to Skred attacks when you can safely pump it or they have no blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a fast combo you can’t interact with, if you’re out of resources in a control mirror, or if you’re dead to a specific hate card. Also concede if you’re at 1-2 life with no interaction and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap cantrips to dig through counterspells, cast Lorien Revealed on turns when you can protect it, and use Skred as a threat that’s hard to counter. Cast spells in response to their counterspells to force trades.
