threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with removal and counterspells to disrupt their mana development. Bring in artifact hate and discard to slow them down. Don’t let them stabilize; finish them before they hit their late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction to stabilize. Use Lorien Revealed to generate blockers and card advantage. Track life total and don’t overextend into board wipes.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Lorien Revealed and card draw. Use Skred as a finisher they can’t easily answer. Force them to use resources on your threats while you dig for answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards and holding up counterspells for key plays. Prioritize Lorien Revealed and card draw over aggressive threats. Use Skred when they have no counterspells left. Track their hand size and graveyard to predict their game plan.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction curve based on the metagame. Against combo/aggro, prioritize early hate and discard to disrupt their game plan. Against control/midrange, increase your counterspell count and add finishers to close out games. Always keep at least one copy of Lorien Revealed and Skred if they’re still in your main deck, and adjust your land count if you’re bringing in multiple dual lands or shock lands.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Lorien Revealed without follow-up, holding up too many counterspells and missing early interaction windows, and underestimating Skred’s vulnerability to board wipes. Always track the opponent’s graveyard and hand size, as Izzet Terror relies heavily on information and timing. In mirrors, card advantage and counterspell efficiency are paramount—don’t trade spells unnecessarily.

philosophy: A five-color Tron variant that leverages bounce spells like Ephemerate to replay Urza lands for exponential mana generation, aiming to combo off or overwhelm opponents with unanswerable threats. It wins by out-accelerating traditional Tron and leveraging card draw to assemble its engine before opponents can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with two or more lands (preferably including at least one Urza land or Expedition Map), or a combination of Mulldrifter + land + Ephemerate. Drop hands with three or more non-basic lands, no Tron pieces, or only single lands without fixing. Prioritize hands that can assemble Tron by turn 3 or draw into the bounce engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Expedition Map to fetch a basic or Urza land. Turn 2: Play a land, cast another land or early spell. Turn 3: Aim to assemble Tron. If possible, cast Mulldrifter to dig for Tron pieces or Ephemerate. Sequence: Fetch -> Play -> Cast -> Dig. Use low-cost spells to protect your lands or dig deeper if behind.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Grixis Affinity): +1-2 artifact hate, +1-2 board wipes, +1-2 early interaction. -1-2 Mulldrifter. vs Control (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): +1-2 combo finishers, +1-2 protection spells, +1-2 additional bounce spells. -1-2 early draw spells. vs Ramp (Elves): +1-2 land destruction, +1-2 fast interaction. -1-2 card draw. vs Mirror: +1-2 additional Ephemerate/bounce, +1-2 protection. -1-2 lands.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; avoid overextending into removal. Use Mulldrifter to dig for Tron/Ephemerate when behind. Manage your graveyard carefully if the opponent has recursion or hate. Prioritize assembling Tron over casting non-essential spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have Tron assembled and a bounce spell ready to protect or follow up. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted hate. Use Ephemerate to protect key lands or bounce opponent's threats if possible. Never commit your entire hand without a plan to protect your engine.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, artifact hate, board wipes, and graveyard recursion. Note if the opponent is playing fast aggro (keep pressure) or control (prepare to combo off). Track their removal count and key answers to avoid walking into hard counters.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Infinite mana loop via Ephemerate + Tron lands, then cast a game-winning spell (e.g., a massive creature, a combo finisher, or attack with infinite mana artifacts). Alternatively, use the mana to cast multiple threats or win via a specific win-con. The loop itself is the finisher; the mana pays for the victory condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have Tron assembled, a bounce spell in hand, and enough mana to either combo off or stabilize against an aggressive board. Also commit when the opponent has no removal for your key pieces or when you can safely dig for the second Tron piece.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you are down to 1-2 lands with no Tron pieces, no bounce spells, and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Also if you are facing a hard counter to your win condition with no sideboard answers or if your hand is completely dead against their strategy.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats, split your combo across turns, use protection spells, or combo off with uncounterable spells if possible. Dig for answers and avoid casting your entire combo in one vulnerable turn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-accelerate with Tron, use land destruction, or combo off before they stabilize. Use early interaction to slow them down and prevent them from reaching their late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early interaction, use Mulldrifter to dig for Tron/Ephemerate, stabilize with board wipes, then combo off or out-value. Prioritize survival over speed until you can assemble your engine.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, dig for Tron/Ephemerate, combo off when they have no counters, or use protection to protect your engine. Force them to use resources on your early game to set up your late game.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Mulldrifter to out-draw, protect your Tron pieces, and combo off first. Bounce their Tron lands if possible and track their removal to avoid walking into hard counters.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Generally, bring in interaction against aggro, protection/combo pieces against control, and land destruction against ramp. Keep the core Tron + Ephemerate engine intact. Prioritize cards that protect your lands or accelerate your combo. Always sideboard in answers to the top 3 archetypes in your local meta.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Tron without a bounce spell, ignoring opponent's hate, and mismanaging mana during the loop. Unique interactions: Ephemerate can bounce your own Tron lands to replay them, but be careful of 'summoning sickness' if using creatures. Mulldrifter synergy with bounce allows digging for the second Tron piece. Always track opponent's removal count and be ready to pivot if your primary win condition is answered.

philosophy: Boros Synthesizer is a midrange-control hybrid that stabilizes early with efficient removal and life-gaining enchantments, then out-values opponents through incremental damage and flying threats. It excels against control and combo decks by weathering early pressure and closing games with resilient, synergistic threats, while struggling against fast aggro and heavy ramp if unanswered.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early interaction (Galvanic Blast, Glint Hawk, or a removal spell), and a Lembas or early creature. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no early plays, or only late-game finishers. Prioritize hands that can play a land, then a Glint Hawk or removal on turn 1, followed by Lembas on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Glint Hawk or a cheap removal spell. If opening with land + removal, hold for potential aggro threats. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Lembas or another threat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Lembas on turn 2 or 3 to start generating food tokens for life gain and pump. If facing aggro, prioritize removal over Lembas early.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Blue Terror: Bring in counterspells or discard, plus artifact hate if needed. vs Mono Red Madness: Bring in burn spells, discard, or board wipes. vs Jund Wildfire: Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal. vs Elves: Bring in artifact destruction, additional removal, maybe life gain. vs Grixis Affinity: Bring in artifact hate, additional removal, maybe enchantment destruction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for interactive spells until you establish board presence. Use Lembas food tokens strategically: pump Glint Hawk or other flyers to close games, or sacrifice for life to stabilize against aggro. Avoid overcommitting to one threat; maintain card advantage through incremental value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready to answer opponent's responses. Hold back if you lack interaction against control or combo. Prioritize trading efficiently—use Glint Hawk's tap ability to chump block or finish players, but don't overextend into sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or lock pieces. Track food token generation and life totals to know when you can safely commit to an attack.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Glint Hawk (pumped via Lembas), direct damage spells, and resilient creatures. Close games by attacking with multiple flyers or using Galvanic Blast to remove a key blocker/planeswalker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal ready, and the opponent is at low life or lacks answers. Commit when Lembas is generating consistent food tokens and you can safely attack through potential removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no removal, a fast aggro deck that outpaces your stabilization, or if you're dead to the board with no comeback tools. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has established an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split second effects if available, or sequence spells to beat counters. Attack with multiple threats to force counter decisions. Use Glint Hawk's tap ability to deal damage without casting spells. Rely on instant-speed interaction to clear the way.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to punish their mana development. Use efficient removal to delete key ramp creatures. Leverage life gain to survive their early attacks while you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and life gain over threats early. Use Lembas to stabilize, then transition to flying threats once stabilized. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and close quickly once ahead.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats to force suboptimal counters. Use direct damage to bypass blockers. Leverage card advantage and incremental value to outlast their resources.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades are paramount. Use Glint Hawk to trade efficiently. Prioritize removing their Lembas or key threats. Control the board with removal and close games with flying threats. Card advantage wins mirrors.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific matchup. Against control, bring in more pressure and counterspells/discard. Against aggro, prioritize removal and life gain. Against combo, bring in hate cards and early interaction. Always keep a balance of removal, life gain, and threats. Adjust your curve to be more aggressive or more resilient based on the metagame.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Lembas too early without protection. Another: Hoarding mana instead of using Glint Hawk's tap ability for incremental damage. Unique interaction: Lembas food tokens can be used to pump Glint Hawk into lethal or trade efficiently. Watch out for artifact/enchantment hate in the meta. Always track opponent's life total and board state to know when to swing.

philosophy: Mono-White Aggro seeks to close games quickly by deploying efficient, resilient threats and leveraging lifelink and combat tricks to outpace opponents before they can stabilize. It dominates slower, resource-heavy archetypes and struggles against extremely fast or disruption-heavy decks that can outpace its clock.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any 7-card hand with 2-3 lands and at least two one-drops. Keep 6-card hands with 2 lands and two one-drops, or 3 lands and one one-drop. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, three+ lands, or only two-drops/three-drops. Prioritize hands with Novice Inspector or Hinterland Sanctifier for early pressure and card advantage.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a one-drop if available. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a two-drop or attack with the one-drop. Sequence lands first to maximize combat trick potential, then cast spells. Attack with all threats unless facing known sweepers. Use Novice Inspector to dig for answers or additional pressure, and Hinterland Sanctifier to stabilize against early aggression.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2/+2 combat tricks, +2 lifelink creatures, -2 draw spells. vs Control/Combo: +2 spot removal, +2 lifegain, -2 aggressive threats. vs Mirror: +2 combat tricks, +2 lifegain, -2 card draw.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a combat resource; always leave open mana for tricks and blockers. Prioritize threats over card draw unless you are behind or facing a control deck. Graveyard management is minimal; avoid self-mill and respect opponent graveyard synergies by carrying graveyard hate if needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit fully when you have a clear damage advantage or the opponent has no removal. Hold back a blocker or trick if the opponent has untapped mana or potential spot removal. Never overcommit to the board if you suspect a board wipe or sweepers are in the deck.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for untapped mana, hand size, and graveyard activity. Key tells: opponent tapping out on their turn often means no removal; opponent playing lands without spells suggests ramp or control; opponent sacrificing creatures indicates sacrifice synergies or pump spells. Track Kor Skyfisher's potential evasion needs.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Unblocked damage from flying or evasion threats, combat tricks on key blockers, and lifelink to out-grind. Get there by maintaining board presence, trading efficiently, and using Novice Inspector to dig for closing threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ threats on board, the opponent has no removal, or you can close in 2-3 turns. Also commit when you can trade up favorably or when the opponent is at low life with no blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one life, have no threats, and the opponent has a clear win condition with no way to interact. Also concede if you are facing a hard lock, infinite combo, or a sweepers-heavy control deck with no sideboard hate.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter. Sequence around known counters by playing around specific spots. Use combat tricks to bypass blockers if countered spells leave you vulnerable, and prioritize threats that don't rely on spells to win.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply maximum pressure early to beat their mana acceleration. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers, and use lifelink to stabilize while they dig for lands.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize blockers and lifelink creatures. Use combat tricks to trade efficiently. Don't chase card advantage; stabilize and out-grind. Use Hinterland Sanctifier to gain life and trade up against aggressive threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure. Don't let them stabilize with cheap removal; use tricks to bypass it. Force them to use answers early, and use Novice Inspector to dig past their removal. Attack with all threats to maximize damage before they can draw into answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and combat tricks. Trade efficiently and use lifelink to out-value. Sideboard tricks heavily, and track opponent hand size to know when to hold back or go all-in.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a hybrid control-aggro game plan. Bring in interaction to handle specific threats, but keep enough threats to maintain pressure. Adjust land counts if needed, and prioritize hands with interaction and early threats. Focus on out-grinding longer games while preserving your clock against faster decks.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers or holding back too much and losing to slow pressure. Unique interaction: lifelink synergizes with combat tricks to stabilize against aggressive decks, while Novice Inspector provides crucial card advantage in longer games. Always track opponent's untapped mana and hand size, and avoid playing all lands on turn 1 if you can cast a one-drop.

philosophy: Dredge is a graveyard-combo deck that wins by rapidly filling its own graveyard with cards via dredge effects, then using recursion engines to generate massive card advantage and lethal damage. It aims to out-value opponents through graveyard synergy while disrupting their plans with hand disruption and hate cards.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or shocklands, or basic Swamps/Forests), at least one dredge enabler (Dredge, Stinkweed Imp, or similar), and a recursion engine or disruption spell. Mulligan any hand with 0-1 lands, all non-basic lands with no fetches, or hands with only disruption and no graveyard synergy.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a dredge enabler or disruption spell. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another dredge enabler or recursion piece. If you have multiple dredge cards, chain them to fill the graveyard quickly. Prioritize establishing a graveyard engine over board presence.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in hate cards like Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Tormod's Crypt, and discard spells. Against control/combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Keep disruption, bring in additional recursion and card draw. Against mirror: Bring in extra hate cards and discard to disrupt their graveyard setup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana should be hoarded for recursion spells and responding to threats. Cards in hand should be used for disruption early, then saved for protection. The graveyard is your primary resource; avoid unnecessary discards and prioritize filling it with high-value targets (Griselbrand, Nexus of Fate, Bitter Ordeal).
midgame_priorities.board_control: Dredge has minimal board presence early. Commit threats only when you can protect them or when you're close to winning. Use disruption to delay opponent's game plan while you dig for your combo.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Tormod's Crypt, Surgical Extraction). Track opponent's hand for counterspells or removal. Note when they start playing removal or hate, as it signals they've identified your game plan.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Griselbrand (drain life), Nexus of Fate (infinite turns/draws), Bitter Ordeal (infinite combat/draws), Dread Return (reanimate threats). Win by chaining recursion to generate lethal damage or infinite turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a recursion engine in play, enough mana to cast it, and the opponent has no immediate graveyard hate or removal. If you can resolve a win condition and have backup recursion, commit fully.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard hate card (Rest in Peace/Leyline of the Void) with no way to remove it, have no recursion engine in play, and the opponent is ahead on board or has a fast win condition. Also concede if you're on the draw with a dead hand and no comeback potential.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast dredge enablers and recursion spells at instant speed when possible. Use cheap, resilient threats that don't rely on a single resolution.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with dredge engines. Use discard to remove ramp spells. If they outpace you, rely on graveyard recursion to out-value their mana advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize discard and early disruption to slow them down. Bring in graveyard hate and removal. Stabilize by using recursion to generate card advantage and life gain (Griselbrand) to outlast their burst.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with graveyard recursion. Use discard to remove key control spells. If they try to counter your combo, use cheap enablers and recursion to overwhelm their resources.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the graveyard war. Prioritize discarding their dredge enablers and recursion pieces. Use Tormod's Crypt or Surgical Extraction to remove their key cards. Control the graveyard with hate cards and win by out-recursing them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against hate-heavy formats, bring in additional recursion and protection. Against fast formats, prioritize disruption and early game pressure. Always keep a balance between graveyard hate, disruption, and recursion. Sideboard in cards that can remove hate (like Thoughtseize/Duress) and backup win conditions.
notes: Dredge is highly dependent on graveyard synergy; avoid playing cards that don't contribute to it. Be mindful of turn-based actions and priority when chaining dredge effects. Watch for opponent's sideboard cards that target the graveyard. Practice sequencing to maximize recursion efficiency. Common mistake: overcommitting to a single win condition without backup recursion.

philosophy: Mono-red self-damage engine that wins by repeatedly casting cheap spells to trigger Kiln Fiend's enter-the-battlefield ability, dealing escalating direct damage to opponents. It aims to outpace interactive decks and overwhelm slower strategies before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Kiln Fiend, and at least one cheap spell (Rite of Flame, Manamorphose, Lightning Strike, etc.). Prioritize hands that can cast Kiln Fiend on turn 2 or 3. Mulligan to hands with only lands, only spells with no early plays, or hands missing both Kiln Fiend and a way to cast it by turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap spell (Rite of Flame/Manamorphose) if possible, or hold for turn 2. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Kiln Fiend, then immediately cast another cheap spell to trigger its ETB for lethal or board damage. If opening with Rite of Flame, consider playing it on turn 1 if you have a land, then casting Kiln Fiend on turn 2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Elves, Affinity): Bring in Lightning Axe, Dragon's Rage Channeler, or additional removal/spot removal. vs Control/Blue Terror: Bring in additional cheap spells, discard (Thoughtseize/Inquisition if Pioneer, or just more Rite/Manamorphose), and maybe some hatebear. vs Mirror: Bring in additional Kiln Fiends, discard, and hatebears. vs Jund Wildfire: Bring in more direct damage, discard, and potentially graveyard hate if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is critical; always keep 1R open for Kiln Fiend ETB triggers and instant-speed spells. Manage hand size by using draw spells (Rite/Manamorphose) but avoid flooding if you have no Kiln Fiends. Graveyard is generally irrelevant unless using specific sideboard cards; focus on hand and library.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Kiln Fiends aggressively when you have a way to trigger them immediately. Hold back if the opponent has a board wipe or sweepers in play. Don't overcommit to the board if you can win via direct damage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, etc.), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for sweepers or hold up interaction. Track their life total to calculate trigger damage needed.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Multiple Kiln Fiends on the board with cheap spells to trigger them, or a single Kiln Fiend with a high spell count. Rite of Flame/Manamorphose can finish games directly.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 2+ Kiln Fiends, enough mana to cast them and trigger them, and the opponent has no sweepers or sufficient life to survive the triggers. Also commit when you can close the game in one turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you have no Kiln Fiends, no cheap spells, and the opponent has a wide board or a sweepers ready. Also concede if you're at 1-2 life and the opponent has a guaranteed removal/sweeper combo that you can't answer.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells that are hard to counter (if format allows), or sequence plays to counter the counter. Use discard to remove counterspells. If possible, cast Kiln Fiend in response to a counter, or use instant-speed spells to trigger it.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them. They will try to outpace you with big threats; you must win before they stabilize. Use discard to hit their mana sources.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal, then pivot to your game plan. You can't out-race Elves/Affinity early, so focus on surviving turn 1-3, then close the game with Kiln Fiend triggers.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Don't let them stabilize. Use discard to remove key answers. Win before they can draw into sweepers or counters.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize killing their Kiln Fiends before they can trigger. Use your own triggers to close the game. Track spell counts carefully.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw damage to interaction and disruption. Add discard to hit their hand, spot removal for their threats, and additional Kiln Fiends for the mirror. Against control, add more cheap spells and card advantage. Against aggro, add early interaction and life gain if available. Always tailor to the specific metagame.
notes: Common mistake: Overcasting spells without Kiln Fiend on board to trigger them, wasting resources. Another: Forgetting that Kiln Fiend's trigger goes on the stack and can be responded to. Always calculate exact damage needed. Manamorphose's life loss is manageable but can be fatal against aggressive decks. Rite of Flame's extra land is crucial for consistency.

philosophy: A fast combo deck aiming to assemble a high-storm Weather the Storm to deal 20+ damage in a single turn, leveraging Manamorphose for card draw and mana acceleration. It wins by outpacing midrange and control while avoiding early aggro through discard and mana acceleration.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Manamorphose, and at least one cantrip or discard spell. Keep any hand with Manamorphose plus two lands and a draw spell. Mulligan to hands that can cast Manamorphose on turn 1 or 2 and have a clear path to Weather the Storm. Avoid pure land hands or hands with only late-game threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Manamorphose if possible, or discard the opponent's key piece with hand disruption. Turn 2: Cast cantrips and draw spells to dig for Weather the Storm and Thirsting Roots. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for disruption and combo pieces. Prioritize finding the combo over developing a board.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in discard spells, mana acceleration, and fast mana. Against Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in more cantrips, protection artifacts, and additional storm enablers. Against Midrange (Jund Wildfire): Bring in hand disruption and faster combo pieces. Against Mirror: Bring in counterspells and additional protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a resource to chain spells, not to develop a board. Keep 1-2 lands in play until the storm count is sufficient. Prioritize keeping combo pieces in hand and manage hand size by cycling through cantrips to find the win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Do not commit creatures early unless necessary to stabilize against aggro. Hold back until you have a clear path to lethal. If the opponent develops a board, use discard to remove key threats before casting storm spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially in Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity), removal (Jund Wildfire), and early pressure (Mono Red Madness, Elves). Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for protection. Track graveyard hate if they play it.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Weather the Storm with a storm count of 10+ for lethal. Thirsting Roots can be pumped or used to trigger additional effects. Manamorphose can be used to draw into the final pieces.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal (e.g., 8+ storm count, opponent at 10 or less life, no immediate counterspells). Commit when you can chain spells without leaving yourself vulnerable to removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you have no way to assemble the combo, the opponent has established an unbreakable board, or you are facing a hard counter to your entire game plan (e.g., heavy graveyard hate plus counterspells with no answer).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig around counterspells. Cast Manamorphose and storm spells in response to counterspells if possible. Use protection spells or discard to remove counterspellers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and fast mana. Do not let them establish a board. Use Manamorphose to accelerate your own game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize discard and early disruption. Use Thirsting Roots or other creatures to block if necessary. Cast Weather the Storm early if possible to close the game before they overwhelm you.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with cantrips and card advantage. Use Manamorphose to draw into answers. Pressure them with early threats if they do not have counterspells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use discard to remove their combo pieces. Hold up counterspells for their Weather the Storm. Prioritize finding Weather the Storm first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against control-heavy formats, increase protection and counterspells. Against aggro, prioritize discard and fast mana. In mirrors, sideboard in more disruption and protection. Always keep at least one copy of Manamorphose and Weather the Storm in the main deck.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to board development instead of combo. Unique interaction: Manamorphose can be used to draw into Weather the Storm and Thirsting Roots in the same turn if sequenced correctly. Be mindful of mana base requirements for a four-color deck; ensure proper shock lands or fetch lands if the format allows.

philosophy: Mono-White Heroic aims to rapidly grow a single creature into an unblockable, lethal threat by casting cheap enchantments that trigger the Heroic ability. It wins by outpacing aggressive decks with explosive turns and outlasting control or combo decks by leveraging enchantment resilience and incremental value.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one cheap creature (e.g., Knight of the White Orchid) paired with a cheap enchantment (Ethereal Armor, Emerge Unscathed, or Hyena Umbra). Keep 3+ land hands only if they contain two complete creature-enchantment pairs. Mulligan hands with 4+ lands, pure land hands, or expensive spells without early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Plains, cast a cheap creature. Turn 2: Play a Plains, cast a cheap enchantment targeting your creature to trigger Heroic for +1/+1, lifelink, or flying. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast the enchantment the same turn or immediately after. Prioritize playing creatures that can attack or set up for a pump turn.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in additional protection enchantments and early threats to stabilize and race. Against control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in more cheap enchantments and card draw to out-value them. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate or targeted removal. Against mirror: Bring in additional pump enchantments and targeted removal to win the value race.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Reserve mana for casting enchantments on your creatures; avoid overcommitting to attacks without protection. Cast one enchantment per creature per turn to maximize Heroic triggers. Use your hand efficiently and leverage land-fetching creatures for mana acceleration.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have at least one enchantment providing protection (e.g., Ethereal Armor for hexproof, Hyena Umbra for flying). Hold back if the opponent has board wipes or targeted removal and you lack answers. Prioritize attacking with your largest, most protected creature.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on your pumped creatures, and counterspells. Note if the opponent is holding up mana for removal or committing to a combo/aggro plan. Track their enchantment count in mirrors to gauge your value race.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully pumped creature with flying, lifelink, and protection. Achieve this by chaining multiple enchantments on a single creature, then attacking. Use creatures that generate card advantage or mana to fuel the final push.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a lethal threat (typically 5+ power with flying/lifelink) and the opponent has no clear answer. Also commit when you're ahead in life and can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock, have no way to break through their board, or are down to a dead hand with no lands. Also concede if you're behind in a mirror and they have a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast enchantments at end step to avoid counters, or use cheap creatures to bait them. If possible, cast them in response to their spells or use instant-speed options.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use protection enchantments to survive their early threats. If they get ahead, use your enchantments to stabilize and out-value them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use your early creatures to trade efficiently. Cast protection enchantments to survive their burst. If you stabilize, transition to your own aggressive plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure them with early threats. Cast enchantments to grow your creatures and force them to use removal. If they sweep, use your enchantments to rebuild quickly.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the value race by casting more enchantments per turn. Track their creature count and prioritize attacking with your largest threat. Use targeted removal to eliminate their key creatures.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, add more cheap enchantments and card draw. Against aggro, include additional protection or early threats. In mirrors, prioritize targeted removal and additional pump enchantments. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't disrupt your mana base or core game plan.
notes: Heroic triggers only when the enchantment targets only your creature; avoid multi-target enchantments. Be mindful of summoning sickness when attacking. Enchantments survive board wipes, allowing quick rebuilds. Watch for flicker effects that can reset your creature's counters. In unknown formats, verify card legality and adjust sideboard accordingly.

philosophy: Dimir Control neutralizes early threats and midrange pressure using efficient removal and counterspells, then transitions into a late-game advantage by leveraging card draw and graveyard synergy to deploy game-winning threats. It excels against combo and aggressive strategies that lack resilient board presence, while struggling against highly resilient midrange or artifact-based decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands and at least one interaction spell (Snuff Out or Tolarian Terror). Prioritize hands with 1-2 lands if they include Snuff Out, Tolarian Terror, and a cantrip or draw spell. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no spells, or hands with 0-1 lands and no early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Snuff Out or Tolarian Terror if the opponent plays a 3+ mana creature or threat. If no threat appears, play a draw spell or cheap cantrip to dig for answers. Maintain a 2-land start when possible; if forced to mulligan to a 1-land, keep only if it includes Snuff Out and a cantrip.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity, bring in additional cheap removal and artifact hate. Against Jund Wildfire and Elves, bring in graveyard hate, additional counterspells, and board wipes. Against Mono Red Madness, bring in discard, artifact destruction, and counterspells. Remove excess draw spells and Lorien Revealed when facing aggressive decks.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 4-5. Use cantrips to cycle through dead cards and find answers without losing tempo. Don't overcommit to Lorien Revealed unless you have board control or protection. Manage your graveyard carefully if facing graveyard hate; consider milling or sacrificing creatures to disrupt opponent plans.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing Lorien Revealed until you've answered the opponent's primary threat or have a way to protect it. Use Snuff Out and Tolarian Terror to clear the board of key threats and enter-the-battlefield effects. Prioritize removing creatures that can attack through or disrupt your game plan.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana bases that indicate ramp (Elves, Jund) or combo (Madness, Affinity). Note when opponents hold up mana for protection spells or counterspells. Track graveyard contents if they play graveyard synergies. Pay attention to when they start casting their win conditions to time your interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed is the primary win condition, often paired with a resilient creature from hand or graveyard. Snuff Out can flashback to clear a final blocker. Tolarian Terror can counter a game-ending spell or remove a key threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have answered the opponent's primary threat, have card advantage, and can protect Lorien Revealed or follow up with a creature. Commit when you're ahead in resources and the opponent has no clear answer or protection.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards, the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., massive ramp or combo lock), and you have no removal or counterspells left. Also concede if you're facing a highly unfavorable matchup with no sideboard cards that help.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flashback on Snuff Out to bypass counterspells. Chain cantrips to find alternative answers. Cast Lorien Revealed when you know they've used their counterspells. Consider casting threats in response to their spells to force unfavorable trades.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early removal and counterspells to stop key ramp pieces. Use Tolarian Terror to counter mana accelerants. Don't let them get ahead in mana; apply pressure with cantrips and removal to disrupt their curve.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep Snuff Out and Tolarian Terror for early threats. Use cantrips to dig for answers without losing tempo. Don't overextend with Lorien Revealed early. Stabilize by turn 4-5, then transition to offense.
threat_responses.against_control: Trade resources efficiently. Use Lorien Revealed to generate card advantage and board presence. Pressure their mana to prevent them from casting their win conditions. Use flashback Snuff Out to clear their threats when they overcommit.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and efficient removal. Use Tolarian Terror to counter their Lorien Revealed or key threats. Flashback Snuff Out to clear their board. Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, reduce draw spells and increase cheap interaction. Against slower decks, add more Lorien Revealed and late-game threats. Always ensure you have answers to the top 3 archetypes. Keep your sideboard flexible to address specific threats like artifact hate, graveyard hate, or counterspell hate.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Lorien Revealed without board control. Always have a plan B if your win condition is countered or removed. Snuff Out's flashback is crucial for late-game removal, so don't waste it early. Tolarian Terror's versatility makes it a key piece; use it wisely against combo and control. Watch for format-specific interactions with graveyard strategies and adjust sequencing accordingly.

philosophy: Jund Gardens leverages graveyard-fueled cheap spells via Fanatical Offering and mana-accelerating lands to apply early pressure while disrupting the opponent's plan. It wins by out-resourcing slower decks, racing aggressive strategies with resilient threats, and outmaneuvering control through value generation and hand disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 mana sources (including at least one Garden or equivalent mana rock) and a turn-1 or turn-2 play. Prioritize hands with Fanatical Offering paired with a land, or a cheap threat/removal combo. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early spells, 0-1 lands with no fixing, or hands with only high-cost spells and no disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if holding a land drop to strip key interaction. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a cheap threat (e.g., Bloodtithe Harvester, Tarmogoyf) or use Fanatical Offering to cast a spell from the graveyard. Sequence lands to ensure double-green drops; use Defile or spot removal only if it enables a lethal threat or removes a critical early blocker.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Thoughtseize, -2 Graze, -1 Fanatical Offering. vs Mono Red Madness: +1 Graze, +1 Defile, -2 Thoughtseize, -1 Fanatical Offering. vs Elves: +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Graze, -2 Thoughtseize, -1 Defile. vs Jund Wildfire: +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Graze, -1 Defile, -1 Fanatical Offering.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve the graveyard for high-value Fanatical Offering targets; avoid over-committing removal to low-impact threats. Use Lembas for life gain or mana when pressured, but don't sacrifice it if you need the mana for a game-winning play. Track hand size to avoid discarding key graveyard synergies or disruption spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no interaction or is tapped out; hold back removal for key threats or combo pieces. Use Fanatical Offering to answer problematic permanents or cast value spells that trade up. Don't overextend into board wipes or sweepers unless you have a clear path to lethal or can stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze), counterspells, and board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for lock pieces or big threats. Track their graveyard size and hand composition to anticipate when they'll have interaction available.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Tarmogoyf, Bloodtithe Harvester, or a resolved Fanatical Offering targeting a high-value spell/creature. Leverage graveyard recursion to maintain board presence and pressure. Use Defile or spot removal to clear the path for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction, when you can safely cast a game-winning threat via Fanatical Offering, or when you have a clear path to lethal. Commit aggressively against combo decks if you can disrupt their win condition or race them with resilient threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing a hard lock with no draw power, if your graveyard is exiled and you lack alternative win conditions, or if you're down to one card against a coordinated combo/aggro deck. Concede when your game plan is completely neutralized and you have no comeback vector.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple threats to force counterspells and trade up. Cast spells via Fanatical Offering to bypass counters if possible, though counters still work. Use discard to remove key pieces and pressure through interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to interrupt their development. Use spot removal on key mana sources and bring in graveyard hate. Don't over-invest in the graveyard if they can exile it; pivot to direct pressure.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and early threats. Use Lembas for life and don't over-commit to the graveyard if you need to race. Prioritize keeping your hand size and applying pressure to close the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use discard to disrupt their plan. Leverage Fanatical Offering to cast value spells they can't interact with. Force them to use resources on multiple threats rather than a single big spell.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war and prioritize keeping hand size. Use Graze/Thoughtseize to strip their key pieces and win via superior graveyard resources and efficient removal. Track their graveyard to avoid over-committing to Fanatical Offering targets.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift focus based on the metagame. Against control, prioritize graveyard hate and hand disruption to neutralize their lock. Against aggro/ramp, lean into spot removal and life gain to stabilize. In mirrors, prioritize card advantage and graveyard protection. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't compromise your early game consistency or mana base.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Fanatical Offering on low-value targets early, leaving you without answers when the game slows down. Remember that Garden's mana ability is a triggered ability, so it can be responded to; sequence carefully. Lembas is crucial for survival against aggressive decks, but don't sacrifice it if you need the mana for a game-winning play. Watch for opponent's graveyard hate; if it resolves, pivot to a more direct game plan and avoid relying on graveyard recursion.

philosophy: Five-color Slivers aims to assemble a critical mass of interconnected creatures that share combat keywords and power/toughness boosts, turning every creature into a board-wiping threat. It wins by out-grinding value engines against control, stabilizing early against aggro, and racing through midrange with relentless, synergistic pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing Plains/Forest for early 1-drops), at least one early Sliver (Predatory, Plated, or Muscle), and a clear path to cast it by turn 2. Mulligan to 5 if you have 0-1 lands, multiple uncastable Slivers, or no early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land matching your earliest Sliver's mana cost (Plains for Predatory/Plated, Forest for Muscle). If you have a 1-drop, cast it. Turn 2: Play another land, then cast a second Sliver or a 2-drop utility Sliver (e.g., Sliver Hive/Queen). Sequence lands to ensure you can hit 2 and 3 mana reliably; fetch/shocks are ideal if your mana base supports them.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs. Mono Red Madness/Elves: +2 Plated Sliver, +2 Predatory Sliver, -2 utility Slivers. Vs. Grixis Affinity/Blue Terror: +2 Sliver Queen, +2 card draw/removal, -2 early 1-drops. Vs. Jund Wildfire: +1 Sliver Overlord, +2 hate bears, -2 low-impact Slivers. Keep core Sliver engines and mana base intact.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Slivers are mana-hungry; always maintain 1-2 mana open to react to sweepers or cast multiple threats. Prioritize casting Sliver Queen/Hive/Overlord to generate card advantage and replace lost threats. Avoid hoarding mana if you can safely commit to a board that trades favorably.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection (e.g., lifelink/first strike trading into removal) or when the opponent is tapped out. Hold back if you suspect a board wipe or spot removal; Slivers excel at trading, so don't overextend into a single sweeper unless you can go face for lethal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Toxic Deluge, etc.), spot removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and combo finishers. Note when opponents hold up mana past turn 3, as they likely have interaction. Track their life total to gauge if you should race or grind.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A board of 5+ Slivers with trample, haste, or lifelink, often fueled by Sliver Queen/Overlord card advantage. Win via combat if you can break through blockers, or via a sudden lethal turn when the opponent is low.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough power to trade favorably into blockers, when the opponent has no sweepers in hand, or when you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Also commit when you've resolved a Sliver engine and can replace losses immediately.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one Sliver against a control deck with multiple sweepers, if you're facing an unbeatable combo that resolves before you stabilize, or if your mana base is broken and you cannot cast threats consistently.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast multiple cheap threats in response to counterspells to force them to spend interaction. Use uncounterable effects if available, or simply out-value them by committing more threats than they can counter.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to finish them before they deploy their finishers. Use spot removal on key ramp targets and prioritize trading Slivers efficiently to stall their development.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early lifelink/first strike Slivers (Predatory/Plated) to trade into their attackers. Don't overcommit to a board that can be wiped; focus on reaching a critical mass of Slivers that can turn the tables with trample or haste.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly with multiple threats per turn to prevent them from stabilizing. Use Sliver Queen/Hive/Overlord to out-grind their card advantage. Don't let them sweep your board without threatening lethal or having a backup plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to card advantage and late-game power. Prioritize Sliver Queen/Overlord to generate value. Win by out-grinding, having better late-game threats, and forcing them to trade inefficiently. Sideboard in more value engines and hate for their specific Slivers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count if the format demands it, but generally keep the core Sliver engines intact. Bring in matchup-specific hate (removal, counterspells, or artifact/enchantment destruction) and adjust your threat curve based on whether you're facing faster or slower decks. Always sideboard in cards that synergize with Sliver abilities (e.g., trample, first strike, lifelink).
notes: Common mistake: Overextending into sweepers without a backup plan. Remember that Slivers share abilities, so losing one creature doesn't strip your board of keywords. Unique interaction: Sliver Hive/Queen/Overlord create a recursive value loop; protect them at all costs. Also, track opponent's life total closely, as Slivers often win by trading up and going face rather than grinding for a single massive threat.

philosophy: Mono Red Pingers is a tempo-aggressive archetype that wins by applying early pressure with cheap, efficient damage spells and a key threat like Kessig Flamebreather, while using spot removal to clear opposing boards. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on fragile boards or slow game plans, but struggles against heavy ramp or resilient aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably Mountains) and at least one source of early damage or removal (Lava Spike, Wrenn's Resolve, or a creature). Keep any hand containing Kessig Flamebreather paired with 2-3 lands. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands with no early interaction/damage spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Mountain, cast Lava Spike or Wrenn's Resolve if possible, or hold mana for turn 2. Turn 2: Play a second Mountain, cast Kessig Flamebreather. Use its ability to ping opposing creatures or go face. If the opponent plays a creature, use Wrenn's Resolve or Lava Spike to clear it. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Flamebreather on time without stalling.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity (control/aggro): Bring in additional Lava Spike and Wrenn's Resolve, remove excess lands. Against Jund Wildfire and Elves (midrange/ramp): Bring in targeted removal or board wipes, plus extra burn to race. Against Mono Red Madness (mirror): Bring in discard or hate cards against their key combo pieces, plus additional pingers to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep mana flexible for instant-speed pingers and interaction. Use Wrenn's Resolve to clear a threat and draw a card, maintaining card advantage. The graveyard is generally irrelevant for this archetype, so do not worry about graveyard recursion or hate. Avoid overcommitting to the board if the opponent has known sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Kessig Flamebreather early to pressure, but be ready to protect it or hold it back if the opponent has spot removal. Use pingers to clear key blockers before attacking. Hold back if the opponent has a sweeper ready or can stabilize with a large creature. Prioritize clearing the board over going face when the opponent has a resilient board state.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, big finishers, and combo pieces. Opponents playing Blue Terror or Grixis Affinity will likely have spot removal or counters; track their mana to predict interaction. Note when they tap out for a threat, as this is your window to ping their board or go face.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kessig Flamebreather pinging face repeatedly, combined with Lava Spike for the kill. If Flamebreather is removed, use remaining burn spells to close out the game. Ensure you have a Mountain in play to maximize Lava Spike's damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough pingers to bypass blockers or when the opponent is at low life (≤5). Commit when you've cleared their board and they have no sweepers or interaction left. Push damage aggressively when the opponent is forced to tap out for a threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one card and the opponent has a board wipe ready, or if you are facing a heavy ramp deck with no way to race. Also concede if you are land-locked and have no burn spells, or if the opponent has stabilized with a game-winning threat you cannot remove.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter. Attack with Kessig Flamebreather to force interaction and make the opponent choose between blocking or taking damage. Use Wrenn's Resolve to clear blockers and draw into answers. Avoid overcommitting to the board when the opponent has open mana.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. Do not engage in long games. Use pingers to clear their early threats and go face immediately. Prioritize damage spells over removal. Bring in more burn and less removal pre-sideboard if you expect a heavy ramp presence.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Wrenn's Resolve and Lava Spike to clear their early threats. Hold back Kessig Flamebreather for key blockers rather than trading into 1-drops. Stabilize the board first, then race back with pingers and burn. Track their hand for removal to know when you can safely commit.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force mistakes and make them use interaction on low-impact threats. Use pingers to clear their blockers and go face. Do not overextend; wait for them to use interaction before committing your board. Wrenn's Resolve is key to drawing into more threats when they lock the board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by using Wrenn's Resolve to draw into more pingers. Track their hand for Lava Spike to avoid overcommitting. Prioritize clearing their Kessig Flamebreather with Wrenn's Resolve or Lava Spike. Control the board with pingers and go face when they have no interaction left.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against control, add more direct damage and card draw to out-value them. Against aggro and ramp, add early interaction and hate cards to disrupt their game plan. In mirrors, prioritize card advantage and hate against their key pieces. Always keep at least one copy of Wrenn's Resolve and Lava Spike in your main deck or sideboard for flexibility.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Kessig Flamebreather without considering removal, and hoarding mana instead of using pingers to clear blockers. Always ensure you have a Mountain in play before casting Lava Spike to maximize its damage. Wrenn's Resolve can target your own creatures if needed, but is best used on opponent threats. Track opponent's mana carefully, as Mono Red Pingers wins by making them use interaction on low-impact threats.

philosophy: Azorius Ephemerate is a control/tempo deck that aims to disrupt the opponent's game plan with efficient counterspells and card selection while using Ephemerate as a versatile interaction tool to bounce threats, enable value triggers, or stabilize. It excels against combo and midrange strategies that rely on committing key permanents to the board, but must carefully manage resources against fast aggro and heavy disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2 lands (including at least one of each color or duals), Preordain, and Counterspell. Keep any hand with Preordain + land, or Counterspell + land. Mulligan to 4+ lands if you have no early interaction. Avoid keeping pure curve-out hands without answers, or hands with 3+ lands and no spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Preordain to set up land/answer/threat. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Counterspell if opponent casts a key spell, or play Ephemerate to bounce a threat. Prioritize maintaining card advantage and answering key threats before committing to a board. If no interaction is needed, develop a cheap creature or spell while holding up mana for counters.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in Lightning Strike/Abrade, additional counters, and artifact/enchantment removal. vs Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in more counters, card draw, and extra Ephemerate for bounce value. vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional counters, and discard. vs Mirror: Bring in extra counters, discard, and sideboard Ephemerate for value trading.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard counterspells for key threats, use Preordain to dig for answers or threats, and avoid overcommitting mana. Keep 1-2 counterspells in hand at all times. Manage your graveyard by avoiding unnecessary scry/dig if the opponent has hate, and use Ephemerate to protect your hand or board when resources are tight.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing multiple threats until you have interaction or a clear path to closing. Use Ephemerate defensively to bounce removal targets or offensively to trigger ETBs. Only commit when you can protect your board or have enough interaction to respond to their answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development to predict whether they are holding counterspells or removal. Note when they hold up mana on their turn (likely counterspell) vs. when they pass empty (likely out of answers). Track graveyard activity and key combo pieces, especially against Blue Terror and Elves.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ephemerate bouncing a key creature to trigger ETB effects, or bouncing an opponent's threat to stabilize and close with card advantage. Secondary finishers include efficient removal and counterspells to protect a closing threat or force a favorable trade.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no counterspells, or you can safely resolve a game-ending threat. Also commit when you can bounce a threat and still have mana for interaction, or when you can force them to use multiple answers on a single threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 cards, the opponent has a proven win condition in hand or on board, and you have no interaction or finishers. Also concede if you are consistently losing the mana race, have dead hands against fast decks, or cannot answer their key threats.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Preordain to dig around counterspells, cast threats in response to their counters, or layer interaction to force them to use multiple spells. Save Ephemerate to bounce their countered spells if possible, or use it to protect your own threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with counterspells on key ramp spells and use Ephemerate to bounce ramp threats before they stabilize. Use discard to disrupt their plan, and avoid letting them reach their late-game power level.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Flood with counterspells and cheap removal. Use Ephemerate to bounce early threats and buy time. Prioritize survival over card advantage early, and transition to control once the board stabilizes.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card selection and efficient threats. Use Ephemerate to bounce their removal or protect your board. Force them to use resources on multiple threats, and look for opportunities to close when they run out of answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war by using Preordain to dig deeper, saving counterspells for their key spells, and using Ephemerate to bounce their threats or trigger value. Prioritize card advantage, timing, and forcing them to use more resources than you do.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure control to matchup-specific strategies. Against fast decks, prioritize removal and counters to stabilize early. Against control and combo, bring in more interaction and card advantage to out-value them. Adjust land counts if needed, ensure you have answers to the most common threats, and avoid overloading on sideboard cards that don't address the top matchups.
notes: Ephemerate is versatile but not a win-con on its own; pair it with creatures that benefit from being cast multiple times or use it defensively to stabilize. Avoid overusing Preordain early; save it for when you need to dig for answers or threats. Watch for opponent's mana development to predict their interaction. Common mistake: holding too many counterspells and running out of mana to cast threats, or using Ephemerate on non-essential targets when you could save it for a game-ending bounce.

philosophy: Esper Affinity is a resilient, artifact-synergistic midrange-control hybrid that leverages cost reduction, recursive spellcasting, and flexible interaction to outvalue opponents. It wins by deploying cheap artifact threats, swinging with Myr Enforcer pumps, and using Thoughtcast and Thraben Charm to control the board while out-resourcing through card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize fixing/duals if available), at least one artifact or artifact creature, and a key enabler or interaction (Thoughtcast, Thraben Charm, or Myr Enforcer). Mulligan on pure land, zero artifacts, or unplayable early turns that lack a way to establish board presence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, drop a cheap artifact (e.g., Manifold Key, Arcbound Worker, or generic artifact) to build affinity and set up mana/artifact synergy. Turn 2: Play a second land, deploy Myr Enforcer or another artifact creature. Use Thoughtcast if you have an instant in the graveyard or can cast one from hand to dig deeper. Sequence artifacts to maximize affinity discounts on your threats, ensuring you can cast them at reduced costs.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness): Bring in Thraben Charm, additional removal, and life stabilization. vs Control (Blue Terror): Bring in Thoughtcast recursion targets, artifact destruction, and counterspell hate. vs Ramp (Elves): Bring in artifact destruction, graveyard hate, and early interaction to disrupt land drops. vs Grixis Affinity (Mirror): Bring in additional removal, card draw, and artifact hate to disrupt their enablers and win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until your board is established. Use Thoughtcast to recycle instants/sorceries from the graveyard, effectively doubling card advantage and digging for key answers. Avoid overcommitting artifacts if the board is unstable; prioritize creatures with haste, evasion, or enabler synergy.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their answers or when you can safely swing with pumped Myrs. Hold back enablers if they can be removed for tempo loss. Use Thraben Charm flexibly: remove their artifacts, pump your attackers into lethal, or stabilize life totals against aggressive strategies.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for their removal suite, counterspells, and board wipes. Note if they are playing around Thraben Charm's artifact destruction mode by protecting key artifacts. Track graveyard contents for Thoughtcast recursion targets and anticipate when they will attempt to clear your board with sweepers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Unstoppable artifact swarms powered by Myr Enforcer, backed by recursive Thoughtcast spells and precise Thraben Charm pumps. Win through combat damage, overwhelming value, or forcing them to trade inefficiently into your interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, their interaction is exhausted, or you can safely swing with pumped Myrs. Commit when you can protect your attackers with removal, evasion, or when you can close the game before they can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock, have no artifacts to cast, and they have multiple board wipes or removal. Concede if you are down to one life, they have a win condition you cannot interact with, and you have no recovery tools or card advantage engine to dig out of the hole.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtcast to cast spells from the graveyard, bypassing counterspells. Sequence artifacts to reduce costs below counter thresholds. Use instant-speed interaction to clear their counterspellers or force them to use resources inefficiently.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops and force trades. Use Thraben Charm to remove key ramp artifacts or pump your threats to race. Do not let them stabilize; trade efficiently and prioritize removing their mana sources.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Thraben Charm's life gain and targeted removal. Use Thoughtcast to recycle removal from the graveyard. Do not overextend; trade artifacts for their threats and close the game quickly once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with artifact synergy and recursion. Use Thoughtcast to dig deeper and apply consistent pressure with cheap threats, forcing them to use resources inefficiently. Respect their sweepers and hold back enablers until they commit to clearing the board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing artifact destruction to disrupt their enablers. Use Thoughtcast to out-recycle and dig for key answers. Track their removal and hold back enablers until they commit. Favor trades that leave you with more artifacts and card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure artifact acceleration to targeted interaction and matchup-specific hate. Against control, prioritize recursion and card advantage. Against aggro/ramp, bring in early removal and life stabilization. In mirrors, emphasize artifact hate and resource denial. Always keep Thraben Charm and Thoughtcast as flexible staples, and adjust your land base if the format demands more fixing or basic lands for protection.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting Myr Enforcer without protection, leading to tempo loss. Always sequence artifacts to maximize affinity discounts. Thoughtcast synergy relies on graveyard management; avoid milling yourself or discarding your own key instants. Thraben Charm's modes are situational; prioritize artifact destruction against artifact-heavy decks, pump in combat, and life gain against aggro. Remember that Esper Affinity is a 0.2% metagame pick, so expect unfamiliar sideboard strategies and adapt by tracking their resource curve rather than assuming standard Affinity patterns.

philosophy: Jeskai Affinity accelerates into massive, resilient artifact threats while leveraging instant-speed interaction to protect its board and close out games. It wins by out-accelerating and out-resolving opponents, turning a few cheap artifacts into unblockable or unkillable game-enders.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana accelerator (Thoughtcast, Talisman, or similar), and a turn-1 or turn-2 artifact or threat. Mulligan on pure land hands, multiple accelerators with no artifacts, or hands lacking a way to play a creature by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtcast or a cheap artifact like Glint Hawk or Ornithopter. Turn 2: Play another land, cast a second accelerator or a medium threat (Memnite, Myr Retriever), or attack with evasive artifacts. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast spells on curve while maintaining mana for instant-speed interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Mono Red Madness and aggro: Bring in Galvanic Blast, Lightning Strike, and artifact protection. Against Blue Terror and Elves (control/ramp): Bring in counterspells, artifact destruction, and additional card draw. Against Jund Wildfire (midrange): Bring in targeted removal, graveyard hate, and protection. Against Grixis Affinity (mirror): Bring in artifact destruction, counters, and disruption to break their acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for instant-speed interaction while maintaining artifact development. Use Thoughtcast to dig for answers or threats. Avoid overcommitting artifacts if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal in hand.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, counters, or removal. Use evasive artifacts to pressure while developing a bigger threat, and use instants to clear blockers or remove key threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal, counterspells, and graveyard recursion. Note when the opponent taps out for a big spell or holds up mana for interaction, and adjust your aggression accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A massive artifact creature (e.g., Arcbound Ravager with +1/+1 counters, Myr Battlesphere, or a hasted threat) combined with burn or combat tricks. Use Galvanic Blast to remove blockers or finish the opponent at 3-4 life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to combat, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can trade artifacts favorably. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns and have enough interaction to protect your final attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock, have no artifacts in play or hand, the opponent has multiple sweepers, or you are down to a dead hand with no acceleration. Jeskai Affinity struggles to recover from board wipes or complete resource denial.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtcast to dig for protection or threats. Cast spells in response to counterspells when possible. Rely on artifacts that enter the battlefield with immediate impact or cast multiple spells in a row to force counters and find answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use Galvanic Blast and other removal to kill their ramp creatures before they stabilize. Do not let them reach their late-game power level.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Glint Hawk and other evasive artifacts to trade efficiently. Hold up Galvanic Blast for their key threats. Stabilize with removal and then close the game with a big threat before they overwhelm you.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap artifacts and cantrips. Force them to use resources on interaction. Close out games quickly before they can set up their win condition, and use instant-speed spells to disrupt their combo or control pieces.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-accelerating and out-resolving. Use Thoughtcast to dig deeper and find answers. Trade artifacts favorably, disrupt their acceleration with counters or destruction, and prioritize board presence over card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific matchup. Against control and ramp, prioritize counters and artifact destruction to break their game plan. Against aggro, prioritize removal and protection to stabilize. Against midrange, add graveyard hate and targeted removal. Always maintain a balance of acceleration and interaction to keep your game plan viable.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting artifacts without leaving mana for instant-speed interaction. Always keep a mana source open for Galvanic Blast or counters. Don't forget to use Thoughtcast to dig for answers or threats. Watch for sweepers and hold up mana accordingly. Jeskai Affinity thrives on tempo and resource advantage, so prioritize efficient trades and timely aggression.

philosophy: Rakdos Affinity leverages artifact synergies and cost reduction to deploy a resilient, low-cost threat curve while using targeted removal and life-loss mechanics to control the board and grind out card advantage. It excels at racing aggressive decks, punishing slow control with relentless pressure, and out-resourcing mirrors through discard and efficient artifact trades.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early artifact (Springleaf Drum, Signet, or Arcbound Ravager), and early interaction (discard or Galvanic Blast/Fanatical Offering). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no artifacts or threats, or hands with only removal and no way to cast them. Prioritize hands that can play a land and an artifact on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast a cheap artifact. If you have Springleaf Drum, play it to enable casting a 1-mana artifact or spell. Turn 2: Develop your board with additional artifacts, cast Arcbound Ravager or Myr Enforcer, and use removal to clear early blockers or go face. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your highest-priority artifact each turn, maximizing Affinity discounts.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in discard (Thoughtseize/Inquisition), early removal (Galvanic Blast, Fatal Push), and life gain if available. vs Control (Blue Terror, Jund Wildfire): Bring in more discard, Reckoner's Bargain, and spot removal to disrupt their game plan. vs Mirror/Combo: Prioritize discard and additional removal to disrupt their engine and out-grind them.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting artifacts to maximize Affinity discounts. Use Springleaf Drum to cast spells out of sequence. Manage life total carefully when using Reckoner's Bargain; only pay 5 life if you can stabilize or close the game. Keep removal for key threats, not just chump blockers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to handle blockers or when the opponent is low on interaction. Hold back if they have sweepers or heavy removal. Use Fanatical Offering to trade artifacts for value (e.g., sacrifice a Signet for a creature, or sacrifice a Ravager to clear a blocker).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), counterspells (Counterspell, Mana Leak), and life gain engines. Note when they tap out for big threats. Track their graveyard if they play discard/life-loss strategies, and monitor their life total to time your Reckoner's Bargain draws.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Galvanic Blast and Fanatical Offering for direct damage or clearing blockers. Arcbound Ravager with +1/+1 counters or Myr Enforcer for combat damage. Reckoner's Bargain to draw into game-ending threats if you're in a grindy position.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to combat damage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can trade artifacts to clear their board. Commit when your life total is safe enough to cast Reckoner's Bargain and draw into a win condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no way to break through, if you're dead to a specific hate card (e.g., Rest in Peace, Null Rod) and have no answers, or if your life total is too low to cast Reckoner's Bargain and you have no finishers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast artifacts in response to their removal or when they're tapped out. Use Springleaf Drum to cast spells at instant speed. Consider casting threats when they have no mana available or when you can trade artifacts to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use discard to hit their mana dorks or key ramp spells. Trade artifacts efficiently to slow their curve. Don't overcommit if they have sweepers, and prioritize clearing their early threats to buy time for your own engine.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early removal (Galvanic Blast, Fanatical Offering) to clear key threats. Play discard to disrupt their hand.
