Stabilize with life total management and Reckoner's Bargain if needed. Race them if you have a clear path to damage, but prioritize survival over reckless aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly with cheap artifacts. Use discard to remove protection spells and counters. Grind with Reckoner's Bargain and artifact value. Force them to use removal on your threats to open up damage, and be ready to swing through with multiple artifacts once they tap out.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through discard efficiency and artifact value. Trade artifacts favorably (e.g., sacrifice a Signet for a creature). Use Reckoner's Bargain to out-draw them. Prioritize clearing their key threats with removal. Track their life total and discard to deny their Bargain, and never overextend without board presence.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your artifact count based on matchup. Against control, increase discard and Reckoner's Bargain to grind. Against aggro, add early interaction and discard. In mirrors, prioritize discard and artifact value. Always keep your core artifact engine intact, but swap in matchup-specific hate or disruption. Remove excess lands if you're running a more aggressive post-sideboard plan.
notes: Avoid overcommitting to Springleaf Drum if you can't cast a spell with it. Don't sacrifice Fanatical Offering on your own artifacts unless it clears a critical blocker or goes face for lethal. Watch your life total carefully when casting Reckoner's Bargain; going below 5 life without a win condition is often fatal. In mirrors, discard is king—prioritize it over drawing extra cards. Always track opponent's hand size to time your discard and Bargain plays optimally.

philosophy: Four-color Storm aims to win by casting a high volume of cheap spells to generate massive storm count, leveraging Dark Ritual for acceleration and Exhume to recycle key pieces from the graveyard. It targets slower, resource-heavy archetypes and outpaces aggressive decks before they can stabilize, relying on explosive turns and graveyard recursion to close out games.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands plus at least one Dark Ritual or equivalent acceleration. Keep any hand containing Exhume paired with a cheap storm spell or a creature that can be cast for free/cheap. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no acceleration, or hands with multiple copies of Exhume but no cheap spells to cast. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn-2 or turn-3 storm combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Dark Ritual or equivalent mana spell. If you have a cheap storm spell, cast it to build initial storm count. Turn 2: Ramp with additional rituals, cast cheap spells, and begin assembling the combo. If you have Exhume and a key piece in the graveyard, consider casting it early to set up recursion. Mana sequencing: Prioritize fetching/shocking into the colors needed for your immediate spell, then stabilize with untapped lands. Never overextend into a potential board wipe without a clear win condition.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity (control/combo): Bring in countermagic or protection spells, plus graveyard hate if the opponent relies on it. Against Mono Red Madness and Jund Wildfire (aggro/ramp): Bring in direct damage, removal, and early interaction. Against Elves (ramp): Bring in artifact/enchantment removal and hand disruption. In mirror matches: Bring in additional recursion protection and discard spells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Dark Ritual as your primary engine; cast it as early as possible to maximize storm count. Use Exhume strategically to recur your most expensive or game-winning spell, not just any cheap spell. Keep a card advantage engine or draw spell in reserve to avoid running out of gas. Monitor your graveyard: if Exhume is your only recursion, protect it or have a backup plan if it's removed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Storm is fragile; avoid committing to the board unless you have a guaranteed win or a way to protect your combo. If the opponent plays a board wipe, be ready to pivot to a backup win condition or concede if you lack recursion. Hold back on casting your entire hand if you suspect a sweep; instead, build storm count gradually and keep a finisher in hand.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially in Blue Terror and Grixis Affinity) and hold up interaction or cast your combo in response to their end steps. Track graveyard hate (like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void) and adjust your recursion plans. Note when opponents tap out for removal or sweepers, as this is your window to go off.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The primary win condition is casting a high-storm spell (like Not Dead After All or a similar combo piece) when your storm count is high enough to win in one turn. Use Exhume to recur this piece if it's in the graveyard, or cast it from hand if you have enough mana. If the combo is disrupted, pivot to a secondary win condition like a large creature or direct damage spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast your entire combo, your storm count is sufficient to win, and you've accounted for potential counterspells (e.g., by casting in response to their upkeep or using protection). Also commit if the opponent has no interaction left and you can win before their next turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., empty hand, no draw spells, opponent has a sweep ready), if your key recursion is permanently removed and you lack alternative win conditions, or if you're down to a dead hand against a fast aggro deck that can win in one turn.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split casts or cast your combo in response to their counterspells. If possible, use cheap spells to force out their counters before going off. Consider bringing in protection or discard to disrupt their hand. If they have heavy counter coverage, consider a backup win condition or concede if the matchup is unfavorable.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Outpace them by going off as early as possible. Use discard or early interaction to disrupt their mana development. If they stabilize, use your graveyard recursion to out-value their larger threats. Bring in artifact/enchantment removal if they rely on it.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early survival: use any available removal, counters, or life-gain to stabilize. If you can't stabilize, go off immediately on turn 2 or 3 if possible. Bring in direct damage and early interaction to slow them down.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure them with early storm count to force them to use resources. If they try to lock you out, use Exhume to recur key pieces and maintain pressure. If they have heavy board wipes, wait for a safe turn to go off, or pivot to a backup plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: The mirror is a race to storm count and graveyard recursion. Prioritize drawing cards, protecting your graveyard, and disrupting theirs with discard. Use Exhume to recur your win condition faster than they can. If they have graveyard hate, focus on hand disruption and direct damage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction and protection based on the metagame: increase counters/protection against control/combo, add early pressure against aggro, and bring in graveyard disruption against recursion-heavy decks. In mirrors, prioritize discard and recursion protection. Always tailor your sideboard to the specific threats, but keep your core storm engine intact.
notes: Four-color storm requires careful mana management; ensure you have enough dual lands or equivalent to support all colors without slowing down. Exhume is powerful but vulnerable to graveyard hate; always have a backup plan if it's removed. 'Not Dead After All' likely functions as a high-storm combo piece or recursive threat; practice sequencing to maximize its impact. Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board or casting too many spells without a clear win condition. Always calculate your storm count and opponent's potential interaction. In Unknown format, check for format-specific rules that might affect recursion, storm count, or mana base.

philosophy: Naya Ramp accelerates mana to cast high-impact threats like Avenging Hunter and Generous Ent ahead of schedule, then amplifies them with Utopia Sprawl to overwhelm opponents. It wins by out-scaling slower decks and out-pacing aggressive strategies before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one ramp spell (e.g., Skyshroud Claim, Farseek, or a mana creature) or a turn-2 land drop. Prioritize hands with Utopia Sprawl or a 3-mana threat if paired with ramp. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only 1-2 lands, or hands with multiple threats but zero ramp or fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a ramp spell or play a mana creature. Turn 2: Play a land, cast another ramp or a 2-mana threat. Turn 3: Drop Utopia Sprawl if you have the mana, or cast a 3-mana threat like Generous Ent. Sequence lands to ensure you hit 4-5 mana by turn 3-4. Chain multiple ramp spells to hit 5 mana by turn 3 to drop Avenging Hunter or multiple threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity (control/aggro): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against Mono Red Madness/Jund Wildfire (aggro/combo): Bring in direct damage, life gain, and early interaction. Against Elves (ramp): Bring in graveyard hate, artifact destruction, and targeted removal. Against mirror: Bring in additional removal and card draw to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana acceleration over card advantage early. Don't overextend into removal; save Utopia Sprawl for your biggest threats. Manage hand size by casting threats when you can't improve the board further. Avoid flooding by converting excess ramp into card selection or threats once you hit 5 mana.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or spot removal. Use Utopia Sprawl on your most resilient threat or the one that will survive combat. Don't drop Utopia Sprawl unless you have a clear path to the opponent or a way to protect it.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and counterspells. Note if the opponent is playing discard or hand disruption. Track their mana development to predict when they can answer your threats. Pay attention to graveyard activity if they play reanimation or recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Avenging Hunter and Generous Ent with Utopia Sprawl are primary finishers. Once you have a 6/6 or 7/7 with haste, push for lethal. Use additional threats to overwhelm blockers or trade into sweepers. Utopia Sprawl can also be cast on a cheaper threat to win combat or push damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Utopia Sprawl on board, a clear path to the opponent, and no immediate sweepers in play. Also commit when you can trade threats favorably or when the opponent is at low life. Don't hold back if you have multiple threats and the opponent has no interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock, have no answers to their sweepers, or are consistently one-dropped with no ramp. Concede if you're down to one card and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition, or if you're in a dead matchup with no sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple threats in a row to force counterspells. Cast Utopia Sprawl on your most expensive threat to make it harder to counter or trade. Use cheap threats to bait counters. If they have heavy counter magic, pivot to enchantment-based strategies or sideboard in protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with faster threats. Use Utopia Sprawl to win combat and stabilize. Bring in artifact/enchantment hate if they rely on it. Don't overcommit to big threats; trade favorably and pressure their life total.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early threats to trade. Use spot removal if available. Don't overcommit to big threats; stabilize with smaller blockers or removal. Utopia Sprawl can be cast on a 2-drop to trade into their 3-drops. Prioritize survival over going wide.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats. Don't let them stabilize. Use Utopia Sprawl to make your threats unblockable or too large to answer. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their win condition. Sideboard in more threats if they have heavy sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing. Use card draw, removal, and better sequencing. Utopia Sprawl on a cheaper threat can win trades. Track their hand to know when they have answers. Prioritize board presence and force them to use removal inefficiently.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to be more interactive. Add hate cards for the top metagame threats. Reduce pure ramp if facing control, increase interaction if facing aggro. Keep Utopia Sprawl as a flexible win condition but consider bringing in additional enchantment hate if they run it. Always carry at least one board wipe and spot removal for versatility.
notes: Common mistake: casting Utopia Sprawl on a 2-drop instead of a 4/5 threat. Another: over-ramping into a dead hand. Remember that Utopia Sprawl is an enchantment, so it can be destroyed by artifact/enchantment hate. Sequence lands carefully to avoid flooding. In the mirror, card advantage and efficient removal win games. Always track their life total and board state before committing to a Utopia Sprawl play.

philosophy: A fast, escalating poison strategy that leverages cheap pump spells and artifact creatures to deal 21 poison counters before opponents can stabilize. Wins against slower midrange, control, and combo decks by applying immediate pressure and using evasion to bypass traditional removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands and a turn-1 creature (Kithkin, Ichorclaw Myr) or a creature paired with a pump spell (Mutagenic Growth, Rancor). Keep any hand with two pump spells. Mulligan pure land hands, pump-only hands, or 3+ land hands with no early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast creature. If you have a pump spell, cast it on your attacker. Turn 2: Play land, cast a second creature or pump spell, and attack with your pumped threat. Attach Rancor to attackers when possible. Prioritize land drops to maintain curve and ensure you can cast threats and pumps on time.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Blue Terror/Jund Wildfire (control/midrange): +2 Mutagenic Growth, +2 Ichorclaw Myr, -2 lands to increase pump density and close out games faster. Against Mono Red Madness/Elves (aggro/ramp): +2 Giant Growth, +1 creature, -1 land to improve early trades and survive their initial burst. Against Grixis Affinity (artifact combo): +2 pump spells to race, -1 land; consider artifact hate if your list supports it. Keep your core fast-poison game plan intact.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always preserve enough for both threats and pumps. Do not overextend without pump support. Keep pump spells flexible to close out games or save creatures from targeted removal. Track your hand size carefully, as Infect often wins by turn 4-5.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have the mana to pump them or when the opponent lacks removal. Hold back pump spells if you suspect spot removal or board wipes. Use artifact creatures to pressure while keeping your lands safe from targeted hate.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for targeted removal (Lightning Bolt, Go for the Throat) and board wipes. Note land destruction or discard effects. Track the opponent's life total and poison counters meticulously to know exactly when you are at lethal range.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Pump spells (Mutagenic Growth, Rancor) combined with creatures to deal 21 poison. Ichorclaw Myr can be pumped to lethal. Attack with a 3+ power creature or chain pumps to close out. Calculate poison damage carefully, factoring in blockers and removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to pump your attacker to lethal poison, or when the opponent has no removal in hand or on the board. If you are at 20 life and they have no threats, commit fully to close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1 life, have no creatures or pump spells, and the opponent has a board wipe or lock in place. Also concede if facing a combo that will win on their next turn and you have no way to race or disrupt.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that can be cast in response to counterspells if needed. Rely on artifact creatures and instant-speed pumps to close out before they can counter key threats. Avoid overcommitting to expensive spells that can be countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them relentlessly. Do not let them stabilize. Use pump spells to close out before their mana advantage translates to board presence. If they deploy a big threat, pump your creature to trade efficiently or kill it before it attacks.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Your game plan is already aggressive, so focus on surviving their early damage. Use pump spells to trade efficiently. If they have removal, hold back. If they are going wide, use your pump to kill their key threats or close out before they do.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly. Do not let them draw cards or stabilize. Use pump spells to bypass removal. If they have a board wipe, save your pump spells for after it resolves. Attack with everything you can afford to pump to force them to answer immediately.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It is a race. Prioritize drawing pump spells and creatures. Track both players' life totals and poison counters. Use your pump spells to close out before they do. If you have a removal spell, use it to clear their threat before attacking.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against control/midrange, increase your pump spell density and artifact creature count to ensure you can close out games quickly. Against aggro, add more efficient removal or additional creatures to trade efficiently. Always keep your core game plan intact: fast poison with pump support. Avoid bringing in too many lands or slow threats that dilute your win condition.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without enough pump spells to close out. Always calculate poison damage carefully. Remember that Ichorclaw Myr is an artifact, so it can be targeted by artifact hate, but it also synergizes well with pump spells. Rancor is an enchantment, so it can be destroyed by enchantment removal. Mutagenic Growth is instant, so you can use it in response to removal to save your creature or close out. Keep an eye on the opponent's life total and poison counters to know exactly when to go for lethal.

philosophy: Mono-Black operates as a resilient midrange-control hybrid, leveraging efficient spot removal and self-mill/tokens to out-value opponents through attrition. It aims to stabilize early, answer key threats, and close games with resilient creatures like Troll of Khazad-dum while out-grinding control and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands and at least one interaction (Defile or Cast Down) or Troll of Khazad-dum. Drop hands with 3+ lands, no removal, or only high-cost threats. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a removal spell or a 2-drop threat by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally hold removal for turn 2. Turn 2: Develop board with Troll or use Defile/Cast Down on opponent's key creature. Sequence lands to ensure black mana availability for spot removal on turn 2 or 3. If facing aggro, prioritize removal; if facing control/combo, develop Troll to apply pressure and prepare for graveyard/token synergy.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in additional spot removal (Cast Down/Defile), life gain, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Control/Combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and card advantage. vs Jund Wildfire/Mirror: Keep mainboard heavy, bring in targeted hate for specific permanents and discard if available.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve removal for high-value targets like finishers or combo pieces. Use Troll's death trigger to generate board presence when forced to trade. Manage hand size by trading removal for tempo or threats, and avoid overcommitting mana when opponent is low on resources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to handle counters or sweepers. Hold back removal if opponent is out of resources or if you can out-value them with Troll's tokens. Prioritize trading Troll for their best creature to maximize value.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counters, or combo win conditions. Note when opponents save removal vs. when they commit it. Track graveyard usage if they play discard or reanimation, and monitor their mana to predict removal windows.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Troll of Khazad-dum (and its tokens) combined with spot removal to clear the path. Cast Down/Defile to remove blockers or finish low-life opponents.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have board control, opponent is at low life, or you can trade removal for their win condition. Commit when you can force favorable trades with Troll's death trigger.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If facing a fast combo that resolves before turn 4, or if you're out of removal against a sweeper + finisher strategy with no hand resources. Fold when you have no interaction left and opponent has established an unanswerable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use removal on threats before they can be countered, or hold removal for when they blow mana on counters. Trade Troll for their uncounterable threats and apply pressure with tokens to force suboptimal counter decisions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp creatures. Troll's tokens can outpace their early game, and you can stabilize by removing their mana sources.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal on turn 2/3 threats. Use Troll to generate tokens and stabilize. Prioritize life gain if available, and avoid overcommitting to board wipes without follow-up.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with Troll. Use removal to clear their blockers. Out-value them with card advantage and resilient threats, forcing them to spend resources on interaction rather than their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades are key. Use Troll to force unfavorable trades. Save removal for their threats. Prioritize hand disruption if present, and track their graveyard to avoid falling for reanimation or recursion.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a balanced midrange shell to a more focused game plan. Against combo/control, increase removal and hate cards. Against aggro, prioritize stabilization and early interaction. Adjust land count if needed for mana fixing or speed, and always carry at least one copy of your primary hate cards to ensure consistency.
notes: Avoid overcommitting to board wipes if you lack follow-up. Troll's death trigger is a key resource—don't sacrifice it unnecessarily. Track opponent's mana and hand size to predict removal usage. In the Unknown format, adapt to specific metagame shifts by prioritizing hate cards against top-tier archetypes, and always keep a backup plan if your primary game plan is disrupted.

philosophy: This deck leverages instant-speed bounce effects like Ephemerate to repeatedly trigger enter-the-battlefield and dies effects, creating value swings and tempo advantages that outlast attrition-based strategies while outmaneuvering control. It wins by converting cheap creatures into multiple triggers, disrupting opponent plans, and closing games with flying threats or repeated ETB value.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals or shocklands for fixing) and at least one Ephemerate or bounce enabler. Keep hands with 1-2 mana creatures that have ETB/DTB triggers or can be bounced for value. Mulligan to pure land hands, hands with only 3+ mana creatures, or hands lacking bounce/removal in fast formats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a 1-mana creature or play a shockland if fixing is critical. Turn 2: Cast a 2-mana creature, then use Ephemerate to bounce it back to hand to trigger its ETB effect, or bounce an opponent's threat if interaction is needed. If drawing Writhing Chrysalis, play it on turn 2 or 3, then bounce it with Ephemerate to trigger its death effect and gain flying/tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: Bring in more threats, card draw, and protection spells. vs Mono Red Madness/Elves: Bring in board wipes, cheap removal, and artifact/enchantment hate. vs Jund Wildfire: Bring in graveyard hate, additional bounce/removal, and hand disruption. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, additional bounce effects, and card advantage engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Ephemerate for high-value targets (your own ETB triggers or opponent's key threats). Don't overcommit mana to bounce loops if you can't follow up. Use shocklands strategically to maintain mana flexibility. Track graveyard resources if Writhing Chrysalis or other DTB effects are involved.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction ready to protect them or bounce opponent's blockers. Hold back Ephemerate if the opponent is likely to have removal or board wipes. Prioritize board presence over trading into nothing; use bounce to reset the board state when falling behind.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, board wipes, and targeted removal. Note if the opponent is saving interaction for your bounce targets. Track graveyard accumulation if they play discard or mill. Pay attention to mana development—if they're playing extra lands or ramp spells, they're likely ahead on tempo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Deploy a high-impact creature with a strong ETB/DTB effect, then use Ephemerate to bounce it for a second trigger, or bounce it to hand to cast it again. If Writhing Chrysalis is in play, bounce it to trigger flying/+1/+1, then attack with a buffed threat. Use Ephemerate on opponent's blockers to clear the path.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have multiple bounce enablers, a clear path to damage, and the opponent has no interaction left. Commit when you can trigger multiple ETB/DTB effects in a single turn to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked board with no removal, have no bounce enablers or threats, and the opponent is ahead on cards and mana. Concede if you're stuck in a control mirror with no card draw or win conditions, or if you're facing a combo that has already assembled its win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ephemerate on your own creatures to trigger ETB effects before they're countered, or bounce opponent's counterspellers if possible. Cast spells in response to their interaction, or use cheap, uncounterable threats if available. Rely on instant-speed bounce to bypass stack interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use Ephemerate to bounce their ramp creatures or key threats. Bring in board wipes and targeted removal post-sideboard. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and keep the board state favorable.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap removal and bounce effects to neutralize early threats. Use Ephemerate to bounce their attackers to buy time for your own threats to develop. Hold back interaction until they commit to a board, then clear it efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure the board with resilient threats that trigger on ETB/DTB. Use Ephemerate to bounce your creatures to trigger effects again, or bounce their removal spells if possible. Outlast them with card advantage and efficient trades. Don't overextend; trade into their interaction when it's safe.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards and having better bounce sequencing. Prioritize keeping Ephemerate for high-value triggers. Use targeted removal to eliminate their threats before they can bounce them. Control the board state by trading efficiently and forcing them to use interaction on low-value targets.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against fast decks, prioritize board wipes and cheap removal to stabilize. Against control/combo, add more threats, card draw, and protection. Always keep at least one Ephemerate and a bounce enabler. Adjust your land base if needed, and consider bringing in graveyard hate or artifact/enchantment removal based on the specific matchup.
notes: Ephemerate only targets creatures, so plan your bounce loops carefully. Writhing Chrysalis's flying trigger is powerful but temporary; use it to bypass ground blockers or finish the game. Avoid bouncing your own creatures if you don't have a follow-up trigger. Watch out for board wipes that can reset your bounce loops. Practice stack sequencing to maximize ETB/DTB triggers without giving the opponent a chance to respond.

philosophy: Serpentine Curve is a U/R tempo-control archetype that seeks to dictate the pace of the game through efficient interaction, card advantage, and escalating threats. It wins by out-interacting aggressive and combo decks early, then closing out midrange and control games with Lorien Revealed or a fully cast Serpentine Curve.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Counterspell, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (cantrip, cheap threat, or Serpentine Curve). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, hands with only Counterspell and no threats, or hands missing dual lands if your curve requires specific colors early.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a dual land (if possible), cast a cantrip or 1-drop to set up the curve. Turn 2: Curve into a 2-drop threat or hold up Counterspell for key spells. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetch/shock lands for fixing, then basics to avoid tapping on critical turns. Always leave mana open for interaction unless you're committing to a curve play.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): +2 Counterspell, +2 Artifact/Enchantment Hate, -2 basic lands. vs Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +2 Lorien Revealed, +2 Card Draw, -2 early threats. vs Combo (Jund Wildfire): +2 Graveyard Hate, +1 Counterspell, -2 finishers. vs Mirror: +1 Counterspell, +1 Card Advantage, -2 spot removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Counterspell as a tempo tool, not a panic button. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and avoid flooding. Never tap out for a Serpentine Curve unless you have protection or the board is clear. Manage your graveyard carefully if Lorien Revealed interacts with it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have at least one counterspell or removal left. Hold back if the opponent has untapped mana and likely holds a sweeper or heavy interaction. Trade efficiently: use a counterspell on a game-ending threat, not a tempo play.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (especially in Jund Wildfire and Blue Terror) and ramp spells (Elves, Grixis Affinity). Note when opponents hold up mana past turn 3—they likely have interaction. Track their graveyard if Jund Wildfire is present, and watch for combo win conditions in Mono Red Madness.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed is your primary win condition; protect it with counterspells or discard. A fully cast Serpentine Curve can also close out games if the board is open. Use card draw to ensure you have enough resources to resolve your finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction left, or when you can protect your finisher with a counterspell or discard. Commit to Lorien Revealed if you have card advantage and they lack removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to basic lands with no interaction, the opponent has a sweeper + finisher on the stack, or you’re facing a locked combo with no sideboard hate. Don’t bluff through known sweepers or heavy interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig through counterspells, cast threats in response to their counterspells, or overload with multiple threats. Sideboard in spells that can’t be countered or that resolve partially.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use Counterspell on their ramp spells. Bring in artifact/enchantment hate and sweepers. Don’t let them get ahead; stabilize with efficient threats and card draw.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early Counterspell and spot removal. Stabilize by trading efficiently and curving into threats that can outpace their aggression. Don’t overcommit to big spells; focus on survival and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with threats and use card draw to out-value them. Sideboard in finishers and hate on their interaction. Force them to use resources on your threats, then close out with Lorien Revealed.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war by holding up counterspells for key spells. Trade efficiently and use card draw to out-resource them. Sideboard in hate for their finishers and prioritize board presence over interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land base to match the metagame: add more basics if facing heavy color-screw, or duals if you need fixing against control. Prioritize hate cards for the top 5 archetypes, and ensure you have enough interaction to stabilize against aggro while maintaining enough finishers to close out control games. Always keep at least one Counterspell in play unless you’re facing a deck that doesn’t care about counters.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Counterspell on tempo plays instead of game-ending threats. Unique interaction: Lorien Revealed’s recursion engine synergizes with Serpentine Curve’s escalating cost; cast Curve early to build graveyard value, then recur it later. Always track opponent’s hand size and mana; bluffing is less effective in this format due to high interaction. Practice mulligan decisions rigorously—this deck is highly sensitive to early game quality.

philosophy: A recursive, aggressive tempo deck that floods the board with cheap goblins, leverages Ichor Wellspring for mana acceleration and extra spells, and uses Unearth to replay key threats from the graveyard. It aims to overwhelm slower decks before they stabilize and out-value control through relentless recursion and search effects.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetches), at least one turn-1 goblin (Goblin Guide, Skirk Prospector), and early recursion/search pieces. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early threats, 2 lands with no goblins or Wellspring, or hands with only high-cost spells. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a goblin on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Goblin Guide or Skirk Prospector. Turn 2: Play land, cast another goblin or Ichor Wellspring. Sequence Wellspring early to enable Unearth recursion or cast a mid-game threat. Use Goblin Matron on turn 2 or 3 to fetch Unearth targets, lands, or specific answers. Tap Prospector for damage or card draw to maintain tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Terror, Affinity): Bring in discard, artifact hate, and early threats. Against control (Jund, Grixis): Bring in more recursion, graveyard protection, and direct damage. Against Elves: Bring in artifact removal, discard, and early pressure. Against mirror: Bring in additional recursion and discard to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana to keep Unearth targets in hand or graveyard. Use Ichor Wellspring to fuel multiple spells or cast a game-winning threat. Use Matron to find specific answers or threats rather than hoarding it. Track opponent's graveyard and exile effects to avoid dead recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit aggressively when the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal. Hold back and stabilize if they have board wipes or heavy interaction. Use Prospector taps for tempo or damage, but avoid overextending into sweepers. Prioritize trading goblins efficiently to maintain board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment, etc.), counterspells, and graveyard hate. Note when the opponent taps out for big threats or has unanswered removal. Track their life total and resource depletion to time your Unearth plays.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Unearthed threats (Goblin Piker, Skirk Prospector, Goblin Chieftain), Wellspring-fueled big threats, or combat with a full board of goblins. Use recursion to replay key threats after sweepers or removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers, low life total, or you have graveyard recursion ready. Commit when you can trade efficiently or when your Wellspring engine is fully online.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has multiple sweepers, you're out of resources, your graveyard is exiled, or you're facing a locked combo deck with no interaction. Concede if you're down to a single threat with no recursion or mana to support it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast cheap threats to force multiple counters, use Unearth in response to sweepers, and use Matron to find answers. Attack with multiple threats to stretch their counter spells, and consider sideboarding in more uncounterable threats or discard to hit their counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat them out. Use discard to hit their ramp spells and mana rocks. Bring in artifact hate and early threats to disrupt their acceleration. Don't let them stabilize into big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with discard, early threats, and artifact hate. Don't overextend into their combat tricks. Use Wellspring to cast a mid-game threat that outclasses their early board. Prioritize life total and resource denial.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early and use recursion to out-value them. Attack through removal and use Wellspring to cast multiple threats. Use Matron to find answers to their key spells. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure on and use graveyard recursion to out-resource them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing graveyard recursion and discard. Use Matron to find better Unearth targets and disrupt their engine. Track their graveyard and exile effects. Win by out-recursing and out-drawing, using Wellspring to fuel multiple Unearth plays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Against control-heavy fields, bring in more recursion and discard to disrupt their game plan. Against aggro, prioritize early threats and artifact hate. Generally, keep the core recursion engine but swap lands for answers. Track opponent's graveyard and exile effects, and adjust your recursion strategy accordingly. Always sideboard in discard to hit key combo or control pieces.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending into sweepers, forgetting to protect Unearth targets, mismanaging Wellspring mana, and not tracking graveyard exile. Unique interactions include using Wellspring to pay multiple Unearth costs in a single turn and Matron fetching Unearth targets from outside the game. Always prioritize life total in aggressive matchups and track opponent's removal to time your Unearth plays. Remember that Unearthed creatures return to the battlefield, not hand, and are exiled at end of turn, so plan your recursion and combat accordingly.

philosophy: A 4-color control deck that stabilizes early with interaction, leverages Lorien Revealed for card advantage and flexible mana, and closes games with Skred’s haste, trample, and artifact/enchantment destruction. It wins by out-resourcing control and combo decks while efficiently answering aggro and ramp threats through patience and resource denial.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for U, R, G), Counterspell, early interaction, or Lorien Revealed paired with a land. Look for 4+ mana sources and answers to early threats (Mono Red Madness, Elves, Grixis Affinity). Mulligan to 1-2 lands, no interaction, or unplayable mana. Prioritize hands that can answer turn-1 threats while developing into Lorien by turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold up Counterspell for key threats or play a cheap interaction. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Lorien Revealed if possible, or hold for interaction. Prioritize basics for U, R, G and use Lorien’s tap ability for flexibility. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Lorien (3RRG) or Skred ({3} with a legendary) by turn 3-4. Avoid tapping out for non-essential plays.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): +2 Artifact/Enchantment Hate, +2 Cheap Removal, -2 Card Draw. Against control/combo (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +2 Counterspell, +1 Graveyard Hate, -2 Early Interaction. Against Jund Wildfire: +1 Board Wipe, +2 Interaction, -2 Finishers. Mirror: Keep mainboard, +1 Skred, +1 Lorien Revealed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Lorien’s sacrifice sparingly; only when you have excess mana or need to dig for answers/finishers. Manage mana to keep Counterspell available for key spells while developing board. Avoid tapping out for non-essential plays. Track opponent’s graveyard and library for combo threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold interaction for high-impact threats (combo pieces, ramp accelerants, or game-ending spells). Commit Skred only when you can protect it or when you’re ready to close. Use Lorien’s mana to answer multiple threats or cast a finisher. Prioritize board state over tempo when facing control.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent’s mana development, graveyard usage, and library draws. Note when they tap out for big spells or combo pieces. Track artifact/enchantment counts for Skred’s ETB targeting. Pay attention to when they hold up counterspells versus when they’re committed to the board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Skred (trample, haste, artifact/enchantment destruction), Lorien Revealed (card draw, flexible mana), and other legendary creatures. Win by out-resourcing, closing with Skred’s trample, or overwhelming with card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, enough mana for Skred + protection, or when the opponent is at lethal and you can’t stabilize. Commit to Skred when you can remove their key permanents or when you’re ready to trade efficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re locked out of lands, opponent has infinite combo, or you’re down to one card against a deck with multiple win conditions. Also concede if you’re consistently drawing dead interaction against a locked board or if your win conditions are completely answered.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Skred’s {3} alternate cost, split spells, or sacrifice Lorien to draw around counters. Layer threats and use artifact/enchantment hate to bypass counters. Force them to use interaction on low-impact spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Answer early mana dorks, use Skred’s ETB to destroy ramp artifacts/enchantments, hold up counters for key acceleration spells. Stabilize with early interaction and close quickly before they reach their late-game power level.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early interaction and cheap removal, stabilize with Lorien’s card advantage, and close games quickly with Skred. Don’t overextend; hold up counters for key threats. Prioritize survival over card advantage in the first two turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with Lorien, use Skred to remove their key permanents, manage counterspells with alternate costs or layered threats. Force them to use interaction on low-impact spells. Win the resource war by out-drawing them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Use Lorien efficiently, track counterspells, and use Skred to remove their Lorien or key threats. Prioritize board control and resource denial. Play conservatively until you can safely commit to a finisher.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and more counters. Against aggro, bring in more early interaction and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control, bring in more finishers and card advantage. Always keep Lorien and Skred unless the format changes drastically. Adjust your sideboard based on metagame shifts, prioritizing hate cards that disrupt opponent’s primary win conditions.
notes: Common mistake: Over-sacrificing Lorien too early. Remember Skred’s alternate cost requires a legendary creature. Lorien’s mana is U/R/G, so you’ll need basics or mana rocks for {3} and {U}{U}. Track opponent’s graveyard and library for combo threats. Don’t tap out for non-essential plays; control wins by patience and resource denial. Always prioritize keeping interaction in hand over developing the board in the early turns.

philosophy: Assemble a recursive food-to-zombie engine using Myr Retriever to fetch key artifacts and food sources, then convert graveyard food into a rapidly growing zombie army via Cauldron Familiar. Out-scale midrange and combo opponents by generating unanswerable board states, while leveraging four-color fixing and card draw to out-grind control.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fixing), Myr Retriever, and at least one food generator or artifact that produces/sacrifices for food. Keep Cauldron Familiar paired with mana or food. Mulligan pure land hands, high-cost spell clusters, or hands lacking both Retriever and Familiar without a clear combo path.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or low-cost mana rock if available. Turn 2: Cast Myr Retriever, fetch a key artifact (e.g., Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or a food generator). If you have Familiar, sequence mana into casting it, then begin generating food tokens and shoving them to the graveyard to fuel the engine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness, Elves): Bring in board wipes, targeted removal, and artifact hate. vs Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): Bring in additional card draw, protection for your engine, and redundant combo pieces. vs Combo/Mirror: Bring in graveyard hate, additional protection, and redundant win conditions.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping mana open for Retriever triggers and Familiar activations. Sequence food generation carefully to avoid graveyard hate. Use card draw (Lembas, cantrips) to maintain hand size and avoid running out of resources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to converting food to zombies or when you can protect your engine. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal in play or hand.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), sweepers, and targeted removal on Familiar/Retriever. Note when opponents tap out for counters or spot removal to identify windows for your combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cauldron Familiar + graveyard food = massive zombie army. Use artifact sacrifice outlets or food generators to keep the engine running and attack with the resulting zombies.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ food in the graveyard, a protected Familiar, and the opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand. Also commit when you can close the game before their combo or beatdown resolves.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has established a hard lock (e.g., Rest in Peace + graveyard hate), you're down to dead hands with no Retriever/Familiar, or you're facing a faster aggro deck with no stabilization tools.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips, multikind spells, or sacrifice outlets to bypass counters. Sequence Retriever to fetch cheap, hard-to-counter pieces. Utilize flash or instant-speed combos if the format allows.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with food/zombies. Use artifact hate to disrupt their mana. Don't let them stabilize; close games quickly before they outpace your engine.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal, board wipes, and early food generation to race. Use Lembas for card draw to out-value them in the late game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with incremental value. Use card draw to out-grind them. Protect your engine with counterspells or protection if available, and be ready to swing with zombies when they tap out.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize graveyard protection, disrupt their Retriever/Familiar, and out-value them with better sequencing and sideboard hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate while preserving the core engine. Against control, add more protection and card draw. Against aggro/ramp, add removal and sweepers. Adjust your mana base if the format allows, and prioritize keeping the Retriever/Familiar/food loop intact.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to food generation without protecting Familiar, ignoring graveyard hate, and mismanaging mana for Retriever triggers. Lembas can be sacrificed for card draw if the engine stalls, but prioritize food generation. Remember that food in the graveyard is your win condition, so protect it from hate and sequence your turns to maximize zombie output.

philosophy: A four-color tempo-control hybrid that synthesizes early interaction, flexible removal, and escalating threats to out-resource and out-interact with aggressive, combo, and midrange strategies. It aims to stabilize the early game with efficient spells and flying creatures, then close out games through card advantage and resilient mid-game threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped entry or fetchable basics), at least one early play (Glint Hawk, Kor Skyfisher, or Galvanic Blast), and a curve that allows a turn-1 or turn-2 spell. Mulligan to 0 lands, 4+ lands with no early spells, or hands consisting entirely of high-cost threats. Prioritize keeping hands that can establish board presence or interaction by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Glint Hawk or Kor Skyfisher if available. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Galvanic Blast to remove a 2-drop, or drop a 2-drop threat. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana availability; play W/U sources first to enable early spells, then add B/R as needed. If the format allows fetch lands or shock lands, prioritize them to smooth mana and enable dual-color spells immediately.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Mono Red Madness/Elves: +2 Disenchant/Path to Exile, +2 Lightning Helix, -2 heavy finishers. vs Blue Terror/Grixis Affinity: +2 Spell Pierce/Counterspell, +2 Thoughtseist, -2 early aggro creatures. vs Jund Wildfire: +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Abrupt Decay, -2 direct damage spells. vs Mirror: +1 additional interaction, -1 land if mana base is clunky.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a tempo resource; hold priority to react to opponent's key spells rather than autopiloting through turns. Use graveyard strategically if recursion is available, but avoid overcommitting unless necessary. Prioritize card advantage and efficient 1-for-1 trades that clear the board or protect key threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the board is clear. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy targeted removal. Use flying creatures to pressure while keeping removal in hand to answer their answers. Trade efficiently to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal, and combo win conditions. Key tells: opponent holding up mana on your turn 3-4, discarding your hand, or playing unusual land drops. Track their graveyard and library if relevant, and note when they start hoarding mana for late-game threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Resilient mid-game threats (Kor Skyfisher variants, larger flyers) combined with targeted removal to clear blockers. If the format includes a specific win-con, it is typically a combination of board presence, card advantage, and precise removal to close out games in 2-3 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can close the game in 2-3 turns. Also commit when you can protect your threats with removal or counters, or when the opponent is at lethal and you have the resources to deliver it.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 cards against a combo deck with a guaranteed win condition, or if you are facing a sweeper + heavy removal lock with no draw power. Also concede if your mana base is completely broken and you cannot cast your spells, or if the opponent has stabilized into an unbreakable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that can be cast in response to counterspells. Employ Galvanic Blast to force counterspells into unfavorable trades. Use flashback or alternative costs if available, and prioritize casting spells when the opponent has no mana to respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops. Use removal on their ramp targets. Do not let them stabilize; trade efficiently and close the game before their late-game threats. If they go wide, use sweepers or targeted removal to clear the board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and flying creatures. Do not overcommit to board stalls; use direct damage to clear paths. Transition to card advantage once stabilized. Track their hand for removal and hold up interaction to protect your key threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats to force interaction. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Out-value them with card draw and resilient threats. Do not let them control the pace; force trades on your terms and punish their mana base if it is clunky.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, efficient removal, and protecting your key threats. Track their hand for sweepers. Win by out-resourcing and making better trades. Sideboard in additional interaction and discard to disrupt their game plan.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure tempo/control shell to a more interactive or aggressive stance depending on the matchup. Bring in hate cards against specific strategies, but keep a balance of interaction and threats. Adjust your land base if the format allows for basic land removal or fetches. Always keep at least one copy of your key interaction spells, and consider bringing in additional discard or counterspells against control and combo decks.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to early threats without protection. Another: mismanaging mana base in a 4-color deck; always prioritize lands that enter untapped or can be fetched. Unique interaction: Glint Hawk's flying makes it excellent for trading with sweepers or forcing unfavorable blocks. Kor Skyfisher's stats make it a solid early blocker. Galvanic Blast is versatile for both removal and finishing off low-health players. Always track opponent's mana to predict their interaction window, and remember that 4-color decks often win by out-resourcing rather than out-speeding.

philosophy: A tempo-control hybrid that leverages bounce effects and Orzhov lifelink to out-value opponents through repeated interactions and efficient threat recursion. It aims to grind out wins against diverse archetypes by disrupting their game plans while steadily building an unblockable or resilient board.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetches for W/B fixing), at least one turn-1 or turn-2 threat (Kor Skyfisher, Refurbished Familiar), or early interaction (Thraben Charm, Ephemerate). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, 3+ non-basic lands without fixing, or hands lacking any way to interact or develop a board.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap threat or hold for interaction. Turn 2: Develop board with Kor Skyfisher or Refurbished Familiar, using Thraben Charm for lifegain, disruption, or temporary board stalls. Sequence lands to ensure consistent access to both white and black mana for charms and instant-speed bounce.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red): +1/+1 counters, direct damage, discard, and early interaction. vs Control (Blue Terror): +card draw, resilient finishers, and hate on counterspells. vs Ramp/Combo (Elves, Grixis Affinity): +artifact hate, graveyard hate, and board wipes. vs Midrange (Jund): +versatile removal, lifegain, and threat recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for interaction versus threat development. Use the graveyard strategically for Refurbished Familiar recursion without overcommitting to it. Avoid bouncing your own threats unless it triggers a specific value engine or buys critical tempo.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the opponent lacks sweepers. Hold back if facing heavy removal or board wipes. Use Ephemerate and Thraben Charm to temporarily stall key threats while you stabilize or close out the game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and combo pieces. Note when opponents save removal for specific threats to plan your sequence. Track graveyard content for recursion threats and monitor life totals to time lifelink-based wins.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kor Skyfisher (evading lifelink), Refurbished Familiar (value engine), and Thraben Charm modes. Win by out-valuing through repeated interactions, cycling threats, or overwhelming with resilient, evasive pressure.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, protection, or the opponent is low on interaction. Commit fully when you can close the game before they can stabilize or find a counterplay.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one card against an unanswerable combo, or if you are consistently out-valued in a grindy matchup with no comeback tools. Concede early if your game plan is completely neutralized and you lack sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split casts, or bounce their counterspells with Ephemerate. Sequence Thraben Charm to disrupt their stack. Play around with timing and avoid overcommitting to single high-value spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their curve. Use artifact hate, graveyard hate, and Ephemerate to bounce ramp targets. Stabilize with lifelinked threats and efficient removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction, lifegain, and board wipes if available. Stabilize with lifelinked threats and efficient removal. Use bounce to temporarily stall key attackers while you set up your game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure and use bounce to cycle threats. Out-value with card draw and recursion. Don't overcommit to one threat; force them to use multiple answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value engine wins. Track graveyard, manage bounce timing, and out-resource with better sequencing. Prioritize keeping threats on the board and use Thraben Charm to disrupt their recursion.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to specific matchups rather than relying on generic answers. Increase interaction against control and combo, add protection and early pressure against aggro, and adjust your land base if mana fixing becomes an issue. Prioritize matchup-specific hate over broad utility cards.
notes: Common mistakes include over-bouncing your own threats without follow-up, mismanaging mana for Orzhov colors, and ignoring graveyard interactions. Unique interactions: Thraben Charm modes can be sequenced for maximum value, and Refurbished Familiar synergizes with bounce effects for repeated triggers. Always track opponent's hand and graveyard to optimize bounce and recursion timing.

philosophy: 4c Ponza is a resource-denial midrange deck that wins by systematically dismantling the opponent's mana and board while establishing resilient, escalating threats. It excels against combo and ramp strategies that rely on consistent resources, while grinding out control and midrange mirrors through superior card advantage, efficient removal, and late-game pump finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one land destruction spell (Thermokarst/Ruination), and a turn-2 threat or interaction. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or 2+ lands with only removal and no threats/land destruction. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands and a spell on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available, or hold for a turn 2 land destruction. Turn 2: Cast Thermokarst to disrupt opponent's mana, or develop a threat like Avenging Hunter. Prioritize fetching/shocking for optimal land drops. Against aggro, hold removal for early threats; against control/ramp, prioritize land destruction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Mono Red Madness): +1 discard, +1 direct damage, -2 land destruction, -1 pump/enchantment. vs Control (Blue Terror, Grixis Affinity): +1 counterspell/interaction, +1 card draw, -2 land destruction, -1 removal. vs Ramp/Combo (Elves, Jund Wildfire): +2 land destruction, +1 hate bear, -2 card draw, -1 removal. vs Mirror: +1 targeted removal, +1 discard, -2 generic interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for interaction until you establish board control. Use cantrips to dig for answers or threats. Avoid overcommitting to Utopia Sprawl without a threat to pump. Manage graveyard by tracking recursion threats; hold graveyard hate if the opponent is building toward it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or land destruction to protect them or answer the opponent's response. Hold back on pumping Avenging Hunter if the board is clear and you can stabilize first. Trade efficiently: use removal on key threats, save land destruction for mana bases or combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana acceleration (Elves, Affinity), combo win conditions (Madness, Terror), and control win cons (Jund, Grixis). Key tells: opponent playing multiple lands early, drawing extra cards, or setting up enchantments/auras. Track their graveyard for recursion or mill threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Utopia Sprawl + Avenging Hunter is the primary win condition, often supported by direct damage or discard to clear the path. Secondary finishers include resilient midrange threats and pump spells that close out games in one or two turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, the opponent has no answers to your pump, or you can clear their blockers with removal/damage. Commit when you've disrupted their mana and they're on a resource deficit.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your disruption, if you're dead-drawn in a grindy mirror with no answers, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., mass recursion, infinite combo) with no hate in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig past counters, cast spells in response to their counters, or use uncounterable threats. Hold up mana for protection, but don't overextend. Consider discard to remove key pieces before they can be countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Aggressive land destruction is key. Prioritize Thermokarst on turn 1-2. Use discard to remove key ramp pieces. Stabilize with early threats and removal. Don't let them get to 4+ lands.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal and discard over land destruction. Use Utopia Sprawl early if you have a threat to stabilize. Trade efficiently and don't overcommit to land destruction. Win quickly with pumped threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with resilient threats. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Dig for answers with cantrips. Force them to spend resources on removal, then close out with a pumped threat or combo.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage and efficient trades. Prioritize keeping removal for their threats. Use discard to disrupt their game plan. Win the resource war by out-grinding them with better sequencing and sideboard cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction density based on the metagame. Against combo/ramp, increase land destruction and hate bears. Against aggro, shift to removal and discard. In mirrors, prioritize targeted removal and discard. Always track opponent's win conditions and sideboard accordingly. Avoid over-indexing on one strategy; keep a balanced toolkit.
notes: Utopia Sprawl + Avenging Hunter is a powerful but fragile combo; protect it with removal or discard. Thermokarst is your best early game spell against ramp/affinity but useless against non-land strategies. Don't overcommit to land destruction in control mirrors. Watch for graveyard recursion in Jund/Wildfire. Practice sequencing cantrips to dig for answers without flooding your board.

philosophy: Izzet Control aims to neutralize early threats with efficient disruption, stabilize into a late-game value engine centered on Lorien Revealed, and close out games through card advantage and incremental damage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early interaction (Force Spike, Counterspell), and Lorien Revealed or a cantrip. Mulligan flood/draw, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands lacking early disruption against aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Force Spike on a 1-drop threat if possible. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Counterspell on a key spell or play a cantrip. Sequence lands to ensure U/R availability; prioritize untapping lands before casting spells to maximize interaction windows.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Red Madness, Elves, Affinity): +2 Artifact Hate, +2 Direct Damage, -1 Counterspell. Vs Control (Blue Terror): +2 Card Draw, +1 Sweepers, -2 Early Interaction. Vs Midrange (Jund): +2 Spot Removal, +1 Graveyard Hate, -1 Counterspell. Vs Mirror: +1 Card Advantage, +1 Efficient Interaction, -2 Finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hold 1-2 mana for interaction until Lorien Revealed is on the board. Prioritize drawing into Lorien over overcommitting to board presence. Use cantrips to cycle dead cards and maintain hand size.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Lorien Revealed only after stabilizing against early aggression. Hold back on casting non-essential spells if the opponent has sweepers or removal in hand. Use Force Spike to protect Lorien from targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development and discard patterns. Key tells: holding up 3+ mana suggests a sweeper or combo piece; discarding draw spells indicates a control/combo plan; playing multiple lands without spells suggests ramp or combo.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lorien Revealed generating Saprolings, combined with direct damage spells and card draw. Win by out-valuing the opponent, sacrificing Lorien for damage/card advantage when threatened, and closing with combat or direct spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers, you have card advantage, and Lorien is on the board. Commit to attacking with Saprolings and casting remaining interaction to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are locked out of interaction, facing a combo that outpaces your stabilization, or holding a dead hand with no lands or draw spells. Concede if the opponent has established an unanswerable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to out-value counters, sacrifice Lorien for card advantage, and avoid overcommitting to expensive spells. Target their counterspells with spot removal or discard if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Force Spike and Counterspell to disrupt mana development. Bring in artifact hate and sweepers to neutralize late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize quickly with cheap interaction and spot removal. Prioritize surviving turn 3-4, then transition to Lorien Revealed for card advantage and a clock. Use direct damage to clear the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with Lorien Revealed and card draw. Pressure with Saprolings to force interaction, then close out with direct damage. Avoid trading spells inefficiently; win on card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on card advantage and efficient interaction. Prioritize casting Lorien Revealed first to establish a board presence. Use Force Spike to protect your Lorien and disrupt theirs. Cycle through your deck to find the next key spell.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate and adjust your mana base if needed. Prioritize card quality over quantity in tight matchups, and ensure you have enough interaction to survive the early game while maintaining late-game value.
notes: Lorien Revealed is legendary, so you cannot have multiple copies; plan around its single existence. Watch for 'Legend rule' interactions if an opponent copies it. Format legality heavily influences card choices, so verify your list's validity. Avoid overextending into sweepers, and always hold up interaction for key threats before committing to Lorien.
